The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, January 1997. © Copyright 1997 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers,
Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
Legionnaires’ Disease:
Seeking Effective Prevention
By J. Donald Millar, M.D., D.T.P.H. (Lond.), George K Morris, Ph.D., and Brian G. Shelton, M.P.H.
Member ASHRAE
uring the Bicentennial summer of
1976, American Legion Conven-
tioneers in Philadelphia suffered a dra-
matic epidemic that left 34 dead. Near
the end of 1976, scientists at the Centers
for Disease Control (CDC) in Atlanta
discovered the bacterium that caused
Legionnaires’ disease and named it
Legionella.
Nearly two decades later, a wealth of
scientific information exists about the
organism, its health effects, epidemiol-
ogy, microbiology, aquatic ecology,
molecular biology, immunology, patho-
physiology, etc. Fortunately, for the
engineer seeking to prevent Legion-
naires’ disease, it is unnecessary to mas-
ter this complexity; the practice of
prevention requires understanding a few,
straightforward facts.
The purpose of this paper is to present
four messages about Legionnaires’ dis-
ease that provide a conceptual frame-
work to guide the crucial role of
practical prevention. Those messages
are:
• Legionnaires’ disease is important;
• Legionnaires’ disease is an environ-
mental disease;
• Legionnaires’ disease is prevent-
able; and,
• Legionnaires’ disease prevention
requires the right strategy.
Legionnaires’ disease is important
Legionellosis is the general term
which describes the two forms of disease
caused by Legionella. They are classical
Legionnaires’ disease, the pneumonia
experienced by the American Legion
conventioneers in Philadelphia; and the
less severe, less frequent Pontiac Fever.
The latter is named for the Michigan city
where the first reported outbreak
occurred among, ironically, workers in a
health department building. Legion-
naires’ disease is not rare or trivial. It is
also not “going away.”
Legionnaires’ disease occurs in epi-
demics and as sporadic (apparently indi-
vidual)
cases.
Surveillance
of
Legionnaire’s disease based on reports of
cases to the Centers for Disease Control
and Prevention (CDC) resulted in 1,604
cases reported in 19941, but this method
of surveillance is notoriously poor. At
various times, CDC epidemiologists have
published estimates of the annual total
number of cases of Legionnaires’ disease,
ranging from 10,0001 to 100,0002 cases.
Legionnaires’ disease is not rare.
In Pontiac fever, the disease is usually
“self-limited,” meaning that the patient
recovers after a few days without antibi-
otic treatment. Legionnaires’ disease is
very different. The disease is typically
severe with fever often as high as 105°F
(40.6°C) and pneumonia which can lead
to respiratory failure and death. The
likelihood that Legionnaires’ disease
will kill its victim depends on the expo-
sure dose, victim’s age, underlying
chronic illnesses, smoking, weakened
immunity (as in infection with HIV-
AIDS) and the quality of medical care
received. Legionnaires’ disease can be
treated successfully with antibiotics;
nonetheless, recovery is not guaranteed.
Victims die in the best hospitals after
expert and timely antibiotic treatment.
The probability of dying of Legion-
naire’s disease, if infected - called the
death-to-case ratio - averages between
5% and 30%3. However, there are
reported epidemics in which as many as
two thirds of patients died 4.
By averaging the higher and lower
estimates one derives an estimated total
for annual cases of 55,000, with 17.5%
for the estimated average death-to-case
ratio. Thus, we estimate the total number
of persons in the United States who die
of Legionnaires’ disease each year at
8,000 to 9,000. This figure is similar in
magnitude to the annual toll of fatal
occupational traumatic injuries of all
causes. The true number of deaths per
year is unknown. Even if these estimates
are high, and the deaths per year in the
About the Authors
J. Donald Millar, M.D., D.T.P.H.
(Lond.) serves on ASHRAE’s Environ-
mental
Health
Committee
and
on
ASHRAE’s Legionella Position Paper
Committee. Currently, he is an occupa-
tional and environmental health consult-
ant to PathCon Laboratories. Dr. Millar
was formerly the director of the National
Institute for Occupational and Safety and
Health (1981-1993) and inaugural director
of the Center for Environmental Health,
Centers for Disease Control and Preven-
tion. Dr. Millar also directed the Smallpox
Eradication Program in West and Central
Africa, the Vanguard of global small pox
eraducation.
Brian G. Shelton, M.P.H. currently serves
on the ASHRAE Environmental Health
Committee, the ASHRAE Legionella Posi-
tion Paper Committee and the Legionella
Guidelines Committee (GPC-12). Shelton
is the vice president of PathCon Laborato-
ries in Norcross, Ga., where he has directed
investigations of numerous Legionnaires’
disease outbreaks.
George K. Morris, Ph.D. is the president
of PathCon Laboratories, Norcross, Ga.,
and is an ASHRAE member. Dr. Morris is
a Fellow of the American Academy of Mi-
crobiology. He was formerly with the Cen-
ters for Disease Control (CDC) where he
served in several positions including Epi-
demic Intelligence Service Officer, chief of
the Epidemiological Investigations Labo-
ratory. Dr. Morris was also the primary au-
thor of the first scientific paper that
described the isolation of Legionella from
the environment.
D
See Millar
Continued on page 24
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
23
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
2 4
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
U.S. are only from 500 to 2000, it is clear
that Legionnaires’ disease is not trivial.
Patients who recover from Legion-
naires’ disease often experience pro-
longed convalescence including physical
and psychological impairment. This can
profoundly affect their quality of life and
may result in continuing costs for medi-
cal care and lost income.
Because it is known that Legionnaires’
disease is building-related with a defin-
able chain of causation, surviving victims
often seek to hold building owners and
managers legally accountable for their
pain, suffering, and costs. In a memoran-
dum to one of us (BGS), attorney Clark
Patten describes the legal ramifications of
an outbreak of Legionnaires’ disease in a
federal building in Richmond, California
that resulted in an out of court settlement.
The issue of liability for Legionnaires’
disease is real and increasing. Legion-
naires’ disease is not going away.
Legionnaires’ disease is an
environmental disease
The natural habitat of the bacterium
that
causes
Legionnaires’
disease,
Legionella, is water but it may be found
in wet soil. However, water contaminated
with Legionella is the usual source of
transmission. The species of Legionella
most commonly associated with Legion-
naires’ disease is called Legionella pneu-
mophila, of which there are at least 15
groups. The serogroup of Legionella
pneumophila most commonly responsi-
ble for disease is serogroup 1. There are at
least 35 species of Legionella, other than
pneumophila.
Legionnaires’ disease is always
acquired from the environment. No
instance of Legionella being transmitted
from an infected person to another per-
son has ever been documented. As Yu
and coworkers noted in 1987, “unlike
most pneumonias, the source for L.
pneumophila can easily be identified.5”
The sources most commonly associ-
ated with outbreaks of Legionnaires’
disease are aerosol-producers such as
cooling towers, evaporative condensers,
whirlpool spas, humidifiers, fogger-mis-
ters, decorative fountains and contami-
nated potable water aerosolized by
shower heads and faucets.6 In respirable
water droplets in the aerosol, the organ-
ism is carried to the lungs where it estab-
lishes the infection.
Amplification
In natural sources such as streams and
rivers, Legionella usually is present in
low concentrations, i.e., 1 colony form-
ing unit per milliliter (cfu/ml) of water,
or less. Such concentrations in natural
settings constitute little hazard. How-
ever, because Legionella is a living
organism, it has the attribute of multiply-
ing rapidly in hospitable circumstances
such as warm water with sufficient
organic nutrients. Also, there is a poorly
understood role for single-celled ani-
mals, protozoa.
The growth process, called amplifica-
tion, can rapidly intensify the degree to
which the water is hazardous if aero-
solized and inhaled. Because the rate of
amplification depends on conditions in
the water that are subject to dynamic
unpredictable change, there is no reli-
able way to assess amplification except
to periodically culture the water and
measure the numbers of Legionella
present. Cooling tower waters have been
cultured and the concentrations of
organisms measured by reliable meth-
ods. These studies show that cooling
towers are frequently contaminated by
Legionella, often with high numbers of
organisms, e.g., greater than 1,000 cfu/
ml of water.7,8,9
No study has answered the question
“what level of Legionella is considered
safe?” However, there is evidence that
detecting amplification is important.
During the past 15 years, several scien-
tific studies,4,6,9,10,11,12 have been pub-
lished in which the results suggest some
correlation between high levels of
organisms in source water and the likeli-
hood of causing an epidemic of Legion-
naires’ disease. This affirms scientific
plausibility that, like other biological,
chemical, and physical agents, the
greater the dose of the hazard, the
greater the risk to the person exposed.
Here it is important to note that risk is a
function of both hazard and exposure.
There may be large concentrations of
organisms which produce no risk because
no persons are exposed. Similarly, smaller
concentrations may produce considerable
risk if many are exposed, or some are
exposed repeatedly.
Implications of being a purely
environmental disease
That Legionnaires’ disease is a purely
environmental disease is good news for
the engineer seeking to prevent illness in
the workplace, community or home.
Being purely environmental means that,
in many important ways, Legionnaires’
disease resembles other familiar occupa-
tional diseases caused by exposures to
chemical vapors, mists, and dusts.
Of course, Legionella bacteria are liv-
ing organisms which can grow, amplify
and infect; this makes them different
from inhaled chemicals and dusts. But,
important similarities to other occupa-
tional/environmental diseases means that
the methods of engineering and industrial
hygiene, which have long protected
workers from other environmental haz-
ards, are applicable for preventing
Legionnaires’ disease. These include
engineering controls, administrative con-
trols, work practices, personal protective
equipment,13 as well as hazard-recogni-
tion, exposure evaluation and environ-
mental monitoring.14
Progress in preventing Legionnaires’
disease has been hampered by an
approach to prevention which, to date,
has almost exclusively focused on the
traditional medical paradigm of disease
control, rather than the engineering-
industrial hygiene paradigm of hazard
control. Among the problems caused by
this orientation is a lack of the emphasis
on measuring the quantitative dimen-
sions of exposure, dose, which has typi-
fied the approach to chemical and
physical hazards in the workplace.
Bringing such principles of engineering
and industrial hygiene to bear on
Legionnaires’ disease is one of the
objectives of this paper.
Legionnaires’ disease is preventable
At the 2nd International Symposium
on Legionella in 1983, Fraser described
the transmission of legionellosis in
terms of a six-link chain of causation.15
Breiman at the International Symposium
in 1992 reiterated the scheme.16
The six links in the chain are (1) an
environmental reservoir of Legionella;
(2) factors enabling amplification (of
Legionella); (3) a means of disseminat-
ing the organism from the reservoir to
susceptible people; (4) a strain of
Legionella virulent enough to infect
humans; (5) inoculation of the organism
at a site in the human where infection
can occur; and, (6) a person who is sus-
ceptible and unable to successfully fight
off the infection.
This model is useful for thinking about
ways to prevent Legionnaires’ disease.
Millar
Continued from page 22
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
25
Breiman said that, “interfering with any
link in the chain will presumably
decrease the occurrence of the disease.”17
However, in looking at the Fraser-
Breiman model, it is clear that only a few
of these links offer any possibility of
intervention. Considering the links in
reverse order, we can do nothing about
the link six, susceptibility; there are no
tools, such as an effective vaccine, by
which to reliably reduce someone’s sus-
ceptibility to infection. Similarly, one
sees no way to influence link five, the
site of inoculation of the organism. Link
four, the virulence of the organism, is
clearly beyond the control of the most
imaginative practitioner of prevention.
Only at link three, the distribution
system, do we begin to see any possibil-
ity for intervention. A distribution sys-
tem can be manipulated in ways that
would make it less hospitable to
Legionella; it can be changed to mini-
mize aerosolization, it can be treated
with enough heat or biocides to assure
that organisms do not survive the pas-
sage from the reservoir to the periphery.
But, in practical terms, building water
systems are vastly complex with numer-
ous networks, connections, and outlets.
They are often vulnerable to damage
from heat and chemicals. To assure no
human exposure at any outlet by inter-
vening in the distribution system -
though theoretically possible - would be
a prodigious challenge. Thus only two
links, factors enabling amplification,
and, the organism in the reservoir, really
offer promise as points of vulnerability
in the chain of causation.
Link one and link two can be
addressed by various decontaminating
methods. It would, of course, be helpful
to know whether or not Legionella are
present in the reservoir, and at what lev-
els; also it will help to assess the existing
factors which enable amplification. To
do this, one can make reasoned guesses
as to water temperature, pH, amount of
organic nutrients, stagnancy, influences
of residual biocides, etc. Some of these
factors, including the presence of organ-
isms, can even be directly measured.
Alternatively, one can wait until epi-
demics occur, isolate the organisms
from patients, do epidemiologic investi-
gations hoping to incriminate a particu-
lar reservoir, and, if successful, one
could culture water from the reservoir
and determine whether or not it contains
organisms identical to those recovered
from the patients.
Because of the incubation period of
Legionnaires’ disease (about one week),
the well known inadequacies of disease
surveillance, and the time consumed
with these investigations, testing the
identified reservoir for the causative
organism is usually performed several
weeks after the real time of transmis-
sion. Considering the dynamics of even
the most stable reservoirs, the probabil-
ity of linking the device that actually
caused the outbreak to the outbreak, is a
gamble of indeterminate probability.
Nonetheless, this is the approach offi-
cially sanctioned by health authorities
for controlling Legionnaires’ disease. To
be
sure,
numerous
instances
are
recorded in which investigators incrimi-
nated reservoirs by this method, did
emergency decontamination of the
incriminated reservoir and stopped epi-
demics. Thus, Legionnaires’ disease has
proven to be readily preventable, even
by a reactive strategy that relies on epi-
demics to identify reservoirs with ampli-
fied Legionella.
A supplement to this approach has
been to urge conscientious routine main-
tenance of reservoirs and systems, assur-
ing
cleanliness,
proper
pH
and
temperature, and adequate levels of resid-
ual biocides. This supplement, mentioned
by Dondero et al in 1980,18 and reiterated
often by others including Barbaree in
1991,19 at least, offers comprehensive if
limited primary prevention; demonstra-
bly this has worked to some extent. Not
every reservoir, even those contaminated
with Legionella, always cause outbreaks.
On the contrary, the number of reservoirs
that have caused epidemics - to the extent
we know them - is a small proportion of
the total potential sources of transmis-
sion. Clearly, Legionnaires’ disease is
preventable.
Nonetheless, it is obvious that present
preventive practice is not adequate. We
are left with cases of Legionnaires’ dis-
ease that total ten to a hundred thousand,
of whom, possibly several thousand die.
Though Legionnaires’ disease is pre-
ventable, our practice of prevention is
not sufficient to prevent it.
Also, the existing approach occasion-
ally produces dramatic failure. In 1985,
Garbe and others4 at CDC reported inves-
tigating Legionnaires’ disease in a hospi-
tal. Two cases of nosocomial (hospital-
acquired) disease were detected early in
1983 from whom L. pneumophila, sero-
group 1, was isolated. An epidemiologic
and environmental investigation ensued.
The hospital’s cooling towers were tested
with negative results. However, potable
water was positive for L. pneumophila
serogroups 1 & 3, including samples
from outlets in the hospital room occu-
pied by one of the patients who acquired
Legionnaires’ disease. No data on the
concentration of organisms in the water
were reported.
Because “of the limited number of
cases identified and the complexity of
the potable water distribution system, no
intervention was attempted” by the
investigators. Active disease surveil-
lance for cases of Legionnaires’ disease
was initiated. A few months subse-
quently, in July and August, an outbreak
occurred of 15 hospital acquired cases
due to L. pneumophila serogroup 1; 10
of these persons died! A second epide-
miologic-environmental
investigation
implicated one of the cooling towers
which had a level of greater than 6,000
cfu/ml of L. pneumophila serogroup 1.
An additional tower had organisms at
the level of 60 cfu/ml. Emergency
decontamination of the two cooling tow-
ers was done. No additional cases were
seen in the next 10 months with “active
surveillance” for cases of legionellosis.
As to the source of contamination for
the cooling towers, the authors con-
cluded that “L. pneumophila was intro-
duced into these cooling waters from the
make-up water,” i.e., the already con-
taminated potable water system which
was apparently neglected at the time of
the first investigation. It seems clear to
us, that the second highly fatal outbreak
occurred because Legionella amplified
in the cooling towers with no attempt at
detection or control despite the earlier
outbreak of two cases. Is it possible that
periodic microbiological testing of the
cooling towers, had it been done, would
have revealed the amplification of
Legionella pneumophila Serogroup 1?
Is it possible that knowing about the
amplification, hospital staff would have
intervened, which, in turn, would have
prevented the second epidemic and
saved ten lives?
What is an effective strategy?
Legionellosis is one of the specific
Building-Related Illnesses. Most trans-
mission occurs indoors, irrespective of
MAINTENANCE A ND OPERATION
2 6
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
whether the victims are workers,
patients, or visitors. For purposes of pre-
vention, it is helpful to view legionello-
sis as an occupational disease. The
handbook of occupational disease pre-
vention - Preventing Occupational Dis-
ease and Injury20 - provides an
“integrated strategy” for preventing
occupational disease “built around the
elementary principle that occupational
disease and injury are caused by expo-
sure to hazards on the job. Primary pre-
vention of these outcomes, requires
controlling exposure.”21 The integrated
strategy is depicted graphically in Fig. 1.
The scheme is best described in the
words of the authors, “from left to right,
this diagram proceeds from exposure to
outcome. Thus, industrial hygiene and
safety engineering activities concerned
with recognition, evaluation, and control
of hazardous exposures are on the left;
medical activities concerned with diag-
nosis of conditions and treatment of peo-
ple are on the right and epidemiological
and toxicological activities requiring con-
sideration of both exposure and outcome
are in the center.” It should be noted that
legionellosis is caused by a microbiolog-
ical hazard, Legionella in water, so
“microbiology” may appropriately sub-
stituted for “toxicology” in the scheme, as
specifically regards legionellosis.
The authors continue, “There are sev-
eral possible paths to the control activity,
indicating different ways to determine
what exposures should be controlled.”
They list the four components of preven-
tion: anticipation of exposures and of out-
comes; hazard surveillance and analysis;
medical surveillance and analysis; the
uses of toxicology (substitute microbiol-
ogy for toxicology in the case of legion-
ellosis); and hazard controls.”22 It is
necessary to understand what constitutes
each of these components of prevention.
Regarding anticipation, the authors
write “health hazards at work can be
anticipated before they are manifest, and
preventive measures can be imple-
mented before disease or injury occurs.”
Regarding surveillance, they advise
“surveillance in the occupational envi-
ronment includes monitoring both haz-
ardous exposures and health outcomes.
It is often desirable to link exposure and
outcome data.” Also, “hazard surveil-
lance consists of both an inventory of
hazards and a quantitative assessment of
exposure ... quantitative assessment con-
sists of measuring the intensity or con-
centration, the variation in time, and the
total duration of exposure, as well as the
number of workers exposed.”23
Later,
they
elaborate,
“exposure
assessment is based on measuring the
concentration of intensity of a hazard ...
and time course of exposure. Ideally,
exposure assessment includes quantita-
tive measurements of exposure for each
individual...”24 They conclude, “if expo-
sure to hazards is excessive, it should be
controlled, regardless of outcome,”25 that
is whether or not disease has yet occurred.
Regarding medical surveillance, they
wrote, “medical surveillance may be
passive, requiring injured or ill people to
appear at a medical facility, or it may be
active in which case health professional
recruit and examine workers at risk.
“Active surveillance is more appropriate
for workers at higher risk ...”26 They
note a limitation of medical surveillance
in observing that “whatever exposure
caused disease may have changed by the
time the disease becomes clinically
apparent.” In summary they note that
“the point of surveillance is to prevent
disease and injury, not merely to docu-
ment its existence.”27
Regarding analysis, they wrote, “Epi-
demiological analysis ... is fundamen-
tally useful in this as in other aspects of
Figure 1: An
integrated
strategy for
preventing
occupational
disease and
injury.
From “Preventing Occupational Disease and Injury.” Copyright 1991 by American Public Health Association. Reprinted with permission
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
27
public health practice,” and “analysis of
occupational hazards .... may be the pri-
mary source of information used to
establish the need for controls.”
As regards control, the authors wrote,
“primary prevention is the preferred
means of disease control. In the work-
place, this is largely an engineering activ-
ity. There is a hierarchy of technical
controls…”
“From most to least effective, techni-
cal controls include positive engineering
that prevents generation of the hazard at
its source, environmental controls that are
implemented in the work environment
and personal protective devices that indi-
viduals workers can wear or use.” They
add, “personal protective devices are less
effective than the other two kinds of con-
trols and should be used ... when positive
engineering or environmental controls
are not feasible.” 28
How does the current approach to
legionellosis compare with this
model?
There is no existing official national
prevention policy for legionellosis
which we can compare with this ideal
model. No standard promulgated by
OSHA, HUD or EPA provides a national
strategy for prevention of legionellosis.
However, preventive practice has been
greatly influenced since the 1970s by
recommendations published by profes-
sionals of the Centers for Disease Con-
trol and Prevention (CDC) in scientific
papers, in CDC’s Morbidity and Mortal-
ity Weekly Report (MMWR) and in let-
ters and public comments.
At the Second International Sympo-
sium on Legionella in 1983, Dr. C. V.
Broome of CDC said, “The apparent
ubiquity of the organism makes it impor-
tant to establish a specific association
with a postulated means of transmission,
as the isolation of the organism from a
particular site does not necessarily imply
that the positive site was the source of
infection.”29
Referring specifically to questions
that had been raised about monitoring of
legionellae-contaminated sources, Dr.
Broome said, “the evidence that contam-
inated cooling towers may play a role in
the transmission of legionellosis raises
immediate questions about the need for
monitoring towers for the presence of
the organism and the feasibility of erad-
icating the organism when it is found.”30
She then enunciated a strategy for
legionellosis in observing that “current
recommendations call for routine main-
tenance of cooling towers and do not
suggest
surveillance
culturing
for
legionellae. However, in an outbreak,
cooling towers must be considered as a
possible source for transmission of the
organism. If an epidemiologic investiga-
tion documents that a cooling tower may
be implicated in transmission, cleaning
and hyperchlorination appear to be
effective in decreasing the risk...”
In a later session on prevention and
control she added, “since a directed
approach to control measures is contin-
gent on identification of cases and inves-
tigation of the method of transmission,
surveillance for both individual cases
and potentially linked clusters of cases
should be emphasized.”31
With minor modifications, the current
approach to legionellosis control con-
sists of (1) identification of outbreaks
through medical surveillance of cases;
(2) epidemiologic investigations to
incriminate potential sources; (3) micro-
biological tests to link case with source;
and, (4) decontamination of the source,
if identified. This appears to have been
CDC’s recommended strategy for pre-
vention of legionellosis since 1983. It is
reflected in various publications and
public statements on legionellosis, from
then until now.10,32,33,34,35,36,37
How does the current strategy
compare with the model?
Looking again at the “integrated strat-
egy for preventing occupational disease
and injury” (Figure 1), we can see that our
current strategy is preoccupied with the
right-hand side of the diagram - the out-
come-oriented, “medical” side. It largely
ignores the left-hand, exposure-oriented,
“engineering/industrial hygiene” compo-
nents. Currently, “outcomes”, i.e., cases of
legionellosis, are identified.
One then progresses leftwards through
“epidemiology” and “toxicology” (replace
“toxicology” with “microbiology” in the
case of legionellosis) to “control” of the
hazard, if one has successfully identified
the actual source. As noted, this reactive,
retrospective process of epidemiologic-
environmental investigations takes time
and, with the passage of time, the likeli-
hood of identifying the actual source of the
outbreak, diminishes. Nonetheless, many
reports show that when the actual source
was identified and decontaminated, the
outbreak stopped.
In the current approach, hazard sur-
veillance is not merely neglected, it is
discouraged. In the literature, at least six
papers,4,5,9,10,12,13 show an apparent
relationship between high concentra-
tions of legionellae in sources, and con-
sequent outbreaks of disease. This
evidence warrants serious attempts to
explore microbiological monitoring of
potential sources as a means of preven-
tion. Yet, on several occasions, state-
ments
published
by
CDC
have
discouraged any such pre-outbreak
monitoring.2,6,10,19,32
The only aspect of current practice
that directly addresses the left hand
“engineering/industrial hygiene” side of
the integrated strategy, is routine main-
tenance of reservoirs according to man-
ufacturers’ or other guidelines. Because
many cooling towers and other reser-
voirs have not produced outbreaks, this
degree of primary prevention practice
has probably prevented cases of legion-
ellosis. However, the continued occur-
rence of outbreaks demonstrates the
shortcomings of the current strategy.
Moreover, the preoccupation with the
right-hand side of the strategy has had
one destructive consequence. As noted
by Weeks et al, a principal reason for
hazard surveillance is to “evaluate con-
trols.”38
By
actively
discouraging
microbiological monitoring of potential
sources of Legionella, the current
approach deprives the engineer and
industrial hygienist of the most direct
means of assessing the effectiveness of
maintenance regimens, namely, docu-
menting their effects on the amplifica-
tion of Legionella.
What is needed in order to move for-
ward in the prevention of legionellosis is to
shift the paradigm from concentrating on
the right-hand, outcome-oriented, “medi-
cal” part of the chart, toward a greater
focus on the left-hand, exposure-oriented,
“engineering-industrial hygiene” side. An
effective prevention policy is “integrated”
in the sense that it integrates exposure-ori-
ented “engineering-industrial hygiene”
practices with the more traditional out-
come-oriented, “medical” activities.
To shift the paradigm and re-focus on
the engineering-industrial hygiene side,
means improved anticipation of poten-
tially hazardous sources and appropriate
hazard surveillance, namely, monitoring
potential sources of transmission to
detect amplification of Legionella. That
MAINTENANCE A ND OPERATION
2 8
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
can only be done by microbiological
testing of high-risk sources at intervals
over time. When analysis shows evi-
dence of amplification of Legionella
(the “excessive” hazard noted on the
chart) one can intervene with control
measures to decontaminate the source
before it produces disease.
Making such a shift would put us in
position to practice “the preferred means
of disease control,” primary prevention.
It would also provide the realistic hope
of reducing the burden of legionellosis.
If we remain committed to the old para-
digm focussed on reacting to outbreaks,
we will guarantee that outbreaks con-
tinue to occur, and that legionellosis will
always be with us.
The opportunity for professional
leadership
Next year we will mark the 20th anni-
versary of the discovery of legionellosis
and the organisms that cause it. Yet, we
seem to be “going nowhere fast” in terms
of prevention. There is need to change
direction. We are convinced that the appro-
priate change is to shift the paradigm
toward primary prevention of disease
through hazard surveillance, monitoring
of sources, analysis and control. The
required re-orientation is toward “engi-
neering-industrial hygiene” activities.
Shifting paradigms is not easy and this
issue will prove amply controversial. Pre-
dictably, attempts to encourage environ-
mental monitoring, for example, will be
greeted with opposition on the grounds
that there is not data available to predict
its efficacy or cost effectiveness. There
will be urgings to postpone any strategic
change pending the results of “prospec-
tive studies” of the levels of legionellae in
reservoirs, and what they mean for the
likelihood of outbreaks, etc.
It should be clearly understood that
the “prospective studies” that would sat-
isfy the purists that there is a direct rela-
tionship between levels of Legionella in
reservoirs and the incidence of disease in
humans exposed to those sources, sim-
ply can no longer be done. Such studies
would require that decontamination be
withheld from reservoirs with known
levels of Legionella in order to observe
“without bias” related disease in the
exposed population; such studies are
ethically unacceptable.
Instead, the efficacy of hazard sur-
veillance including environmental mon-
itoring must be proved by empirical
observations of its ordinary use in the
practice of primary prevention. There is
no other choice. If we delay starting until
“efficacy is clearly defined,” we will
wait forever. On the other hand, if we
proceed to introduce hazard surveillance
in limited ways with clearly defined tar-
gets and objectives, we will acquire the
knowledge necessary to produce an
effective “integrated” prevention strat-
egy for legionellosis.
Conclusion
Despite two decades of ever increas-
ing information about many aspects of
legionellosis, the disease seems to be as
common as ever producing tens of thou-
sands of cases and thousands of deaths
each year. Preventive measures, though
partially successful, clearly are not suf-
ficient. In addition to the professional
and human reasons for re-energized
interest in prevention, it is also clear that
building owners and managers face
increasing legal attention for any cases
of legionellosis acquired on their pre-
mises or transmitted from their devices
and systems. Thus, there is increasing
urgency to find a prevention strategy
that really works to interrupt transmis-
sion before disease occurs.
The
existing
officially-sanctioned
approach is based on using outbreaks as
the means of tracing back to contami-
nated reservoirs. This approach lacks
emphasis on hazard surveillance and
exposure assessment. Hazard surveil-
lance of potential sources of transmis-
sion, if feasible, offers the hope of
detecting the amplification of Legionella
organisms and guiding early intervention
to prevent the hazard.
A newPrevention strategy is needed
based on proactive engineering-industrial
hygiene,
exposure-oriented
activities
rather than relying on the existing strate-
gies based on the reactive, disease-ori-
ented medical methods. The goal is a
truly integrated national prevention strat-
egy that will reliably reduce the burden of
legionellosis.

The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, January 1997. © Copyright 1997 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers,
Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
ASHRAE Standard 62R:
How It Could Affect Segments
Of the Air-Conditioning Industry
By James A. Reese
Member ASHRAE
roposed revisions to the current
ASHRAE Standard 62-89-Ventila-
tion for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality
would have a major impact on the
HVAC industry. The proposed revisions
expand the details for providing accept-
able ventilation, and for the first time
mandate roles and requirements for var-
ious segments of the air-conditioning
industry as indicated in the purpose:
“To define the roles and requirements
for ventilation, source, management,
and cleaning in providing acceptable
indoor air quality.”
“To specify methods for determining
minimum ventilation rates... ”
“To specify ventilation system design,
operational, and maintenance require-
ments for various types of occupied
indoor spaces...”
Here’s how the new standard might
affect the key players:
OCCUPANTS
Buildings complying with the new
standard should provide better indoor air
quality to a majority of occupants. Many
of the significant instances of “Building
Related Illness” (BRI) have been traced
to bad design, improper commissioning,
and poor maintenance, which the new
standard addresses. Sick Building Syn-
drome (SBS) where perception plays an
important role, should be reduced by
provisions dealing with high humidity
and ventilation efficiency. The threat of
legal consequences will encourage
building owners and operators to be a lot
more responsive in addressing building
occupant complaints.
The standard takes the position that if
smoking is allowed in a building, that
building does not meet the standard.
Unlike Standard 62-89, ventilation rates
prescribed in the new standard no longer
include provisions for smoking. However
the appendix does describe in informative
language how acceptable perceived
indoor air quality can be provided for
smokers and non-smokers in the presence
of environmental tobacco smoke (ETS).
It also establishes comfort criteria for
“smoke free” and “smoke exposed”
zones.
OWNERS
Owners concerned about providing
good indoor air quality for their occu-
pants are provided much more compre-
hensive information about how to
achieve it with the new standard. While
the ASHRAE standard cannot designate
legal responsibility, if (or more likely
“when”) the new provisions become part
of major building codes, the owner will
have ultimate responsibility for seeing
that the building is designed, con-
structed and maintained to provide good
indoor air quality.
The specter of litigation will undoubt-
edly encourage much more owner con-
cern in choosing designers, products,
contractors and maintenance companies
with the knowledge and indoor air quality
experience to protect their investment.
“Good IAQ buildings” will undoubtedly
pay back with satisfied tenants, higher
occupancy rates, and reduced legal expo-
sure. While many buildings are already
capable of meeting the new standard, the
cost to comply for other buildings will
vary depending on the system chosen and
could even be lower. For example, venti-
lation costs for high density occupancies
such as schools and theaters will be sig-
nificantly less.
ARCHITECTS/ENGINEERS
The biggest burden of the revised
standard probably falls on the design
team. Documentation requirements for
all design criteria including the building
envelope must be provided as permanent
records for the building and will man-
date more careful attention. The benefit
is a better road map for construction and
service activities, not only initially, but
also for all future changes.
Architects have a major stake as well
as engineers. The building skin must be
designed to minimize the risk of micro-
bial growth inside walls. Location of
equipment rooms and openings in build-
ings for air intake and exhaust must sat-
isfy IAQ requirements.
System design in the past has been
driven by thermal requirements with
ventilation often treated as a minor con-
sideration. The new standard basically
requires a thermal design and a ventila-
tion design which must then be inte-
grated. More time and attention to detail
About the Author
James A. Reese, P.E., Member
ASHRAE, is vice president of Air
Products International, York Interna-
tional Corporation, and a member of
the ASHRAE committee that devel-
oped
the
proposed
revision
to
ASHRAE Standard 62-1989. Reese
started his career in 1961 as a sales en-
gineer at Trane and later became a ma-
jor
stockholder
and
CEO
of
Temperature Industries, a company
that was acquired by York. He has
written a number of technical publica-
tions and holds several U.S. patents.
P
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
31
will be required in determining air clas-
sifications and compliance with rules for
how each affects ventilation design,
developing strategies for mitigating
strong contaminant sources, and insur-
ing required full load and minimum load
ventilation to each space.
Equipment and system components
must be selected that meet all the require-
ments to minimize microbial contamina-
tion.
Maximum
relative
humidity
requirements (mandated for humid cli-
mates) for full and part load will require
more careful choice of systems, equip-
ment and controls. All this requires more
time and exposure to legal liability if not
done properly.
Air cleaning becomes a much more
important part of system design in the
new standard. Particulate filters meeting
a minimum 60% efficiency at 3 microns
per the coming new ASHRAE Standard
52.2 (equal to 30% dust spot filter rated
per existing Standard 52.1) will be
required on virtually every piece of air-
handling equipment. While the condi-
tion of outside air at the building site
must be determined and documented,
the standard does not require treatment
of outside air.
There is a caveat however. The stan-
dard says that failure to treat “unaccept-
able” outside air may result in not
achieving “acceptable indoor air quality”
without air cleaning. Since many times,
bad outside air includes smelly volatile
organic compounds (VOC’s) which are
frequently transient, the designer is left to
negotiate with the owner as to how much
he is willing to pay to ensure “acceptable
indoor air quality.”
The solution may lead to selection of
VOC filtration using activated carbon or
other chemical sorbents after determin-
ing acceptable threshold levels for each
contaminant identified in the site survey.
This analytical approach which the stan-
dard permits can also be used for build-
ing related contaminants (off-gassing
from paint, carpets, furniture, cleaning
materials, etc.).
More careful thought needs to be given
to future tenant changes. Such things
need to be planned for such as to how
construction areas will be “sealed off”
during the changes. Including an auxil-
iary exhaust system in the original design
is one suggested non-mandatory solution.
Smoking is not permitted in buildings
complying with this standard, If smok-
ing is to be allowed in a building, there
are stringent but varying design options
to provide perceived comfort depending
on the amount of smoking taking place
(incidental, moderate or heavy). In any
event “smoke free zones” must be com-
pletely isolated from “smoking zones”
through building pressure management
or with physical separation. Air from
“smoking zones” must be 100%
exhausted with makeup air from “smoke
exposed,” “smoke free” zones, or the
outdoors.
CONTRACTORS AND
MAINTENANCE COMPANIES
The construction of buildings under
the new standard will require more
attention to detail. Equipment and duct
construction must conform to require-
ments established to minimize microbial
growth. Closer attention to specifica-
tions will be required since there is likely
to be more legal ramification for devia-
tion from design. Consultants are less
likely to approve substitutions to speci-
fied components so more purchasing
effort will be required to establish costs
and delivery times. Porous materials
must be protected during construction
from exposure to moisture. The HVAC
system must be protected from dust dur-
ing construction. Testing and commis-
sioning
requirements
may
require
additional staff and special training for
some contractors.
The maintenance requirements repre-
sent a major opportunity for professional
service companies. Owners will likely
be more prone to delegate responsibility
for documentation, inspection and test-
ing requirements; and response to IAQ
complaints.
CODE OFFICIALS
The new standard is written in code
language using “shall” for all mandatory
requirements which makes it easier to
adopt intact into various ventilation
codes. It has some requirements which
are simple to verify but others are neces-
sarily complex where verification of
compliance will require a higher level of
understanding of ventilating systems
and the code.
MANUFACTURERS
For manufacturers, the standard will
require modifications to many products
to comply. Some systems and associated
products will become more popular and
others less. Requirements to eliminate
sources of dust and moisture accumula-
tion involve almost all airside products.
Dehumidifying coils must be selected
for almost no moisture carry-over and
drain pans must be positively draining.
Many terminal products such as fan
coils, unit ventilators, fan terminals and
VENTILATION
precepts
• Best strategy - reduce or eliminate source of pollution
• Dilute contaminates with outdoor air, filtered recirculated air or air transferred from adjacent space
• Separate contamination between building-related and people-related sources
• Minimum ventilation and supply airflow rates for all occupancy zones
• Three design approaches allowed depending on building size, complexity and other factors
• Prescriptive minimum outside air requirements for building contaminants
• Prescriptive or CO2 monitored performance alternative for people related outside air requirements
• Smoking not permitted to meet the standard, are addressed only in the appendix to achieve acceptable
“perceived indoor air quality”
• Responsibility for indoor air quality defined through out life of building with design, commissioning,
and maintenance documentation required
3 2
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
unitary products will have difficulty
meeting upgraded filter requirements
without redesign.
The options for analytical design,
multiple space analysis, and demand
control by sensing CO in garages and
CO2 in occupied spaces should encour-
age development in sensors, filtration
and controls. The limit of 60% maxi-
mum relative humidity for humid cli-
mates will be difficult to achieve at part
load for unitary DX products and valve
controlled, chilled water fan coils. Sepa-
rate dehumidification units and latent
heat exchange will undoubtedly become
more popular.
In summary, there will be winners and
losers. Manufacturers that come up with
creative solutions have a lot to gain.
SUMMARY OF REVISED
VENTILATION STANDARD
The proposed revisions to ASHRAE
Standard 62 are very comprehensive. The
following summary will provide the
reader with some idea of the major
changes as submitted to the standards
committee. It in no way covers all the
details for all the requirements. Based on
the number of comments received from
the public review just completed, another
review will probably be required. In the
meantime it behooves ASHRAE mem-
bers who have not commented on the first
draft to become familiar with it.
PRECEPTS
• Best strategy - reduce or eliminate
source of pollution.
• Dilute contaminants with outdoor
air, filtered recirculated air or air trans-
ferred from adjacent spaces.
• Separate ventilation requirements
between building related and people re-
lated sources.
• Minimum outdoor ventilation and
supply airflow rates for all occupancy
zones.
• Three design approaches allowed
depending on building size, complexity,
and other factors.
• Smoking not permitted to meet the
standard. Addressed only in the appen-
dix to achieve acceptable “perceived in-
door air quality.”
• Responsibility for indoor air quality
defined throughout life of building with
design, commissioning, and mainte-
nance documentation required.
BASIC ASSUMPTIONS
• Building-related and human-related
contaminants are generally additive in
their sensory effects. Therefore, the pre-
scribed amount of outdoor air for occu-
pant-generated contaminants cannot be
used to also remove building-related
contaminants.
• Biological contaminants (Virus,
fungi, bacteria, etc.). can and do cause
significant health problems. Therefore,
good particle filtration is important for
health.
• CO2 is a surrogate for human bioef-
fluents only, not for IAQ
• It takes about three times (15 cfm
vs. 5 cfm per person or 7.05 L/s vs. 2.4
L/s per person) as much fresh air to
achieve unadapted (visitor) perceived
comfort as for adapted (occupant) per-
ceived comfort for bioeffluents. Hu-
mans adapt rather quickly to most odors.
Therefore, the ventilation requirements
for building and people contaminants
are based on adapted occupants.
• Microbial growth is a major factor
in building-related illness. It requires
moisture and a source of nutrients, such
as dust, to grow. A sustained space rela-
tive humidity of 70% will provide suffi-
cient
moisture
for
many
micro-
organisms to grow.
Class I
without unusual sources
(offices, classrooms, hotel
rooms) may recirculate or use
as transfer air without
pretreatment
Class III
significant contaminant
intensity (public toilets,
commercial kitchens, indoor
swimming pools) may be
recirculated if first cleaned to
contaminant levels of Class I air
Class IV
noxious or toxic fumes or gases
(paint spray booths, filtered
fume hoods, refrigerating
machinery rooms) shall not be
recirculated or used for transfer
air and shall be directly
exhausted to the outside
Class II
mild contaminant intensity (e.g.
copy rooms, dining areas, single
toilet rooms) may be
recirculated within room or class
III, IV, or V spaces or
recirculated to any class space if:
Cleaned to remove odors and
80% particulate filters @ 1-3
microns (if high moisture,
dehumidified to level OK for
diluted 9 parts Class I to 1 part
Class II or outdoor air and no
plenum fan terminals or fan
coils withing 10 ft.
Class V
high concentrations of
dangerous particles,
bioaerosols or gasses (exhaust
from fuel burning appliances,
evap condenser and cooling
tower outlets) shall not be
recirculated or used for transfer
air and shall be directly
exhausted to the outside
A i r C l a s s i f i c a t i o n s
or
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
33
• It is necessary to ensure that ventila-
tion is delivered right to the occupants,
not just brought into the building. The
concept of system ventilation efficiency
has been introduced to quantify how ef-
fectively a system can be expected to
distribute its outdoor air intake.
• The benefits of humidification are
inconclusive. Some studies show a pos-
itive impact on perceived IAQ and oth-
ers
the
opposite
from
a
health
standpoint. Therefore, it is not required
by the standard.
• Buildings cannot provide accept-
able indoor air quality in the presence of
environmental tobacco smoke.
DESIGN DOCUMENTATION
REQUIRED
• Design criteria.
• Air cleaning and filtration efficien-
cy for each AHU.
• Minimum outdoor airflow rates for
each AHU.
• Outdoor air quality assessment.
• Assumed air change effectiveness.
• Means to measure and balance out-
door air and provide temporary exhaust.
• Design intent narrative.
• Control sequence narrative.
• VAV box minimum supply air set
points.
• Humid climate design provisions.
• Building envelope construction.
AIR CLASSIFICATIONS
Air is divided into 5 classes which
reflect the expected degree of contami-
nation. Acceptability of air for recircula-
tion and required separation distance
between exhaust and intakes is a func-
tion of the class of air.
Class I Without unusual contaminant
sources (offices, classrooms, hotel
rooms). This air may be recirculated or
used as transfer air without pretreat-
ment.
Class II Mild contaminant intensity
(e.g. copy rooms, dining areas, single
toilet rooms). This air may be recircu-
lated within it’s own space or to class III,
IV, or V spaces or recirculated to any
class space if:
Cleaned to remove odors and 80%
particulate filters @ 1-3 microns (if high
moisture, dehumidified to level OK for
filters); or, Diluted 9 parts Class I to 1
part Class II or outdoor air; and no ple-
num fan terminals or fan coils within 10
ft. (3.05 m).
Class III Significant contaminant
intensity (public toilets, commercial
kitchens, indoor swimming pools. This
air may be recirculated if first cleaned to
contaminant levels of Class I air.
Class IV Noxious or toxic fumes or
gasses (paint spray booths, filtered fume
hoods, refrigerating machinery rooms).
This air shall not be recirculated or used
for transfer air and shall be directly
exhausted to the outside.
Class V High concentrations of dan-
gerous particles, bioaerosols or gasses
(exhaust from fuel burning appliances,
evap condenser and cooling tower out-
lets. This air shall not be recirculated or
used for transfer air and shall be directly
exhausted to the outside.
VENTILATION DESIGN
PROCEDURES
Ventilation design methods
• Simple Systems Procedure - mini-
mum ventilation and supply air rates per
unit floor area from tables for simple
systems (e.g. single zone) with “built in”
factors for ventilation effectiveness and
system efficiency
• Prescriptive Procedure - method
to determine minimum outside air into,
and minimum total airflow to, each
space for more complex systems. The
minimum design ventilation rates for
occupancies and types of space are pro-
vided in a table.
Corrections for ventilation system
efficiency, air change effectiveness and
critical zone analysis are made to the
above design ventilation rates based on
three system categories:
1. “Once through” 100% outside air
systems such as ventilation for fan coil
terminals;
2. Single zone systems such as with
all air unitary heating/cooling units; and,
3. Multiple zone systems such as con-
stant or variable volume reheat, double
duct, and fan terminal.
For multiple zone systems, the pro-
cedure required to establish the total
amount of outside air involves identify-
ing the “critical zone” and then solving
equations to assure that the minimum
amount of outside air and total supply
air will get to each zone. Calculation
can be done manually but is best done
on a computer spread sheet. It recog-
nizes the outside air content of supply
air whether directly piped to the space
or indirectly through transfer air from
adjacent spaces
• Analytical Procedure - a method
of determining ventilation rates based
on analysis of expected contaminant
emission for the specific building. Rec-
ommended for spaces where there are
strong or numerous sources, for spaces
where credit for air cleaning is to be
taken, for spaces being designed for es-
pecially low concentrations of contam-
inants or for spaces where other levels
of acceptance are the design goal.
Odor, sensory irritation, and health ef-
fects are all considered. Acceptable in-
door air quality based on limiting
contaminant concentrations is the de-
sign goal. Each contaminant of concern
must be analyzed individually to arrive
at a system solution. Generally used for
unusual or intermittent sources.
Minimum flow rates (prescriptive
procedure)
DVR (Design Ventilation Rate)
Required outdoor air ventilation to a
space based on the type of space, its
occupancy pattern, sources of indoor air
contaminants in that space, and credits
(if applicable) for cleaning recirculated
air. These rates have an area component
related to floor area for building-related
contaminants and another related to the
number of people:
DVR = Rp Pd D + RbAb
Rp = rate per person
Pd = design # people
D = people diversity
Rb = rate/building sqft
Ab = area building
MSR (Minimum Supply Rate)
A combination of outside air and
recirculated air (when necessary) to sat-
isfy a minimum requirement of 15 cfm
per person in occupied spaces on hourly
averaged basis. The recirculated portion
must be filtered to the “equivalent” of
outdoor air.
MSR=>15 (Pd D)/Eac and= > DVR/Eac
if DVR <15 Pd, then
MSR = (DVR + Vr)/Eac
and Vr = 100 (15 Pd D - DVR)/Ef
VENTILATION
3 4
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
Pd = design # people
D = people diversity
Eac = air change effectiveness
DVR = design ventilation rate
Vr = recirculated air adjusted
for filtration efficiency
Ef = filter efficiency
Minimum Total Outdoor Air Rate
Vot = Vou/Ev
where:
Vou = uncorrected outdoor air rate equal
to sum of DVR required by each
space served by the system
Ev = ventilation system efficiency - the
efficiency with which the system
delivers outdoor air to the least
well ventilated space (critical
zone), takes into account AHU
mixing effectiveness, air change
effectiveness and multiple space
analysis.
Note: multiple space analysis is best
done using a computer program (e.g. the
spread sheet is available on the Internet
with the public review draft).
Ventilation controls
• Zone fans for unitary systems that
are used for ventilation must operate
continuously while space is occupied or
provide controls to ensure hourly aver-
aged MSR.
• Pre-occupancy ventilation required
depending on the magnitude of the
building component of the DVR.
BUILDING DESIGN
REQUIREMENTS
Air intake minimum separation
distances (ft.)
• Minimum distance specified from
property line, traffic, cooling towers and
other sources of contaminants.
Air intake - rain entrainment (also
applies to equipment)
• Max. 0.01 oz./ft2 per AMCA std.
500-1994 (15 min.) for louvers and mist
eliminators.
• Max. 1000 ft/min. (5.1 m/s) and tilt-
ed 45 degrees down for rain hoods.
• Louvers or mist eliminators with
drain pans - same std. as for cooling coils.
• Bird screens - prevent penetration
by 1/2 in. (1.27 cm) probe, corrosion re-
sistant and < 8 in. (20.32 cm) inside face
of air entry.
Air exhaust and vent outlets
• Minimum distances are specified
based on “stretched string” distance in
ft. (ft. X .3048 = meters) between closest
exhaust and intake point. Dilution fac-
tors apply depending on class of air (e.g.
Class 1 = 5 ft., Class 5 = 50 ft.). Separate
exhaust outlet distances for property line
(e.g. Class 1 = 0, Class 5 = 15 ft.).
Note: Two-year implementation delay
for manufactured equipment.
minimum
stretched string distance, Q is limited to
>150 cfm (70.84 L/s) and <3000 cfm (1
416 L/s) Q = exhaust volume, cfm
D = dilution factor
V = exhaust discharge velocity, fpm,
positive blowing away, negative blow-
ing towards inlet
Air distribution systems
• Provide means to measure and ad-
just airflow rate at outdoor intake and to
each space.
EQUIPMENT REQUIREMENTS
Coils
• Eight row max. unless 18 ins. (45.7
cm) space between.
• Negligible water carry over beyond
drain pan. The carryover is defined as
0.04 oz./ft2 (1.184 mL/m2) per hour at
ARI std. rating conditions.
• Drain pans sloped and trapped under
all dehumidifying coils. The pans must
drain in 3 minutes with fan on and pud-
dles <2 in. (5.08 cm) in diam. and < 1/8
in. (.31 cm) deep.
• Auxiliary or secondary drain pans
do not require sloping or trapping.
• No exposed insulation from up-
stream end of coil to end of drain pan nor
closer than 1/2 in. (1.27 cm) to headers.
Humidifiers, Air Washers, and Di-
rect Evaporative Coolers
• Potable make-up supply.
• No chemicals or additives emitted
into air (unless approved by government).
• Nothing down stream within ab-
sorption distance recommended by
manufacturer.
Access for inspection and mainte-
nance (equipment and built-up
systems)
• Around unit - as recommended by
manufacturer.
• Access doors and panels for inspec-
tion and cleaning in outdoor intakes,
mixed air plenums, up stream of 4 row
or less heating coil, up and downstream
of greater than 4 row heating coils, all
dehumidifying cooling coils, air wash-
ers, evaporative coolers, heat exchang-
ers, air cleaners, drain pans, fans,
humidifiers, airflow measuring stations.
• To all ventilation system compo-
nents, e.g. VAV terminals, fan coil
units, sensors, etc.
Filters
• Sixty percent ASHRAE 52.2 mini-
mum efficiency at 3 microns (equivalent
to 30% dust spot efficiency under 52.1)
for all HVAC equipment supplying air
through duct work exceeding 10 ft.
(3.048 m) in length or through humidifi-
er, evaporative cooling, fin tube heating
coil or cooling coil including fan coil
units, unit ventilators and other unitary
equipment.
• Filter racks to minimize bypass air.
Note: Two-year implementation delay
for unitary manufactured equipment.
Internal Insulation
• Metal nosing or sleeves on leading
edges at fan discharge, access door
openings, and where duct liner preceded
by uninsulated duct.
• “Durable” when rated per UL181 or
ASTM C 1071 erosion tests.
• No exposed insulation for Class 4 or
5 return or exhaust air.
• No exposed insulation from leading
edge of dehumidifier coil or water spray
coil to end of drain pan (may be coated
or double wall).
External insulation (with sealed
vapor retarder) required for:
• Cooling ductwork except where in
conditioned space or return plenums
other than in humid climates.
• Chilled water supply and return
piping.
• Domestic cold water piping <55°F
(12.8°C).
SYSTEM OPERATION AND
CONTROL
VAV system
• System shall measure and control
outdoor air at building intake > 90% of
minimum required
• Minimum zone supply rates (MSR)
specified.
• Time averaged MSR on hourly basis
S
0.09 Q D
V 400
⁄
–
(
)
=
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
35
is acceptable such as in a VVT system.
• If fan terminal has fin tube coil or
longer than 10 ft. (3.048 m) of supply
duct, air must be filtered.
Pre-occupancy ventilation controls
• Formula provided for time ventila-
tion is required before occupancy.
• Exceptions for hotel/motel and oth-
er irregularly occupied spaces and spac-
es requiring less than 15 minute pre-
ventilation.
Optional CO2 based demand
control ventilation
• Requires minimum outside air equal
to building ventilation requirement (Vb)
adjusted for system ventilation efficiency.
• Can not be used if ventilation design
used simple systems approach.
Optional CO based demand control
• Acceptable for ventilation control in
garages.
• Exception: where diesel fumes are
main contaminant.
Temporary reduction for systems
such as VAV changeover
• May go below design minimum pro-
vided hourly averaged ventilation rate
maintained.
Episodic period of poor air quality
• May reduce below requirements for
up to 4 hr./day if outside contaminants ex-
ceed specified limits and are not filtered.
Occupant controlled ventilation
• Controls must be readily accessible.
• Minimum building ventilation must
be maintained.
Outside air delivery to fan coil
units and other ceiling unitary
equipment
• Within 5 ft. (1.524 m) of fan coil
unit FCU and ceiling unitary equipment
• or 15 ft. (4.572 m) if outside air di-
rected toward unit at 500 fpm (2.54 m/s).
Humidity control in humid climates
• Sixty percent maximum average
daily RH when building occupied under
full and part load conditions, 70% max-
imum RH when building not occupied.
• No minimum RH, humidification
not required.
• Net positive or neutral average
building pressure in humid climates.
Supplemental exhaust
• May be required for future renova-
tions (see Construction and Start-up
section).
• Design documents must indicate
how it can be provided.
Combustion air and unvented
appliances
• Ventilation requirements may not be
sufficient for make-up air requirements.
• Make-up air to be provided per local
codes.
CONSTRUCTION AND SYSTEM
STARTUP
• Written notification to “responsible
party” before any major construction.
• Comply with all codes related to
water, air, and radon penetration of en-
velope.
• Elevators protected from garage
fumes.
• Filters must be in place for operation.
• Spaces with major construction,
renovation, or remedial work must be
isolated, maintained at negative pressure
and keep return ducts capped.
• Protection from weather and mois-
ture for porous materials.
• Substitutions from spec must be re-
viewed carefully.
• Air systems shall be balanced in ac-
cordance with
• ANSI/ASHRAE Std. 111-1988 or ap-
proved alt.
• Drain pans shall be tested for con-
formance per designated test procedure.
• Ventilation supply air systems shall
be substantially free of dirt and debris
and clean filters installed before startup.
• Minimum and maximum ventilation
capability shall be tested to confirm sys-
tem design (outside air dampers and
VAV system controls).
• Ventilation systems must purge 48
hours before occupancy after major con-
struction.
• Inspection and documentation of
each ventilation system.
OPERATION AND MAINTENANCE
• Responsible party for Operation
and Maintenance (O&M) shall be iden-
tified and shall acknowledge their re-
sponsibility.
• O&M staff shall be competent.
• Provide owner with O&M manual,
control diagrams, air balance report,
construction drawings and design crite-
ria/assumptions documentation.
• Inspection and maintenance records
shall be maintained.
• Design documentation maintained
on site and all changes documented.
• Minimum outdoor and supply air
rates must be supplied continuously
while building operated.
• Pre-occupancy ventilation shall be
provided per design.
• Ventilation must be modified to
comply with any change of use.
• Cooling tower water systems shall
be treated and maintenance recorded.
• Particle filter change-out shall be es-
tablished (at max. pressure drop, on
timed basis, or if wet).
• Gas phase and electronic filters
changed per manufacturer’s recommen-
dations.
• Outdoor air dampers shall be visual-
ly inspected or remotely monitored to
confirm performance every 3 months or
per O&M documentation.
• Supply and return air plenums in-
spected every 2 years.
• Coils (other than room units) shall be
visually inspected at least every 6 months
and shall be cleaned when fouled.
• Drain pans shall be inspected and
cleaned if needed at least every 6
months in humid climates and annually
elsewhere.
• Outdoor air louvers, bird screens
and adjacent areas shall be inspected
and cleaned at least every 6 months.
• Sensors for demand control such as
CO2 shall be recalibrated a minimum of
once every 6 months or per O&M.
• Airflow for supply, return, exhaust
and outside air from all air handling units
(not including unit ventilators, induction
units, or fan coil units under 2000 cfm or
944 L/s) shall be measured at design con-
ditions every 5 years. Deviations greater
than 10% must be reported.
NATURAL VENTILATION
• Included in standard but with specif-
ic design criteria.
RESIDENTIAL REQUIREMENTS
• Applies to single family, boarding
houses, and dwelling units of multi-fam-
ily buildings.
• All dwellings require installed ex-
haust capacity in kitchens, baths & toi-
lets. The amount is 100 cfm (47.19 L/s)
intermittent or 25 cfm (11.8 L/s) contin-
uous. Make-up air from adjacent spaces.
Operable window OK for separate toilet
VENTILATION
3 6
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y , 1 9 9 7
or powder room. Local controls for in-
termittent device.
• Outdoor air continuously supplied
to dwelling (15 cfm/person or 5 cfm/100
sq.ft. whichever greater) (7.08 L/s per
person or 2.4 L/s per 9.3 m2 ) by me-
chanical, “demonstrated” or natural ven-
tilation as defined.
• Air intake minimum of 10 ft. (3.05
m) from known contaminant sources,
rodent screen on inlet.
• Outdoor air provided in all habitable
rooms, distribution means described.
• Ventilation devices tested and certi-
fied as in AMCA HVI 920 or equivalent.
• Kitchen exhaust device rated at
maximum of 1.5 sones.
• Ventilation design information, op-
eration and maintenance information
provided to owner.
• Multiple dwellings designed to seal
adjacent spaces (horizontal and vertical)
to ensure outside air requirement is not
transfer air from adjacent spaces.
• Ventilation system shall not lead to
continuous spillage of combustion air of
combustion appliances.
• Self closing gasketed doors between
living area and attached garage.
• Operation and maintenance in ac-
cordance with design documentation.
CONCLUSION
There will undoubtedly be many
additional contributions from the public
review completed Dec. 12. The pro-
posed revisions to the ASHRAE stan-
dard are written in code language with
the expectation that this standard in its
final form will be adopted in part or in
total by code authorities. It represents
“plowing new ground” with the addition
of mandated design documentation,
commissioning,
and
maintenance
requirements.
The belief is that improved design;
and adherence to that design in the star-
tup followed by proper maintainance
throughout the life of the building will
do more to promote acceptable indoor
air quality than just the addition of more
outside air. In fact, proper use of the pre-
scriptive or analytical design approach
should lead to reductions in outside air
from the previous standard. CO2 moni-
toring can reduce the outside air even
further.
Thus, it is believed by this writer that
the new standard deals with all the major
factors in providing good perceived
indoor air quality. It sets the stage for
even further reductions in outdoor air as
experience is gained in reducing build-
ing-related
contaminants,
improved
air cleaning, and better sensors for
dynamic control of VOC and particulate
contamination.
This bodes well as the industry fights
to reduce energy consumption in the
face of the next challenge, global warm-
ing.

ASHRAE Journal|June 2002
47
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, June, 2002. © Copyright 2002 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or
in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
Sizing Replacement
Chiller Plants
About the Author
I
By Deng Shiming, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
Deng Shiming, Ph.D., is associate professor at
the Department of Building Services Engineering
at The Hong Kong Polytechnic University.
n many buildings, the installed cooling capacity of the chiller plant is
greater than actually required.1 Plant oversizing is a consequence of
one or more of the following:
•
Lack of actual end-use data;
•
Inadequacy in cooling load calcu-
lation methods. It has been said, “the cal-
culation of cooling load can never be
more than a good estimation of the ac-
tual cooling load.”2 However, for various
reasons, such as a degree of uncertainty
about what the actual peak load will be,
estimations are often on the high side. In
many cases, building cooling loads are
estimated by rules of thumb, resulting in
overestimated peak loads;3
•
The possible misuse of safety
margins or standby capacity for sizing
chillers;
•
The fee arrangements of consulting
engineers, which may be linked to the
total sum of project investment;
•
The speculative nature of some
buildings (actual usage may ultimately
differ from what was originally intended).
Oversized chiller plants often are op-
erated in an energy inefficient manner.
Normally, a chiller is operated at its maxi-
mum operating efficiency region, with a
load of around 80% of its maximum ca-
pacity. An oversized chiller likely oper-
ates for most of the time at a lower load
condition, resulting in a lower operating
efficiency. The situation may be worse
for plants with equally sized constitut-
ing chillers operating in extremely low
building in Hong Kong. The new total
cooling capacity was determined using
the bi-hourly operating data of the exist-
ing plant recorded by plant operators
over a full year. Hourly and monthly ac-
tual cooling load profiles were obtained.
The maximum cooling load was identi-
fied and used as the basis for determin-
ing the new total cooling capacity to be
installed. Also, by analyzing the load
profiles, the cooling load distribution
frequency was obtained. This helped de-
termine the number of constituting chill-
ers and their respective sizes to ensure
the highest possible operating efficiency
for most of the plant’s operating time af-
ter the replacement.
The Case Study
The hotel building was built in 1988
and has a total floor area of 37 000 m2
(398 265 ft2), with 15 stories above the
ground and two levels of basement. In
this five-star hotel, there are more than
450 guest rooms, as well as restaurants,
business centers, etc. The hotel build-
ing is centrally air conditioned, with its
chiller plant located in the basement.
Since the building is close to the sea-
front, there are four equally sized direct
sea water-cooled water chillers, each
having a rated cooling capacity of 580
ton (2040 kW). CFC 11 is the refriger-
load conditions (e.g., winter operation
in Hong Kong, when a large chiller has
to be operated to cope with a small load).
Currently, many water chillers use
CFCs as refrigerants. Because of the ac-
celerated phaseout of CFC refrigerants,
these water chillers need to be upgraded
either by retrofit or replacement. For wa-
ter chillers older than 10 years, and for
long-term planning concerning younger
CFC chillers, replacement is recom-
mended.4 This not only eliminates the
CFC problem, but also upgrades the over-
all energy performance of the chiller
plant, helping contribute to sustainable
development.
Chiller replacement offers a unique
opportunity for a thorough review of the
installed cooling capacity of a chiller
plant. Normally, key plant operating pa-
rameters such as electrical power input,
chilled water flow rate, chilled water tem-
peratures, etc., are recorded by plant op-
erators on an hourly or bi-hourly basis.
These recorded data can then be used to
obtain the actual building cooling load
profiles that are useful in optimizing the
new total cooling capacity to be in-
stalled in the plant, and the sizes of indi-
vidual constituting chillers.
This article presents a case study for a
chiller replacement program in a hotel
48
June 2002|ASHRAE Journal
800
700
600
500
400
300
200
100
0
ant used. Given the business nature of the hotel, and the
subtropical climate, air conditioning is provided 24 hours a
day, year-round.
Past operating records show that the chiller plant is enor-
mously oversized, because the highest cooling load recorded
in this building has never been more than 1,000 tons (3516
kW) since it started operation. In winter operation in February,
when the ambient air temperature is about 60.8°F (16°C), the
plant is normally operated at a load of less than 200 tons (703
kW). However, a 580-ton (2040 kW) chiller has to be operated,
leading to very low efficiency, and frequent automatic shut-
down due to low evaporating pressure cutoff.
In response to the CFC issue, the hotel management has
decided to carry out a two-stage chiller replacement program.
In the first stage, two of the four 580-ton (2040 kW) chillers
are to be replaced by two new chillers of smaller capacity but
higher efficiency. To determine the new total installed cool-
ing capacity, as well as the number of new chillers and their
respective sizes, an analysis was made of the supply and re-
turn temperatures and the mass flow rate of chilled water,
which were recorded on a bi-hourly basis for the 12 months
of 2000. This year was typical in terms of hotel business
operation and local weather.
To reduce the amount of data to be processed without los-
ing adequate representation and accuracy, only the operat-
ing data on a selected day of the month were analyzed, as-
suming that the load profiles of all other days in that month
were the same as that of the day selected. This assumption
proved reasonable as it has been demonstrated that the en-
ergy use for space cooling in Hong Kong hotels is not di-
rectly influenced by occupancy levels/usage levels of meet-
ing and dining facilities in the hotels.5 Because it is not pos-
sible to open the hotel windows, the air conditioning must be
turned on (usually at a low fan speed setting) in unoccupied
guestrooms and/or meeting/dining facilities, to maintain an
odor-free internal environment.
To ensure consistency for the 12 months, data from the same
day each month should be selected for processing (e.g., data
from the 21st day of each month). In this case study, the data
from the 27th day of each month were analyzed as the yearly
maximum cooling load occurred on Aug. 27, 2000. It is neces-
sary to ascertain whether this maximum load occurred when
the weather was at or near its peak dry- and wet-bulb tempera-
tures. In the current case study, the daily maximum air dry-
bulb temperature on Aug. 27 was 83.1°F (28.4°C), with a very
high daily mean relative humidity of 93%.6 When compared
to the outside design dry- and wet-bulb temperatures for cool-
ing in Hong Kong (91.8°F and 79°F respectively [33.2 °C and
26.1 °C] at 0.4% levels), 7 although the dry-bulb temperature
was lower, the enthalpy of the outside air was higher than that
at the peak design condition because of higher relative hu-
midity. This confirmed that the maximum load occurred
when the weather was at its peak.
Figure 1 shows an example of the bi-hourly cooling load
profiles of the hotel building (October 2000). The peak load
for the month was just over 700 tons (2461 kW), occurring at
2 p.m., with the minimum load of 357 tons (1255 kW) occur-
ring at 4 a.m.
After compiling the load profiles for all 12 months, as shown
in Figure 2, the yearly maximum and minimum cooling loads
were identified. As can be seen from Figure 2, the yearly peak
load was 950 tons (3340 kW), occurring in August, and the
yearly minimum at 141 tons (495 kW) in February. The yearly
maximum cooling load can be used as the basis for determin-
ing the new total cooling capacity in this chiller replacement
program. Allowing a 10% increase as a safety margin, the total
new installed capacity would be about 1050 tons (3692 kW).
Having determined the new total cooling capacity to be
installed, the next to be calculated is the number of individual
chillers and their respective sizes. This can be done by analyz-
ing the hourly cooling load profiles (such as that shown in
Figure 1 for October) for all 12 months, to obtain a cooling
load distribution frequency as illustrated in Figure 3. The pro-
cedures to obtain Figure 3 are explained as follows:
First, it was assumed that the load intensity during each two-
hour interval (Figure 1) remained constant. Then, for a particu-
lar range of cooling load (e.g., 250 to 350 tons [879 to 1230
kW]) in the ith month, the monthly total number of hours (Hi)
when the chiller plant was operated in this particular load
range can be calculated by:
