• Dramatic reduction in the design heating loads from 1,091
to 607 Mbh. Heating load reduction to 200 Mbh at the
actual operating conditions.
• Reduction in air changes per hour from 44.5 to 23.9 at the
design conditions and to 6.8 at the actual operating condi-
tions.
• The estimated annual operating energy cost declined from
$49,000 for the CV-RH system to $29,126 for the VAV-RH
system, providing an estimated savings of $20,774. The
energy cost at the actual operating conditions was even
lower at $20,230, resulting in higher savings of $29,670.
The reduced air change rates have also resulted in increased
safety due to reduction in the room turbulence, which would
have occurred with the CV-RH system.
Achievements and Results
The system was calibrated to the predetermined airflows and
was fully set up within two days. No unforeseen situations
occurred during this period, and the lab was turned over to the
owner in one week, including the system instructions.
After six months of operation, it was decided to confirm the
design parameters that affected the diversity. The building man-
agement system was programmed to log the trend of the HVAC
system at five minute intervals, 24 hours a day and seven days a
week. In addition, two hoods were equipped with zone presence
sensors, working in conjunction with the fume hood controls.
These zone presence sensors were intended to automatically re-
duce the exhaust airflow and corresponding makeup air by 40%
during the absence of an operator from the detection zone.
The trend log variables included:
• Total makeup air system flows,
• Exhaust airflows from two fume hoods,
• Sash positions of two fume hoods,
• Total exhaust airflow and
• Fume hood exhaust alarms.
The analysis of the data logged during the seven-week pe-
riod between Aug. 5, 1994 and Sept. 30, 1994 produced
useful results. The highlights of these results from the
Table 4: Construction costs and energy savings.
data logging are:
• Figure 2 indicates daily peak makeup airflow. It shows that
the makeup airflow did not exceed 5,600 cfm (2643 L/s) or
43% of the comparable constant volume system.
• Figure 3 indicates daily average makeup air. It shows that
the makeup airflow did not exceed 2,200 cfm (1038 L/s) or
17% of the comparable constant volume system.
• Figure 4 indicates Ventilation System Performance. It

(
shows that the average ventilation rate or exhaust is re-
duced by 83%, compared to the constant volume system.
It also shows that the actual peak exhaust was 43% of the
comparable constant volume system.
• The analysis also indicates that the typical fume hood usage
with full sash opening was about 60–70 minutes per day.
Conclusions
The on-site monitoring confirmed the assumptions and con-
clusions reached during the design. Since the actual makeup
airflow did not exceed 5,600 cfm (2643 L/s), the diversity calcu-
lations used in sizing the HVAC system were confirmed. How-
ever, the highest average makeup airflow of 2,200 cfm (1038
L/s), logged by trending, was considerably lower than the esti-
mated average airflow of 5,500 cfm (2595 L/s). This means that
the researchers followed the operating procedures by lowering
sashes when they were not in front of the hoods. This has
further reduced the payback period of the premium cost of this
control system, to about six months.
The lab has now been in operation for more than two years and
the number of researchers has doubled. During this time the “Di-
versity Alarm” has been initiated only occasionally and the re-
searchers have responded appropriately by lowering the sashes
when they leave the hoods. The researchers have expressed satis-
faction with the design and operation of the HVAC systems.

ASHRAE JOURNAL

A S H R A E J o u r n a l
4 9
About the Author
By Alfred E. Guntermann, P.E.
Fellow ASHRAE
atex Foam Products of Ansonia,
Conn., manufactures foam rubber pil-
lows and mattresses in a large indus-
trial facility. An engineering consult-
ing firm was selected to study, provide engi-
neering and manage the replacement of the
facility’s chiller system.
The Talalay Process is used at the manufac-
turing facility, which uses refrigeration units to
produce –20°F (–29°C) glycol that is pumped
through aluminum molds and freezes the latex
foam compound to form tiny cells. Once the cells
are formed, the compound must be cooked to
“set” the foam rubber at high temperatures by a
boiler that heats the glycol to 230°F (110°C).
In-between the –20°F and 230°F (–29°C and
110°C) glycol, the Talalay Process uses two in-
termediate temperature glycols to provide “free”
preheating and precooling of the molds without
more energy—similar to an HVAC exhaust air/
makeup air coil run around loop system.
The aluminum molds are glycol-to-latex rub-
ber heat exchangers. Four primary piping loops
provide the four glycol temperature fluids to
each mold through secondary branch lines.
Branch lines connect the primary loops to the
molds through headers. At both the inlet and
outlet of the mold header, each branch line has a
two-way valve that controls the temperature of
the glycol in the mold. Once the production cycle
is started manually, the glycol temperature is
controlled by an automatic timer, which cycles
the inlet and outlet two-way valves. Production
typically occurred 24 hours per day for approxi-
mately six days per week.
The original refrigeration system used six 502
hermetic reciprocating chillers each rated at 160
tons (563 kW) if producing 45°F (7°C) chilled
water, but only 28 tons (99 kW) if producing
–20°F (–29°C) glycol. About 900 gpm (57 L/s) of
–20°F (–29°C) glycol was circulated through the
primary cold supply loop and cycled to the
molds. The chillers water-cooled the condens-
ers and were supplied from a well water system
based on approximately 1 gpm/ton (0.0179
mL/J) and 95°F (35°C) condenser head pressure.
Alfred Guntermann, P.E., is the principal of
Guntermann Engineering in New Haven, Conn.
He is the secretary for ASHRAE’s College of
Fellows and past president of ASHRAE’s Fort
Wayne, Ind. chapter. This is Guntermann’s sec-
ond ASHRAE Technology Award.
Refrigeration System Replacement
In a Manufacturing Operation

A typical pillow mold
showing six recently
completed pillows. Gly-
col at –15°F (–26°C) is cir-
culated from the new
chiller to up to 48 molds.
The “cold” glycol is part
of the process to form
cells in the rubber. The
mold receives the cold
glycol, followed by low
intermediate glycol, high
intermediate glycol, and
finally the cells are
“cooked” in hot glycol.
The entire process takes
about 30 to 40 minutes
per mold.

For this project, two 120 ton (422 kW), 300 hp (224 kW) open
screw compressor chillers totaling 240 tons (845 kW) were in-
stalled to replace the six chillers, which provided 168 tons (592
kW). This improved the average full load efficiency from 2.8
kW/ton to 1.95 kW/ton. Installing evaporative condensers,
pumping systems and a variable speed drive on one chiller
improved the average part load efficiency to 1.33 kW/ton.
Ammonia was chosen as the chiller’s refrigerant for environ-
mental reasons, even though R-22 was slightly more efficient at
–30°F (–34°C) suction temperature.
New Equipment
Base Chiller Analysis. Based on the published
manufacturer’s data for the original chillers at the operating
conditions of –30°F (–34°C) suction and 95°F (35°C) condens-
ing temperatures, the full load efficiencies are 2.8 kW/ton. Com-
paratively, the two new 120 ton (422 kW) chillers’ full load effi-
ciency for 94°F (35°C) condensing temperature is 1.95 kW/ton.
This improved the chillers’ full load efficiency by 27.6%.
Evaporative Condenser. The original chiller system utilized a
10 hp (7 kW), 300 gpm (19 L/s) well water pump to supply a shell
and tube heat exchanger. Even though the shell and tube heat
exchanger was cleaned twice a year to remove a heavy tube film
buildup, the chiller’s condensing temperatures were observed
to be as high as 120°F (49°C), which corresponds to a full load
efficiency of 3.7 kW/ton, compared to 2.8 kW/ton at 95°F (35°C).
Over the year, a bad fouling factor is likely to reduce the aver-
age efficiency by 5% to 10%, or 20 to 40 kWh. The secondary
side of the heat exchanger supplied both an industrial process
and condensing water regulating valves, which maintained 95°F
(35°C) chiller condensing temperature.
Two oversized 180 ton (634 kW) evaporative condensers
allow the chiller to operate at 89°F (32°C) full load condensing
temperature, which improved the chiller full load efficiency from
1.95 kW/ton to 1.7 kW/ton. Further, the evaporative condenser
fans drive the chiller head pressure down to 65°F (18°C) before
modulating, which improves the chiller efficiency to 1.2 kW/
ton. The evaporative condenser loads vary with outdoor air
temperature as outlined in Table 1.
Based on the Connecticut weather data, the outdoor air dry-
bulb temperature is above 55°F (13°C) for 3,800 hours per year,
or 44% of the 8,640 hours per year.
Table 1: Evaporative condenser consumption (kW).
Since the original chillers’ 10 hp (7 kW) well water pump and
30 hp (22 kW) condenser/process water pump have been elimi-
nated, and since the condenser fouling factor improved sub-
stantially, the additional evaporative condenser fan and pump
electrical loads will be less than the original well water and con-
denser pump electrical loads.
New Screw Compressor Energy Analysis. The original chiller
reciprocating compressors had cylinder unloaders. The pub-
lished part load efficiency varied from 2.8 kW/ton at 100% load
to 3.6 kW/ton at 18% load, with an average of 3.11 kW/ton. The
new screw compressors part load efficiency varied 1.7 kW/ton
at 100% load, 2.0 kW/ton at 75% load, 2.5 kW/ton at 50% load
and 4.3 kW/ton at 25% load.
Since there was no set production schedule, it was decided
that the average chiller efficiency was the best chiller efficiency
estimate. The screw manufacturer’s published data for the part
load kW/ton for 89°F (32°C) condensing temperature and
–30°F (–34°C) suction temperature are outlined in Table 2.
At 65°F (18°C) condensing temperature, a similar calculation
provides an average chiller efficiency of 1.58 kW/ton. Since the
weather data for Connecticut shows the outdoor dry bulb tem-
perature is below 65°F (18°C) 56% of the year, the yearly aver-
age kW/ton is:
= 0.44 × [(2.25 + 1.58)/2] + 0.56 ×1.58 = 1.73 kW/ton
Based on a fixed number of molds and a calculated average
load per mold, the chillers would operate at an average of 120
tons (422 kW). Based on 120 tons (422 kW), 7,000 hours per
year, 1.73 kW/ton, and $.09/ kWh, the new chillers average
annual cost would be $130,788 compared to the original chillers
average annual cost of $235,116 at 3.11 kW/ton at the same
conditions for an annual savings of $104,328.
Variable Speed Drive. A variable speed drive was added to
one of the two screw compressors. Based on the manufacturer’s
Table 2: New chillers. Constant speed/slide valve is at 89°F
(32°C) condensing temperature and –30°F (–34°C) suction
temperature.

5 2
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 1 9 9 8
data, compared to a slide vane, the variable speed drive im-
proves the screw compressor’s part load efficiency from (1) 2.0
kW/ton to 1.8 kW/ton at 75% load, (2) from 2.5 kW/ton to 1.9
kW/ton at 50% load, and (3) from 4.3 kW/ton to 2.9 kW/ton at
25% load at 89°F (32°C) condensing temperature and –30°F
(–1°C) suction temperature. The variable speed drive chiller
operated continuously. The second chiller with a slide vane
operated fully loaded at 1.7 kW/ton when the total mold load is
between 120 and 240 tons (422 and 845 kW).
To minimize the variable speed drive losses a 98% efficiency
drive was selected, and the peak load only increased from 1.7
kW/ton to 1.71 kW/ton. Based on the manufacturer’s data, the
calculated average efficiency at 89°F (32°C) condensing tem-
perature is 1.93 kW/ton and at 65°F (18°C) condensing tem-
perature is 1.42 kW/ton. With a variable frequency drive, the
annual average kW/ton is:
= 0.44 × [(1.93 + 1.42)/2] + 0.56 ×1.42 = 1.52 kW/ton
Based on the new chiller’s average of 1.52 kW/ton, 120 tons
(422 kW), 7,000 hour per year and $0.09/kWh, the annual chiller
energy cost with a variable drive is $114,912 per year for an
average annual energy savings of $15,876.
–10°F (–23°C) Glycol Temperature. Increasing the chiller’s
glycol supply temperature from –20°F (–29°C) to –10°F (–23°C)
would increase the chiller suction temperature from –30°F to –
20°F (–34°C to –29°C). This would reduce energy by (1) im-
proving the chiller efficiency, and (2) reducing the required chiller
load. While increasing the glycol temperature too much could
cause mattress/pillow failure, the original reciprocating chillers
utilized return water step controller which cycled the glycol
temperature between –30°F to –10°F (–34°C to –29°C).
Because the new screw chiller can operate from a single leav-
ing-chilled-water proportional set point control, a more con-
stant leaving glycol temperature can be attained.
Based on the manufacturer’s data at –10°F (–23°C) glycol
temperature (–20°F [–29°C] suction temperature) with a vari-
able speed drive, the chiller efficiency is 1.79 kW/ton for 89°F
(32°C) condensing temperature and 1.23 kW/ton for 65°F (18°C)
condensing temperature. Therefore, with a variable frequency
drive and –10°F (–23°C) glycol, the yearly average kW/ton can
be calculated as follows:
= 0.44 × [(1.79 + 1.23)/2] + 0.56 × 1.23 = 1.33 kW/ton
Not only does the chiller efficiency improve by raising the
leaving glycol temperature, but the actual mold cooling load is
reduced using a 10°F (–12°C) temperature difference with the

edition formerly in this space.
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
S e p t e m b e r 1 9 9 8
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
5 3
R E F R I G E R A T I O N
heat transfer calculation listed earlier. Thus, the chiller load for
the original fixed number of molds decreases from 137 to 119 tons
(482 to 419 kW). And for the fixed number of molds, the chiller load
is reduced from 120 to 102 tons (422 to359 kW), a 15% reduction.
Based on 1.33 kW/ton, 102 tons, 7,000 hour per year, and $0.09/
kWh, when operating at –10°F (–23°C) glycol temperature with a
variable frequency drive the annual chiller energy cost is $85,491.
Production Efficiency
The original intent of increasing the chiller capacity from 180
tons to 240 tons (634 to 849 kW) was to provide partial standby
in case of a breakdown. However, the new screw compressors
proved to be far more reliable than the original reciprocating
compressors, and up to the full 240 tons (849 kW) has been
utilized to increase production output.
The design team also assisted in evaluating the mold flow
rates, entering glycol temperatures and production rates to
optimize production and energy consumption. As a result, the
cold supply, low intermediate, high intermediate and hot sup-
ply glycol pumps’ capacities were increased. This increased
the pumping energy consumption.
Notably, if the production rate doubles, both the original
chillers energy costs and the new chillers energy costs would
double. The annual energy savings would increase from
$133,789 to $279,602.
Innovation
This was one of the first operational large variable speed
drives utilized on large refrigeration systems. The current prac-
tice among refrigeration manufacturers is to provide multiple
compressors so that more compressors are fully loaded. For
example, the chiller manufacturer recommended two 90-ton (317
kW) chillers and one 70-ton (246 kW) chiller. If the load were
180 tons (634 kW), the two 90-ton (317 kW) chillers could oper-
ate at full load and peak efficiency. But at 185 tons (651 kW), the
third would be very inefficient.
If the efficiency of the smaller chillers were equal to the two
120-ton (422 kW) screw compressors, the average efficiency
would be equal. However, the 90-ton (317 kW) chiller had a full
load efficiency of 2.14 kW/ton at 95°F (35°C) condensing tem-
perature and –31°F (0°C) suction temperature, compared to an
equivalent 1.95 kW/ton for the 120-ton (422 kW) chillers. Fur-
ther, the three chillers increased the initial cost from $270,000 to
$360,000 without the variable speed drive.
The initial cost for the variable speed drive was $54,000, the
annual savings is $15,876, and the simple payback was 3.4 years.
The variable speed drive has allowed fully automatic part load
adjustment for efficiency operation for three years.
Operation and Maintenance
The high costs of maintaining six aging reciprocating chill-
ers that operated approximately 7,000 hours per year was elimi-
nated. The operation of the new chiller system includes auto-
matic stop/start of the second chiller, pumps and evaporative
condensers. The evaporative condensers have sand filters,
water treatment and indoor sumps to allow for automatic drain
down and start-up during winter.
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
5 4
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
S e p t e m b e r 1 9 9 8
Cost Effectiveness
The installed cost for the new screw chiller system with a
variable speed drive, evaporative condensers, two new me-
chanical equipment rooms, ventilation systems, ammonia
detection systems and some piping modifications was
$800,000. Based on the equivalent loads, the original chill-
ers’ annual cost was $235,116 compared to $85,491 for the
new screw compressor system with a variable speed drive,
evaporative condensers, and operating at –10F° (–23°C)
glycol. The $149,625 annual savings provides a 5.35 year
simple payback.
By increasing the pumping system for an additional $135,000,
the annual production rate could be doubled. Including pump-
ing costs and doubling the capacity, the new system would
have an annual energy cost of $238,817 compared to the origi-
nal chillers’ equivalent annual cost of $522,747. Thus, the com-
bined initial cost of $935,000 and annual savings of $283,930
provide a simple payback of 3.29 years.
Because this project replaced the existing chiller process
system, which was vital for the operation of the company,
the changeover to the new systems were critical. The entire
project was fast-tracked and installation planned for a three-
week summer maintenance shutdown. Three competitive
bids were obtained during the engineering phase on all ma-
jor equipment, including chillers, evaporative condensers,
pumps and variable speed drives.
Based on construction bid documents, competitive bids were
obtained from a refrigeration contractor, structural steel con-
tractor, piping contractor, insulation contractor, electrical con-
tractor, interior walls contractor, plumbing contractor and HVAC
contractor. The entire installation went together without prob-
lems, on time and on budget.
The combined improvements increased production, reduced
energy costs, resolved well water problems, ameliorated envi-
ronmental problems and reduced maintenance costs, making
this an extremely cost effective system. The system has been
operating with a minimum of trouble for over three years. And
finally, the project was 50% co-funded by the local electric util-
ity, in an effort to keep industry in Connecticut.

ASHRAE Journal
September 1998
ASHRAE JOURNAL
I
ncreasing combustion airflow rate is a common modifi-
cation to increase power and net efficiency. To get more
power out of a gas turbine, simply increase the mass
flow rate of air into the compressor. More air begets
more power. Automobile engines use inlet air compressors (tur-
bos). Gas turbines (also called combustion turbines because
they can use natural gas, fuel oil and other fuels) use super-
charging or inlet air cooling to increase the mass flow rate.
Combustion turbine inlet air cooling (CTIAC) systems are com-
monly used to increase the air-
flow rate and therefore in-
crease the power produced.
Parameters that affect the
design of CTIAC installations
and operations include the
type of turbine, climate, hours
of operation, the ratio of airflow
rate to power generated, and
the value of the power gener-
ated. This article discusses
methods and techniques used
in CTIAC applications.
Increasing Capacity
The volumetric flow to
most turbines is constant, and
therefore increasing the air
density increases the mass
flow rate. A CTIAC system in-
creases the capacity of tur-
bine/generators by increasing the air density of the combus-
tion air. Typically, the increased mass flow rate also decreases
the rate of fuel consumed per unit of power produced (the heat
rate), after all parasitic cooling power is considered.
The power produced by the turbine is nearly a linear func-
tion of air mass flow rate. By ignoring the additional mass flow
from the fuel, an ideal gas the mass flow rate is:
Hence, power output is a linear inverse function of temperature.
As the inlet air temperature increases, such as on hot sum-
mer days, the capacity of a turbine decreases,1 as shown in
Figure 1. If the inlet air is cooled to a lower temperature, the
power increases, along with increased efficiency (decreased
heat rate). The approximate cycle efficiency, η, can be expressed
in terms of the isentropic compression process from compressor
inlet temperature, T1, to compressor outlet temperature, T2, as:
Hence, the efficiency also increases for decreasing T1.
Turbines
Combustion turbine systems operate as simple cycles or as
combined cycles, shown schematically in Figure 2. In the com-
bined cycle, the combustion turbine operates in parallel with a
steam turbine, where the steam is generated either by combus-
tion turbine exhaust gases with
a heat recovery steam genera-
tor, by supplemental firing to
the turbine exhaust or by us-
ing a separate heat source.
Industrial combustion tur-
bines are single-shaft ,heavy-
duty turbines, with generating
capacities of 20 to more than
250 MW, operating with natu-
ral gas or distillate oil.
Aeroderivative turbines are
modified aircraft engines of 500
kW to over 40 MW capacity,
typically using natural gas fuel.
Both types are rated at the In-
ternational Standards Organi-
zation (ISO) inlet air conditions
of 59°F (15°C) air temperature,
60% relative humidity, and 14.7
psi (101 L) absolute pressure
(sea level), at a generator power factor of 0.9.
Industrial turbines use constant inlet air volumes, while
aeroderivatives operate with multiple compressor shafts and
varying air volumes. Newer turbines operate with lower airflow
rates/units of power and therefore, decrease the cooling re-
quired for an incremental power increase.
Cooling Systems
CTIAC designs include turbines that operate only a few hours
per year, for demonstrating power reserve or for peak demand
power. Both evaporative cooling systems and thermal energy
About the Author

and Program Subcommittee Chair for the Task Group for Com-
bustion Gas Turbine Inlet Air Cooling. He is also the secretary
of ASHRAE Technical Committee 6.9, Thermal Storage.
Turbine Inlet Air Cooling
William E. Stewart, Jr., Ph.D., P.E.
Member ASHRAE

inlet air temperature.

Nomenclature
m mass flow rate
T temperature

R gas constant

Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
September 1998
ASHRAE Journal
3 3
T U R B I N E I N L E T A I R C O O L I N G
storage (TES) systems allow cooling dur-
ing turbine operation without coincident
parasitic power, except for pumps. TES
systems allow the use of small capacity
refrigeration systems, operated only dur-
ing off-peak hours.
For turbines operating continuously
or several hours per day, fuel costs and
availability are important factors, and fa-
vor on-line cooling systems, such as
evaporative systems or refrigeration sys-
tems without thermal storage.
The most prevalent CTIAC system is
evaporative cooling, using wetted media,
due to low installation and operating
costs. Some CTIAC installations use re-
frigeration systems, operated continu-
ously or with TES.
An ideal evaporative cooling process
occurs at constant wet-bulb temperature,
cooling the air to 100% relative humidity.
The inlet warm air is partly in contact with
the liquid water and transfers heat to the
liquid water, evaporating part of the liq-
uid water into vapor. As the air transfers
heat to evaporate the liquid water, the tem-
perature of the air decreases and results
in the air cooling effect.
As an example, an evaporative cool-
ing process is shown in Figure 3, where
the incoming, ambient air is shown as
Condition 1, which is 100°F (38°C) and
30% relative humidity. As the liquid wa-
ter evaporates, cooling occurs along a
line of constant wet bulb, approximately
74°F (23°C). If the process reaches satu-
ration, the air-water vapor mixture is at
Condition 2, where the temperature is 74°F
(23°C). The typical evaporative cooling
system allows the air-water vapor mixture
to reach 85 to 95% of the difference be-
tween the dry bulb air temperature and
the wet bulb temperature. The 90% con-
dition is shown as 3. The mixture tem-
perature at Condition 3 is warmer than at
saturation, approximately 77°F (25°C).
Evaporative cooling can also be used
before or after cooling coils. For example,
air is sensibly cooled to a lower dry-bulb
temperature, for example 85°F (29°C), as
1-4 in Figure 3, then evaporatively cooled
along a line of constant wet bulb of 70°F
(21.1°C). For 90% effectiveness, the out-
let air temperature is 71°F (22°C), Condi-
tion 5. The other option is to first evapo-
ratively cool the air from Condition 1 to
85°F (29°C), Condition 6, and then sensi-
bly cool the air. To reach the same inlet air
temperature of 71°F (22°C) from Condi-
tion 6 using a cooling coil, the air under-
Figure 2: Typical combustion turbine simple (left) and combined cycle systems (right).
goes both sensible and latent cooling to
Condition 7, due to coil cooling charac-
teristics. If a combination of sensible cool-
ing (cooling coils) and evaporative cool-
ing are used, sensible cooling should be
used first and then evaporative cooling
to reach the minimum temperature with-
out having latent cooling (condensation)
by the cooling coils.
A secondary fluid cooling (chilled
water, brine or refrigerant) process is also
shown in Figure 3, where the air-water
vapor mixture is being cooled from the
same initial condition, Condition 1. The
process decreases the enthalpy (and tem-
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
3 6
ASHRAE Journal
September 1998
the economic value of a CTIAC system.
A refrigerant cooling coil system utilizes either vapor com-
pression or waste heat operated absorption. The air cooling
process is identical to the chilled water process shown in Fig-
perature) of the air-water vapor mixture as the mixture is cooled.
The water vapor content (the humidity ratio) remains constant
as the mixture decreases in temperature to Condition 8, 73°F
(23°C). Continued cooling will follow the cooling coil perfor-
mance curve. If cooled to 40°F (4°C), the condition is shown as
Condition 9. Condition 9 represents a mixture where the relative
humidity of the air is near 100% with a decreased humidity ratio,
having forced much of the water vapor to condense.
A chilled water (or brine) system can be utilized in conjunc-
tion with thermal energy storage. The TES system is usually
justified, from a cost standpoint, only for turbines that operate
a few hours per week or to increase reserve power. A chilled
water system will probably have a higher capital cost and use
more energy than a refrigeration cooling coil system because of
the secondary fluid loop, the pumping required, and the in-
creased size of cooling coils.
The chilled water system, though, requires less refriger-
ant piping and inventory, and is therefore less susceptible
to refrigerant leakage. Thermal storage allows the reduction
of refrigeration equipment size and reduces on-peak para-
sitic energy, which has a significant effect upon system
economics.3 Parasitic loads for a TES system that operates
only a few hours per week will usually not severely impact
Figure 3: Evaporative and coil cooling processes.

edition formerly in this space.

ASHRAE Journal
3 7
T U R B I N E I N L E T A I R C O O L I N G
Efficiency Improvements: Base Loaded Turbines. When maxi-
mum power is desired every hour of the year, a continuous
CTIAC system is justified in warm climates to maximize turbine
output and minimize heat rate.
Other Benefits and Considerations
• CTIAC systems require additional space and increase main-
tenance.
• Evaporative media or cooling coils pose a constant inlet air
pressure loss, though the cooling systems may be used during
warm weather. Evaporative media also acts to filter the inlet air.
• CTIAC systems that reduce the air temperature below satu-
ration can produce a significant amount of condensed water–
potentially a valuable resource, that can be used to provide makeup
water for cooling towers or evaporative condensers.
• CTIAC systems are energized only when required.
• Emissions can decrease due to increased overall efficiency.
• A CTIAC system allows matching the inlet air temperature to
the required turbine generating capacity, allowing 100% open inlet
guide vanes and eliminating guide vane pressure loss penalties.
Summary
Combustion turbine capacity is a function of compressor
mass flow rate, typically controlled by inlet air temperature. The
types of cooling systems are varied, the final selection driven
by the cost. Engineering economic analyses of power require-
ments, cooling techniques, site operational requirements, costs
and climatic data are required to evaluate each CTIAC option.
Details of systems, equipment, case histories, economics and
project implementation can be found in the Design Guide: Com-
bustion Turbine Inlet Air Cooling Systems.5
References
1. MacCracken, C. D. 1994. “Overview of the progress and the poten-
tial of thermal storage in off-peak turbine inlet cooling.” ASHRAE
Transactions, 100(1): 569–571.
2. Ebeling, J. A. 1994. “Combustion turbine inlet air cooling alterna-
tives and case histories.” Proceedings of the 27th Annual Frontiers of
Power Conference.
3. EPRI. 1995. Operating Practices Guidebook, TR-105102.
4. Source energy and environmental impacts of thermal energy stor-
age. Thermal Energy Storage Collaborative of the California Energy
Commission (prepared by Tabors Caramanis & Associates). Jan. 1996.
5. Stewart, Jr., W. E. 1997. Design Guide: Combustion Turbine Inlet
Air Cooling Systems (Final Report, RP-902), Atlanta: ASHRAE. 
ure 3. The DRC system can provide cooling during all hours of
turbine operation. The refrigerant system must be sized to meet
the peak cooling required and therefore the refrigeration sys-
tem is larger than for a storage system.
Installations
The turbine/generator at a Nebraska company has an ISO
rating with distillate oil fuel of 59.8 MW and a peak rating of
65.2 MW. Inlet air cooling, from an air ambient temperature of
100°F to 40°F (38°C to 4°C), produces a net power increase of
approximately 12 MW or about 21%.2 The Nebraska site uses
the first CTIAC system with ice storage.
The aeroderivative turbine at a San Diego site generates both
electricity and steam. The refrigerant system operates continu-
ously providing an inlet air temperature of 43°F (6°C), increas-
ing the capacity from the ISO rating of 30 MW to 37.5 MW.
A generating station near Kansas City utilizes two industrial
turbines with evaporative cooling. The turbines are ISO rated
at 48 MW. At the design ambient conditions of 97°F and 75°F
(36°C and 24°C) wet bulb, the evaporative cooling system in-
creases the capacity of each turbine by 3.5 MW.
Advantages of CTIAC
Capacity Enhancement. Use of CTIAC for newer turbines,
with lower airflow rates per unit of power generated, is even
more economical than for older turbines, as the lower flow rates
require less cooling capacity to lower inlet air temperatures, re-
sulting in smaller evaporative coolers, refrigeration equipment
and thermal storage systems.
Heat Rate Improvement. Fuel mass flow rates increase with
increasing inlet air flow and increasing turbine output, but typi-
cally at a smaller rate. CTIAC systems can be used primarily for
the improved heat rate and the corresponding fuel cost savings
per unit of power produced.
Turbine Life Extension. Turbines operating at lower inlet air
temperatures have extended life and reduced maintenance.
Lower and constant turbine inlet air temperatures reduce the
wear and tear on turbines and turbine components.
Increased Combined Cycle Efficiency. Decreased inlet air
temperatures result in decreased exhaust gas temperatures, po-
tentially decreasing the capacity of the heat recovery steam
generators for steam turbines and absorption equipment. The
greater airflow rate of a CTIAC system, though, usually results
in an overall increase in capacity, as the effect of increased
exhaust mass flow rate exceeds the effect of decreased tem-
perature.
Delayed New Generation. With a CTIAC system with in-
creased generation capacity, the requirement for additional ac-
tual or reserve generation capacity can be delayed.
Base Load Efficiency Improvements: Thermal Storage. An
ice or chilled water TES system can help level the base load of
a power generation facility and produce stored energy by elec-
tric chiller equipment during off-peak periods, which tends to
increase the efficiency of power production. Electric chillers
operated at cooler night temperatures have a higher efficiency
and operate at reduced condenser temperatures, which can also
use less source energy.4

3 9
ASHRAE JOURNAL
ariable air volume (VAV) systems provide many
zones of control at a relatively low cost and save
fan horsepower. Additionally, they indirectly control
building humidity at all load conditions. These advan-
tages make them a popular system choice, but achieving proper
ventilation with VAV systems can be confusing.
Consider the simplest and most common system: the single-
duct VAV, illustrated in Figure 1. The air handler delivers pri-
mary air, a mixture of recirculated air and outdoor air, to the VAV
boxes through a single duct. Each VAV box then regulates the
flow of primary air and sends supply air into the space to main-
tain the desired sensible temperature. Leaving each space, re-
turn air is diverted at the air handler to become either exhaust air
or recirculated air.
Providing proper ventilation for this multiple-space VAV sys-
tem presents a three-part challenge:
• Design for peak ventilation capacity.
• Operate with required ventilation at all loads.
• Control to minimize the ventilation energy impact.
Challenge 1: Design for Peak Ventilation Capacity
To size the coil and chiller, the required outdoor airflow at
design (peak load conditions) must be found. Many designers
merely use the sum of the space ventilation requirements to
find outdoor airflow. That would be correct if ventilation air
were delivered directly to each space—systems with separate
distribution paths for ventilation air can be designed—but most
VAV systems use one duct to deliver a mixture of first-pass
outdoor air and recirculated return air to meet both thermal and
ventilation requirements. All spaces receive the same primary
air mixture even though space ventilation needs differ.
When one space, the “critical space,” receives the precise
mixture needed for adequate ventilation, all other spaces are
overventilated. Air returning from the critical space—fully satu-
rated with contaminants from the space—is no longer suitable
for diluting those contaminants; its dilution capacity is com-
pletely “used.” On the other hand, air returning from
overventilated spaces—not fully saturated with space contami-
nants—is still suitable for diluting those contaminants; it still
has “unused” dilution capacity.
Consequently, air returning from all spaces always retains
some dilution capacity. Recirculated return air adds its dilution
capacity to the primary air while exhausted return air discards
its dilution capacity. Exhausted air lowers system dilution ca-
pacity and lowers the efficiency of the ventilation system.
To ensure proper ventilation, ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-
1989, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality 1 man-
dates the use of a multiple-space equation (Equation 6-1)—
derived for recirculation credit and exhaust penalty—when cal-
culating the minimum total outdoor airflow required for correct
ventilation of a multiple space system.
Space Ventilation
The simplistic system illustrated in Figure 1 includes three
spaces and a recirculating air handler. To find minimum total
outdoor airflow for the system, first determine the space-re-
lated parameters:
Space Ventilation Required (Rp or Ra). Using the Ventila-
tion Rate Procedure and Table 2 in Standard 62-1989, find the
ventilation airflow required Rp (cfm/person) or Ra (cfm/ft2) for
each space.
Design Ventilation Rate (DVR). Space ventilation rate (Vo)
for each occupied space can be established (Vo = P × Rp or Vo
= A × Ra). Design ventilation rate is determined either by space
population at design occupancy (P = Pd) or simply by space
area (DVR = Pd × Rp or DVR = A × Ra).
Air Change Effectiveness (Eac). How well does supply air
mix with air in the occupied zone within the space? Properly
selected diffusers typically result in complete mixing (e.g. Eac =
1.0) of cool supply air. Overhead delivery of warm supply air
typically results in stratification and “bypass” (e.g. Eac = 0.8).
Minimum Primary Airflow (Vm). In a single-duct VAV sys-
tem, primary airflow to the box and supply airflow to the space
are equal. Primary airflow to each space modulates between
minimum (Vp = Vs = Vm) and design (Vp = Vs = Vd) airflow in
response to thermal load. This example uses an arbitrary mini-
mum primary setting of 20% of design airflow (Vm = 0.20 × Vd
cfm) for each space.
Worst-case Space Ventilation Fraction (zm). The occupied
zone in each space requires a fraction (z = Vo ÷ Eac ÷ Vs) of
ventilation air in its supply air (Vs).
At worst-case, the highest possible space ventilation frac-
tion (zm) occurs with supply airflow at its minimum setting (Vs =
Vm) and design space ventilation rate is required (Vo = DVR).
This worst-case ventilation fraction must be calculated for each
space (zm = DVR ÷ Eac ÷ Vm). The minimum primary airflow
(Vm) should be set for each space in accordance with overall
design objectives. High minimums slightly increase tempering
(reheat) needs and significantly reduce the total outdoor airflow
requirement. Low minimums slightly reduce tempering needs and
significantly increase the total outdoor airflow requirement.
Ventilation Where It’s Needed
By Dennis A. Stanke
Member ASHRAE
About the Author
Dennis A. Stanke is an applications engineer for the Worldwide
Applied Systems Group of The Trane Company, La Crosse, Wis.
He serves on ASHRAE Standing Standard Project Committee 62.1
and is a member or corresponding member of ASHRAE Technical
Committee 1.4, Control Theory and Applications, TC 9.7, Testing
and Balancing, TC 5.2, Duct Design, and the IAQ ’98 Steering
Committee.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, Oct. 1998. © Copyright 1998 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning
Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permis-
sion of ASHRAE.
V
4 0
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
O c t o b e r 1 9 9 8
System Ventilation
Using the space-related information outlined earlier, calcu-
late the system-level parameters, including ventilation system
efficiency and worst-case or highest minimum total outdoor
airflow, (Figure 2).
Critical Ventilation Fraction (Z). The space with the high-
est ventilation fraction (z = Vo ÷ Eac ÷ Vs) becomes the ventila-
tion-critical space for the system and defines the critical venti-
lation fraction (Z = largest z). Labeling space ventilation rate
(Vo = Voc) and space supply airflow (Vs = Vsc) for the critical
space, and assuming Eac = 1.0, this definition of Z reduces to
the Standard 62-1989 definition (Z = Voc ÷Vsc). At design, (Vo =
Voc = DVR and Vs = Vsc = Vm), the critical space ventilation
fraction becomes Z = DVR ÷ Eac ÷ Vm.
Total Ventilation Airflow (Von). At any condition, Von =
ΣVo. At design conditions (P = Pd) total ventilation airflow,
corrected for population diversity, can be found (Von = PF ×
ΣDVR). Conservative designers assume population diversity
factor PF = 1.0 (as in Figure 2). More aggressive designers
estimate population diversity at design (see sidebar).
Standard 62-1989 tacitly assumes PF = 1.0, but allows a popu-
lation adjustment for individual intermittent-occupancy spaces,
based on an estimated average occupancy (P = Pd = Pa) and an
adjusted design ventilation rate (DVR = Pa × Rp) for those
spaces.
Total Primary Airflow (Vpt). At any condition, Vpt = ΣVp.
Total primary airflow at design is the sum of space primary airflow
values at design conditions (Vpt = DF × ΣVd) adjusted for load
diversity (see sidebar). Figure 2 uses an unusually low load
diversity factor (DF = 0.67) to simplify illustrated calculations.
Standard 62-89 defines total supply airflow (Vst) rather than
total primary airflow (Vpt). However, while identical in single-
duct VAV systems, supply airflow may be greater than primary
airflow in fan-powered or dual-duct VAV systems. Defining to-
tal primary airflow for design calculations leads to more general
results.
Average Ventilation Fraction (X). Average ventilation frac-
tion is simply total ventilation airflow divided by primary air-
flow at design (X = Von ÷ Vpt). ASHRAE 62-89 defines “uncor-
rected ventilation fraction” (X = Von ÷ Vst), which is identical to
average ventilation fraction for single-duct VAV systems, since
Vpt = Vst.
Ventilation System Efficiency (Ev). For single-duct VAV sys-
tems, Ev can be defined in terms of average and critical ventila-
tion fractions, i.e. Ev = 1 + X – Z. When the critical space venti-
lation fraction (Z) is significantly greater than the system aver-
age ventilation fraction (X), the return acquires significant dilu-
tion capacity from overventilated spaces. Much of this capac-
ity is lost in the exhaust air resulting in low ventilation system
efficiency.
Total Outdoor Airflow (Vot). Finally, use the multiple-space
equation (MSE) (see sidebar) to calculate the required worst-
case total outdoor airflow while properly accounting for venti-
lation system efficiency: Vot = Von ÷ Ev. Use worst-case total
outdoor airflow, Vot, to determine design cooling capacity.
Other VAV Systems. The equation for ventilation system
efficiency used in the simplistic illustration presented in Figure
Standard 62-1989 requires that designers of multiple-space
systems solve Equation 6-1 to determine the required ratio
of first-pass outdoor airflow to primary (supply) airflow,
i.e. Y = X ÷ (1 + X – Z). With that ratio, required total
outdoor airflow (Vot) can be calculated: Vot = Y × Vpt.
Equation 6-1 can also be expressed in the following
form (dubbed “MSE”). The divisor represents ventilation
system efficiency.
MSE: Vot = Von ÷ (1 + X – Z) = Von ÷ Ev
The equations and variables necessary to solve MSE
are listed below. Some variable names and definitions used
here differ a bit from those in Standard 62-1989 allowing
application to a broader range of ventilation systems.
General Equations
Ev
= 1 + X – Z
Vo
= P × Rp (or…Vo = A × Ra)
Von
= ΣVo
Vot
= Von ÷ Ev
Vpt
= ΣVp
Vs
= Vp (for single-duct VAV)
X
= Von ÷ Vpt
z
= Vo ÷ Eac ÷ Vs
Z
= largest z
Design Equations
DVR
= Pd × Rp (or, DVR = A × Ra)
Von
= PF × ΣDVR
Vpt
= DF × ΣVd
zm
= DVR ÷ Eac ÷ Vm
Z
= largest zm
PF
= ΣPb ÷ SPd
DF
= Qb ÷ ΣQd
Equation Variables
A
= space area (ft2)
DF
= load diversity factor
DVR
= space design ventilation rate (cfm)
Eac
= space air change effectiveness
Ev
= ventilation system efficiency
P
= space population (persons)
Pb
= system block population (persons)
Pd
= space design population (persons)
PF
= population diversity factor
Qb
= system block load (Btu/h)
Qd
= space design load (Btu/h)
Ra
= ventilation required for building (cfm/ft2)
Rp
= ventilation required for people (cfm/p)
Vd
= space primary airflow at design conditions (cfm)
Vm
= space minimum supply airflow (cfm)
Vo
= space ventilation rate (cfm)
Von
= total ventilation airflow (cfm)
Vot
= total outdoor airflow (cfm)
Vp
= space primary airflow (cfm)
Vpt
= total primary airflow (cfm)
Vs
= space supply airflow (cfm)
X
= average ventilation fraction
z
= space ventilation fraction
zm
= worst-case space ventilation fraction
Z
= critical space ventilation fraction
The Multiple-Space Equation

1 can be applied to any multiple-space system, but it does not
account for the local recirculation that occurs in fan-powered
VAV systems or in dual-fan, dual-duct VAV systems. Conse-
quently, it results in conservatively low ventilation system effi-
ciency for VAV systems that provide more than one ventilation
path to each space. Generalized equations for ventilation sys-
tem effectiveness, applicable to a wider range of multiple-space
systems, have been derived but are not discussed here.2, 3
In addition, real buildings often include a mixture of ventila-
tion systems. Total ventilation airflow (Vot) must be found sepa-
rately for each system in a building.
Challenge 2:
Operate with Required Ventilation at All Loads
The design procedure described earlier identifies worst-case
total outdoor airflow, but what happens to ventilation require-
ments during normal operation? Table 1, Column A illustrates a
VAV system at full load and at two part-load conditions.
Full Load
For illustration, assume that the critical space requires mini-
mum primary airflow for its thermal load, while the other spaces
require much more primary airflow and the system fan delivers
design primary airflow. This full-load condition corresponds to
the worst-case design scenario described above. Ventilation
system efficiency (Ev = 1 + 0.180 – 0.700 = 0.480) determines
total outdoor airflow (Vot = 1,800 ÷ 0.480 = 3,750 cfm [1770 L/s]).
70% Load
On a cool summer morning with a 70% cooling load, space
primary airflow values change, but assuming no change in space
population, space ventilation airflow values remain constant at
design (Vo = DVR). The critical ventilation fraction (Z = 0.500)
and the average ventilation fraction (X = 1800 ÷ 7000 = 0.257)
change as shown. The ventilation system efficiency increases
at this load condition (Ev = 1 + 0.257 – 0.500 = 0.757). Less
overventilation occurs in non-critical spaces so less unused
dilution capacity exhausts. The system requires less total out-
door airflow (Vot = 1,800 ÷ 0.757 = 2,380 cfm [1123 L/s]) even
though required space ventilation airflow (Vo = DVR) remains
unchanged.
35% Load
On an autumn day with a 35% cooling load, primary airflow
values change again. As was true for the 70% load condition,
the current ventilation system efficiency dictates the total out-
door airflow requirement. At this condition, critical space venti-
lation fraction (Z = 0.700) and current average ventilation frac-
tion (X = 0.514) result in increased ventilation system efficiency
(Ev = 1 + 0.514 – 0.700 = 0.814) with decreased total outdoor
airflow (Vot = 1,800 ÷ 0.814 = 2,210 cfm [1043 L/s]).
Fixed Outdoor Air Damper Position
Traditional VAV systems “set” minimum outdoor airflow us-
ing an outdoor air (OA) damper at a fixed minimum position;
actual outdoor air intake volume, of course, varies as total pri-
mary airflow varies. Can the ventilation volume requirement be
met with a fixed-position outdoor air damper? No. A fixed out-
door air damper position results in relatively constant outdoor
airflow percentage, but a variable outdoor airflow volume.
Table 1, Column B, illustrates the results of a common de-
sign mistake: fixing the OA damper position to deliver average
ventilation fraction (X = 0.18 or 18% outdoor air) at all operating
conditions. It results in underventilation at full load (1,800 cfm
[850 L/s] versus 3,750 cfm [1770 L/s]) and at part load (1,260 cfm
[595 L/s] versus 2,380 cfm [1123 L/s] and 630 cfm [297 L/s]
versus 2,210 cfm [1043 L/s]).
Some designers fix the OA damper position to deliver critical
ventilation fraction (Z = 0.70 or 70% outdoor air) at all loads.
This results in overventilation (7,000 cfm [3300 L/s] vs 3,750
cfm [1770 L/s]) both at design and at part load.
Fixed Outdoor Airflow
It takes the right volume, not percentage, of outdoor air to
meet the ventilation requirement in Standard 62-1989. Using
direct or indirect sensing, total outdoor airflow volume must be
maintained at or above the worst-case minimum total outdoor
airflow (Vot) at all load conditions by adjusting the outdoor air
damper position as primary airflow changes. Table 1, Column
C, shows the effect of maintaining outdoor airflow at the design
value (3,750 cfm [1770 L/s]), which is overventilation at all part-
load conditions.
Challenge 3:
Control to Minimize Ventilation Energy Impact
The design procedure discussed earlier yields a worst-case
total outdoor airflow. However, ventilation system efficiency in-
creases during normal operation since less unused dilution ca-
pacity exhausts at part load. Can the ventilation system be con-
trolled to assure proper ventilation without costly overventila-
tion? Yes, using a “ventilation reset” scheme as explained later.
Figure 1: Simple three-space, single-duct VAV system.
4 2
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
O c t o b e r 1 9 9 8
ASHRAE Requirements
Standard 62-1989 states that “… when mechanical ventila-
tion is used, provision for airflow measurement should be in-
cluded,” and that for VAV systems, “… provision shall be
made to maintain acceptable indoor air quality throughout
the occupied zone.” And, with regard to multiple-space system
ventilation, it states: “Where more than one space is served by
a common supply system … the system outdoor air quantity
shall then be determined using Equation 6-1.”
In other words, proper ventilation for multiple-space VAV
systems must be determined using the MSE, outdoor airflow
measurement and control. It follows that a control scheme that
senses both space primary airflow and total outdoor airflow
and solves the MSE dynamically to reset the total outdoor
airflow setpoint (Vot) will satisfy Standard 62’s ventilation re-
quirements. The term ventilation reset is used to describe this
control scheme.
Basic Ventilation Reset
Assuming space population and ventilation requirements
remain constant (Vo = DVR), decreased primary airflow leads to
higher ventilation system efficiency at part load and a decreased
need for first-pass outdoor airflow. A basic ventilation reset
control scheme senses actual primary airflow (Vp) and calcu-
lates the current ventilation fraction (z) for each space, finds
the current total outdoor airflow setpoint (i.e. finds Z, Von and
Vpt, then solves MSE to find Vot) for the system, and finally
modulates total outdoor airflow to maintain the new setpoint.
More sophisticated control schemes that account for changes
in population and recalculate ventilation requirements (Vo = P
× Rp) can also be used. For instance, systems with predictable
space population can use a simple time-of-day schedule to es-
timate population, recalculate Vo and reset total outdoor air-
flow (Vot) as described above.
Alternatively, actual space population may be determined,
either directly by “people-counting” or indirectly by sensing a
tracer gas (e.g. differential CO2). Actual population can then be
used to recalculate Vo and reset Vot, again, by solving MSE
dynamically.
Implementation. Basic ventilation reset is easily implemented
Although not specifically included in Standard 62-1989,
some designers account for “population diversity” when
designing ventilation systems. At any given time, the ac-
tual population may be design value (P = Pd) in one space
and less than design value in all other spaces. Since all
spaces are not at design population simultaneously, it fol-
lows that the design ventilation requirements for the sys-
tem could be based on the highest population in any given
time block rather than sum-of-peak population (ΣPd).
To account for system population diversity some de-
signers estimate actual space population for each hour,
then find a population diversity factor—the ratio of the
highest population in any hourly block to the sum-of-
peak population (PF = ΣPb ÷ ΣPd) and use it to reduce
total ventilation airflow accordingly (Von = PF × ΣDVR).
Conservatively assuming that actual population always
equals design population (P = Pd and PF = 1.0) results
in overventilation at all loads. On the other hand, aggres-
sively estimating “block” population (Pb << Pd and PF
<< 1.0) can result in significant underventilation at all
loads.
Most VAV system designers account for “load diver-
sity” when finding the primary airflow. At any given time,
actual cooling load may equal design value (Q = Qd) in
some spaces and less than design value in other spaces.
It follows that total primary airflow for the VAV system at
design conditions can be based on the highest space
cooling load in a given time block rather than sum-of-
peak space cooling loads.
To account for system load diversity, hour-by-hour
space load can be used to find the highest system cool-
ing load (Qb) during any hourly time block. Then, load
diversity factor can be found as the ratio of the highest
load in any time block to the sum-of-peak load at design
conditions (DF = Qb ÷ ΣQd) and used to reduce total
ventilation airflow accordingly (Vpt = DF × ΣVd).
Population and Load Diversity

Ev = 1+ X – Z = 1+ 0.18 – 0.70 = 0.48
Vot = Von ÷ (1+X – Z) = Von ÷ Ev
= 1,800 ÷ 0.48 = 3,750 cfm
Vpt = DF × ΣVd = 0.67 × ΣVd = 10,000 cfm
Von = PF × ΣDVR = 1.0 × ΣDVR = 1,800 cfm
X = Von ÷ Vpt = 1,800 ÷ 10,000 = 0.18
Z = largest zm = 0.70

with sufficient capacity, operated to ventilate properly, and
controlled to avoid significant operating cost increases. Are
there any other concerns related to VAV system ventilation?
Absolutely.
Freeze Protection and Mixing
Proper VAV system ventilation leads to a high percentage of
outdoor air at part load. During cold weather, mixed air tempera-
ture can approach outdoor air. The mixed airstream can stratify
and water in coils can freeze.
Water in coils can be protected from freezing in many ways.
For example, drain the coils, keep the water in motion, add gly-
col to the water, use face-and-bypass dampers to limit cold
airflow or preheat the outdoor air.
Streams of air at very different temperatures are difficult to mix.
Air blending devices offer effective mixing but usually require
more space (longer air handlers) and more fan horsepower. As an
alternative, preheating the outdoor air before the mixing box de-
creases the airstream temperature differential, allowing good mix-
ing without blending and offering freeze protection as well.
Airflow Sensing
Outdoor airflow must be determined or sensed for proper
VAV system ventilation. Many indirect airflow-sensing schemes
(e.g. fan tracking, differential temperature sensing4) are unable
to meet the 10% accuracy criterion typical for air balancing
measurements. Although direct-sensing methods using inde-
pendent airflow-monitoring stations can be designed to meet
accuracy needs, they tend to be costly (requiring field installa-
tion and field calibration) and difficult to apply (straight duct-
length requirements). Injection fan schemes with airflow sens-
ing and fan speed control can be used successfully although
using the data-rich environment afforded by communicating
controls throughout the VAV system. Specifically, use pres-
sure-independent VAV terminal units with direct digital con-
trols (DDC/VAV), a building automation system (BAS) with
simple equation-solving capability, an air handler with DDC
controls and a means for measuring and maintaining outdoor
airflow. Figure 3 illustrates this implementation.
In operation, individual DDC/VAV controllers sense primary
airflow (Vp) in the spaces they serve and calculate current
space ventilation fraction (z = Vo ÷ Eac ÷ Vp) using values
entered or calculated for Vo and Eac. The BAS continuously
polls each DDC/ VAV controller, collecting current primary air-
flow Vp, required space ventilation airflow Vo and current ven-
tilation fraction z from each. It then calculates total primary
airflow (Vpt = ∑Vp), total design ventilation airflow (Von =
∑Vo), and average ventilation fraction (X = Von ÷ Vpt), deter-
mines critical space ventilation fraction (Z = largest z), and uses
these values to find ventilation system efficiency (Ev = 1 + X –
Z) and a new outdoor airflow setpoint (Vot = Von ÷ Ev). The air
handler DDC controller receives the new setpoint and modu-
lates outdoor airflow as required to maintain it.
What’s It Worth? Basic ventilation reset control reduces
the outdoor airflow at part load and saves operating costs. Can
it be justified? That depends on building type and location.
The outdoor air load in an office building in Miami, for instance,
may be reduced by 8% while the same building in Toronto or
Minneapolis may realize a 30% reduction. An economic analy-
sis program can be used to quantify the savings associated
with ventilation reset.
Other Challenges?
As shown earlier, VAV ventilation systems can be designed
Table 1: Part load operation.

4 7
V E N T I L A T I O N
they, too, may be costly.
Fixed orifice differential-pressure
(plenum-pressure) schemes can be
used when the mixing box geometry
allows accurate and stable static pres-
sure sensing. Factory-installed direct-
sensing methods, specifically de-
signed for the outdoor airstream usu-
ally offer the best solution in terms of
both installed cost and flow-sensing
accuracy.
For the past several years, factory-
installed and factory-calibrated airflow
sensors that use differential pressure
measurements to accurately sense out-
door airflow, even at very low velocities,
have been available. These sensors,
based on proven VAV box flow-sensing
technology, typically require very little
straight duct, add little to fan static pres-
sure requirements, and sense accurately over a wide range of
temperatures. Innovative pressure signal processing allows very
accurate flow measurement (within 10% of actual flow) over the
full range of outdoor intake airflow, from 100% (when econo-
mizing) to as little as 5% of full flow.
Building Pressure
Maintaining a slightly positive pressure inside buildings—
especially in humid climates —increases comfort and helps to
avoid IAQ problems related to microbial growth and outdoor
contaminant sources. Preserving constant outdoor air volume
helps assure uniform building pressure, regardless of primary
airflow. However, resetting the outdoor air volume downward
at part load (as described earlier) may lead to negative building
pressure. Building pressure control, usually best implemented
using modulating relief airflow in VAV systems, becomes vital
when ventilation reset schemes are used to reset outdoor air-
flow volume.
Subcooling at Part Load
Since a VAV system provides low dew point primary air when
cooling, space relative humidity is adequately maintained. How-
ever, in a lightly loaded space, the VAV box closes to minimum
airflow when space-cooling load is lower than VAV-box cool-
ing capacity. The space subcools and relative humidity rises.
To avoid subcooling, primary air must be tempered to match
cooling capacity with cooling load. In other words, tempering
(terminal reheat) must be available, usually throughout the
year, not just during cold weather.
Some designers reset supply air temperature upward at part
load in an effort to avoid subcooling. In many climates, supply
air temperature reset schemes should include return air humid-
ity or dew point limits. Remember, when supply air dry bulb
temperature rises, supply air dew point also rises. Without
limiting the temperature reset based on return air moisture con-
tent, reduced system latent capacity may lead to unacceptable
space relative humidity.
Summary
• Single-duct VAV systems must be designed using the mul-
tiple-space equation to meet Standard 62-1989 ventilation re-
quirements.
• As primary airflow varies during normal operation, total
outdoor air intake volume must be maintained as appropriate
for the occupancy load. The percentage of outdoor air in the
primary airstream must change—no more fixed dampers!
• Well thought out control schemes that allow outdoor air-
flow reset can minimize the operating-cost impact of proper
ventilation at part load.
References
1. ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-1989, Ventilation for Acceptable In-
door Air Quality. p. 11.
2. Warden, D. 1995. Outdoor air—calculation and delivery. ASHRAE
Journal (37)5:54–63.
3. Ke, Y.P., and S.A. Mumma. 1996. “A generalized multiple-spaces
equation to accommodate any mix of close-off and fan-powered VAV
boxes.” ASHRAE Transactions 102 (1): 183–192.
4. Kettler, J.P. 1998. “Controlling minimum ventilation volume in
VAV systems.” ASHRAE Journal. (40)5:45–50.

ASHRAE JOURNAL
ne definition of noise is “a sound that is loud, un-
pleasant, unexpected or undesired.”1 Put the word
“electrical” in front of noise and it is a good de-
scription of what can happen when electrical cir-
cuits leave the design lab and are used in the real world. This is
especially true with adjustable speed drives (ASDs). In particu-
lar, the most popular drive today, the pulse width modulated
inverter, is one of the main culprits for noise problems in motor
drive applications.
Many technical papers about prob-
lems with ASDs discuss using filters and
formulas to solve these problems. The
purpose of this article is to demonstrate
a method to quickly identify the electri-
cal noise in an ASD installation and offer
possible solutions to some of the most
common problems.
Most large, reputable motor drive
manufacturers have the filters, line reac-
tors, isolation transformers and other de-
vices that may be required to mitigate
noise problems if the problem can be
identifed. Using a high quality test in-
strument, such as a good oscilloscope,
is the key to tracking down these prob-
lems. If the noise cannot be character-
ized visually, then solving the problem is simply guesswork.
Reflected Wave Voltages
The most common ASD problem today is reflected wave
voltages that create two to three times per unit overvoltages at
the motor terminals with pulse-width modulated (PWM) drives.
A full explanation of this problem goes beyond the scope of
this article. However, it can be stated simply that the overvolt-
ages at the motor terminals become greater as the rise time of
the pulses becomes shorter and the cable length between the
PWM drive and the motor gets longer, due to an impedance
mismatch created by the long cable. Figure 2 shows the rela-
tionship between rise time, cable length and the impact it has
on the magnitude of the reflected voltages.2 Figure 3 shows a
detailed view of the pulses and the “ringing” overvoltages that
are present.
To discover whether an installation has this problem, mea-
sure the phase-phase voltage at the motor terminals. Be careful
though, peak voltages two to three times that of the DC bus
may be present and not all instrument manufacturers have
scopes and/or probes that can safely handle these overvolt-
ages. Check the IEC-1010 safety rating of the scope being used
to be sure it is rated for the application (CAT III - 600V or
better), and be sure to use the probes with the highest safety
ratings available for this measurement.
Most test equipment manufacturers do not design their
probes to make a continuous overvoltage measurement, which
can exceed 2,000V peaks. When using a line-powered bench
scope, CAT III differential/isolation probes are the only way to
make safe measurements. Do not use standard scope probes,
as they are not designed to withstand the amount of energy
present should the overvoltages cause
the probes to fail.
Figure 4 shows the pulse-modulated
envelope present at the motor terminals
with 100 ft (30 m) of cable between the
drive output and the motor. Contrast this
waveform with the modulation envelope
in Figure 5 that has only 6 ft (2 m) of
cable between the drive output and the
motor. Notice there is a peak voltage dif-
ference of about 200V. In addition, no-
tice there is only about 5V RMS differ-
ence between the two waveforms. This
means that a voltmeter will not reveal this
problem, because a 5V difference prob-
ably will not be noticed.
The real danger of this overvoltage
condition is the damage it can do to the
motor windings over time, because the problem may not show
up when the PWM drive is first installed. Many PWM drives
are installed without considering the overvoltage effects of
long cabling between the PWM output and the motor. And
while improved efficiency of the newest and latest PWM drives
is achieved by making the rise times faster on the output pulses,
this can make the overvoltage problem even worse, and the
need for shorter cabling even greater.
If an overvoltage problem is detected before the motor has
failed, and the cabling cannot be shortened, then there are some
“fixes” that can be done to minimize the effects. However, if a
motor has already failed and must be rebuilt, then it should be
rebuilt with specially insulated wire. A new brand of insulated
wire on the market provides significantly more protection against
overvoltages without adding insulation thickness and the same

In Adjustable Speed Drives

at Fluke Corporation in Everett, Wash.

popular drive today,
the pulse width
modulated inverter, is
one of the main
culprits for noise
problems in motor
drive applications.

stator can be used without modification. If the motor has been
damaged beyond repair, a motor designed to meet NEMA MG-
1-1993, Part One specifications (sustained Vpeak ≤1600 volts and
rise time ³0.1 µs) should be used as a replacement motor for
PWM applications where sustained overvoltages may be oc-
curring.
If the cabling in the PWM application cannot be shortened,
one of three filtering techniques as shown in Figure 6 can be
used to solve the problem:
1. An external “add-on” low-pass filter can be installed be-
tween the PWM output terminals and the cable to the motor, as
a way to slow the rise time.
2. Another approach is to install an R-C impedance match-
ing filter at the motor terminals to minimize the overvoltages or
ringing effect.
3. In some applications, such as submersible pumps or drill-
ing machines, it is not possible to access the motor terminals,
and other methods of minimizing overvoltages are required.
One method is to apply series reactors between the PWM out-
put terminals and the cable to the motor. While this is a simple
solution, the line reactors may be big, bulky and expensive for
large horsepower applications.
It is interesting to note that newer drives have faster rise times
to improve the efficiency of the drive, which increases the en-

The dashed line superimposed on the square wave pulses
in Figure 1 represents a plot of the RMS voltage of the
varying widths of the pulses. The greater the amount of time
that the pulse is high, the greater the RMS voltage. This
means the RMS voltage applied to the motor can be varied
by adjusting the width of the pulses. The frequency of the
pulses is known as the carrier frequency.
The motor current waveform shows that the motor is only
responding to the low frequency component, or period of
one set of positive and negative pulses. Therefore, varying
the period of one cycle of pulses, also known as the modu-
lation frequency, will vary the frequency at which the motor
responds.
How PWM Drives Work
To summarize, the RMS voltage applied to the motor can
be altered by varying the width of the pulses, while the
frequency of the RMS voltage as seen by the motor is
changed by varying the modulation frequency. In this way,
the V/Hz ratio can be held constant while varying the speed
and voltage together for constant torque applications, or
the V/Hz can be varied at different rates for variable torque
applications.
Figure 1: Simplified dia-
gram of a pulse width
modulated inverter with
output voltage and cur-
rent waveforms.

N O I S E
ergy savings. However, much of the en-
ergy savings will be consumed by the ne-
cessity of installing filters because of the
faster rise times. If an application requires
longer cabling runs, upgrading to a new
energy efficient PWM drive may not have
the expected payback if the decision is
based solely on energy efficiency savings.
Bearing Currents
When motor shaft voltages exceed the
insulating capability of the bearing grease,
flash-over currents to the outer bearing
will occur, thereby causing pitting and
grooving to the bearing races. The first
signs of this problem will be noise and
overheating as the bearings begin to lose
their original shape and metal fragments
mix with the grease and increase bearing
friction. This can lead to bearing destruc-
tion within a few months of ASD opera-
tion and can cause costly motor repair
and downtime.
One integrated circuit manufacturer
learned this lesson the hard way and had
to replace all of their PWM driven motors
within six months of installation.
There is a normal, unavoidable shaft
voltage created from the stator winding
to the rotor shaft due to small dissymme-
tries of the magnetic field in the air gap
that are inherent in the design of the mo-
tor. Most induction motors are designed
to have a maximum shaft voltage to frame
ground of <1 Vrms.
Under 60 Hz sinewave operation, the
bearing breakdown voltage is approxi-
mately 0.4–0.7 volts. However, with the
fast edges of the transient voltages found
with PWM drives, the breakdown of the
insulating capacity of the grease actually
occurs at a higher voltage—about 8 to 15
volts. This higher breakdown voltage cre-
ates higher bearing flashover currents,
which increases damage to the bearings
in a shorter amount of time.
Research in this area has shown that
shaft voltages below 0.3 volts are safe
and not high enough to cause destruc-
tive bearing currents. However, voltages
from 0.5 to 1.0 volts may cause harmful
bearing currents (>3A) and shaft voltages
>2 volts may destroy the bearing.
As the shaft voltages are caused by
fast rise times of the PWM drive pulses,
the voltages will appear as inconsistent
peaks and should be measured using an
oscilloscope and not a DMM. Even if the
DMM has peak detect, enough variation
between peaks exists to make the reading
unreliable. Another measurement tip is to
make the shaft to frame ground voltage
measurement after the motor has warmed
to its normal operating temperature as
shaft voltages may not even be present

on the modulated pulse.

waves.

filtering techniques that

when the motor is cold.
Suggested solutions to this problem
include: using shaft grounding devices,
insulating both bearings, using a faraday
shield in the motor, using conductive
grease, and installing ceramic bearings or
filtering between the ASD and the motor.
Additionally, when using a high carrier
frequency, reduce the carrier frequency
to 4 KHz or less to minimize the shaft
voltages.
While this article discusses only some
of the noise problems that can be encoun-
tered with ASDs, it is always helpful to
characterize the electrical environment
with high quality test instruments. The
problem cannot be solved unless what is
happening electrically can be visualized.
Secondly, resist the temptation to add fil-
ters and protective devices unless it is
known for certain how it will impact the
overall ASD installation. And finally, ask
for help from the ASD manufacturer or
qualified consultant if necessary.

ASHRAE Journal

ASHRAE JOURNAL
SHRAE Research Project 863, Operating Experiences
with Commercial Ground-Source Heat Pumps1 re-
ported on a number of systems with widely diverse
installation costs and designs. Some installations
had high first costs with extended economic paybacks and some
were lower than conventional systems. However, most installa-
tions were between the extremes. A few designs were simple,
while others were more complex and redundant.
I have often thought my distaste for complex HVAC systems
was rooted in my academic background and in the “real world,”
complicated systems are necessary. However, I have recently
read and heard
similar opinions
from respected
ASHRAE mem-
bers who have
made a living by
designing rather
than by teaching
or marketing. One
referred to the de-
sign philosophy
called “the Hydra
phenomenon...
where simplicity
has been replaced
with complexity….”2 Another attempt to identify the reasons
for high construction costs used the term “gadgets” when al-
luding to added devices in HVAC systems that may not always
be needed.3
Ground source heat pumps (GSHPs) are inherently simple,
reliable, and efficient systems. While they are not inexpensive,
their first cost can be driven through the roof by overdesign,
added complexity, and “gadgets.” With this added complexity
and cost comes reduced reliability, increased maintenance and
in some cases higher operating costs.
The simplicity of GSHPs results from the fact that excellent
comfort can be delivered by using constant air volume and by
using unitary heat pumps that can operate efficiently without
precise water flow control. Closed loop systems can operate
effectively with water flow in the 2 to 3 gpm/ton (0.03 to 0.05
mL/J) range, while open loop system flows can be much lower.
Simple room thermostats almost always give optimal occupant
comfort and satisfaction without compromising energy effi-
ciency. Service personnel with moderate experience and skill
levels can perform troubleshooting on equipment and controls.
Proprietary controls that will not communicate with other equip-
ment are available only from a limited number of water-to-air
heat pump manufacturers.
This article lists suggestions for a subset of GSHPs referred
to as ground-coupled heat pump (GCHP) systems.4 The cost of
a GCHP system in a small office building is estimated for both a
simple and complex design. This article is not intended to sub-
stitute for more comprehensive GSHP publications, design tools
and design training that are currently available.
Design Suggestions
1. A geotechnical survey of the site should be conducted
before the ground loop is designed. Sampling at shallow depths
(10 to 40 ft [3 to 12 m]) should include sieve analysis and mois-
ture content, which is typically performed with a hollow stem
auger. This should
be accompanied
by deeper bores
that identify the
soil type (clay,
sand, gravel, rock
type, etc.), static
ground
water
level, drilling diffi-
culty and difficulty
of inserting verti-
cal U-tube heat ex-
changer. Methods
are being devel-
oped to bypass
much of this activity by inserting a U-tube, imposing a thermal
load in the field and determining thermal properties with in-
verse methods.
2. Thermally fused high-density polyethylene (HDPE) should
be used for all in-ground piping. Specifications are available.5
This pipe is rugged, reliable, and relatively inexpensive. Spe-
cialty fittings for GCHP applications are increasingly available.
It is recommended for use inside the building and some codes
specifically permit its use.6 Although the required number of
pipe hangers are increased, it is generally less expensive than
metal piping and the need for corrosion inhibitors in the water
loop can be eliminated.
About the Author
Ground Source Heat Pumps
By Steve Kavanaugh, Ph.D.
Member ASHRAE
Simple, Efficient, Reliable
Steve Kavanaugh, Ph.D., is a professor of mechanical engi-
neering at the University of Alabama in Tuscaloosa. He is past
chair of ASHRAE Technical Committee 6.8, Geothermal En-
ergy. He holds a doctorate in mechanical engineering from Okla-
homa State University.

3. Experienced ground loop
contractors should be consulted.
Good contractors have methods
of installing U-tubes and piping
in-ground headers that are reli-
able, effective, and can be easily
purged of air, dirt and debris. Nov-
ice GCHP designers may not ap-
preciate the value of pre-
assembled headers until they re-
alize the difficulty of assembling a
large field-fabricated reverse-re-
turn header in a deep, muddy
trench.
4. Avoid using a large amount
of low thermal conductivity grout.
Designers often specify grouting
the entire bore, although grouting the upper portion of the bore
provides more than adequate ground water protection. Low
conductivity grouts add to the cost of systems in two ways.
They are expensive and reduce the effectiveness of the loops
so that more length is needed. Thermally enhanced grouts are
available and should be considered when the entire bore must
be grouted. Borehole diameters should be minimized to reduce
grout cost and thermal penalty.
5. Vertical bores must be sufficiently separated to minimize
long-term heat storage effects that must be offset with longer
loops. Although design programs should allow the engineers
to consider the impact of bore separation distance, the mini-
mum recommended is 20 ft (6 m) for typical commercial build-
ings. This distance may need to be 25 ft (7.6 m) for buildings
that have no heating requirements and very high equivalent
full load cooling hours. However, the minimum distance could
be as little as 15 ft (4.6 m) if the full load heating hours are
significantly greater than the cooling hours or if there is a high
rate of ground water movement at the site.
6. High efficiency, single stage heat pumps with a minimum
ARI-330 cooling energy efficiency ratio (EER) of 13.5 Btu/w-h
(COP = 4.0) should be specified. The economy of scale nor-
mally associated with larger units is not realized with GCHP
systems. Water-to-air heat pumps larger than 6 tons (21 kW)
typically have two compressors with individual refrigeration
circuits. Efficiencies of larger units are typically much lower
than smaller units. When the added cost of central ductwork
and equipment room space for larger units is included, minimal
savings are realized compared to two smaller units.9
The designer is cautioned against using two-speed units in
cooling mode-dominated applications. While they may appear
to have a high rated EER, closer examination normally reveals
that the high rating was obtained at low compressor speed and
high fan speed (with no latent cooling capacity). The primary
figure of merit for commercial building applications is normally
the high speed cooling EER (with an acceptable latent capacity).
