G R O U N D S O U R C E H E A T P U M P S
7. The use of highly sophisticated and/or propri-
etary controls should be avoided—especially in ap-
plications where building owners and occupants do
not understand how to operate the system. Comfort
and efficiency can be obtained without central DDC
systems. Programmable single-stage thermostats are
appropriate for almost all applications (auxiliary heat
is normally unnecessary for commercial buildings
with typical internal heat gains). They are afford-
able, can be linked with almost all types of equip-
ment, and allow equipment service without electronic
interfaces.
8. Excessive pump energy use should be avoided.
A well-designed system should only require 5 to 7.5
pump horsepower per 100 tons of cooling (11 to 16
pump watts per kW cooling capacity). These guide-
lines can be met by circulating 2.5 to 3 gpm per ton of
peak block load (0.4 to 0.5 mL/J) and limiting system
head to 50 to 75 ft of water (150 to 224 kPa).
9. Simple pump control should be incorporated. A
single variable speed pump (with a back-up) that has
a differential pressure sensor across the building sup-
ply and return headers at a location near the end of
the piping runs is the most sophisticated method necessary.
Diverting flow around the ground loop pays little dividend since
a balanced heat load on the water loop rarely occurs. When
balance does occur, the part load is usually small, loop flow is
low and the energy savings realized by avoiding the head loss
in the ground loop is minimal. Constant speed circulator pumps
can be used as an alternative when interior central piping is
expensive and multiple ground loops make more economic
sense. This situation occurs in many buildings with minimal
load diversity and non-compact floor plans.
10. Avoid using excessive amounts of antifreeze solutions.
Design procedures should generate minimum loop tempera-
tures. Many larger ground loops require little or no antifreeze
since commercial buildings typically have greater cooling loads
than heating loads and water-to-air heat pumps reject almost
twice the amount of heat they absorb.
11. The costs of interior piping for central systems should be
examined. In some applications, they are very high. System
costs can be lowered if several “sub-central” loops are used
rather than one central loop. While some diversity may be lost
and ground loop requirements increased, total system cost may
be lower because of the savings in interior piping. This situa-
tion occurs in many buildings with minimal load diversity and
non-compact floor plans (i.e. elementary schools) and in retro-
fits where interior piping may be expensive.
Typical ground loop cost for a variety of conditions are listed
in the next section. These costs are used to compare total GCHP
system cost with those of conventional systems. Examples of
alternatives are presented that both add to and reduce GCHP
costs while maintaining the quality and performance of the
ground loop.
Ground Loop Cost
A survey was mailed to 20 ground loop contractors with
large system experience in April 1996. Five surveys were re-
turned and Table 1 was developed based on the results. Two
additional projects provide further information regarding loop
cost.1, 7
Cost of a Simple GCHP for A Small Office Building
The cost of three different HVAC options for the 13,000 ft2
(1200 m2) office building shown in Figure 1 will be compared.
Multiple single-zone unitary equipment generally is the lowest
cost alternative. The cost of serving each of the seven zones is
found for this equipment from Means Mechanical Cost Data.9
The cost of a multizone rooftop can be easily estimated from the
same source. However, the cost of the GCHP option is more
difficult to compute since many of the sub-systems, materials
and labor activities are not reported in Means Mechanical Cost
Data. 9
The details of GCHP design cannot be covered in an article
of this length. However, one aspect of GCHP design is the
necessity to budget the amount of heat transfer to and from the
ground to determine both short and long-term impact of heat
storage in the ground. Table 2 details the type of information
that may be necessary in order to account for heat storage
effects.
The information in Table 2 can be entered into a GCHP de-
sign program with information regarding soil properties, U-tube
data, and heat pump performance. Information regarding re-
quired bore lengths, liquid operating temperatures, equipment
capacity and efficiency are calculated. The required bore length
of 7,790 ft (260 m) is the result for the example building for the
conditions specified. Table 3 is an example of an output for a
GCHP design program. Shorter or longer bores are certainly
possible for other soil conditions and heat pump operating
parameters.
The basis for computing the resulting GCHP system cost is

1. The ground loop is sized based on equipment capacity
(40 tons) rather than diversified block load (28.5 tons).
Some engineers have designed loops based on rules of
thumb rather than careful consideration of building loads.
$ = Cost40 ton – Cost28.5 ton = (40 ÷ 28.5) × $41,000 – $41,000 =
$16,540
($1.27/ft2)
2. High solids (low thermal conductivity) bentonite grout
is specified for entire bore without regard to actual con-
tamination potential. Note that Table 1 is based on grout
at the top of the bore, which is used to prevent migration
of surface water (or polluted ground water) into fresh wa-
ter aquifers.
Add 1,950 ft to bore to maintain loop performance

Grout additional 9,130 ft of bore
(560 bags at $9.00)

$15,330
($1.18/ft2)

are specified
$15,000 to 26,000
($1.15/ft2 to $2.00/ft2)
4. Larger (two-stage) heat pumps are specified for larger
zones (1, 2, 5, 6 in example). Often, they are vertical units
located in the equipment room, and return ductwork is
required.
$ 8,650

(150 ft2 @ $70/ft2)

Some Reasons
For High GCHP Costs
from Means Mechanical Cost Data. Ductwork, pump, valve,
insulation, common HVAC fitting and control costs are calcu-
lated directly. The cost of the ground loop is found using aver-
age values in Table 1. Interior piping is HDPE and current aver-
age pipe and fittings cost used are from two major vendors.
Labor costs, overhead and profit were computed from the cost
of installing thermally fused polybutylene pipe and fittings,
which can be found from Means Mechanical Cost Data. Fi-
nally, the cost of ground source heat pump units was found by
adding $100 per ton to the material cost of standard water-to-air
heat pumps.
Option 1: Single Zone Rooftops\* (7 units, 40 tons total)
Electric cooling, gas heating, insulated low velocity galva-
nized steel duct, supply and return, standard controls, and curbs.
Costs include all materials, labor, overhead and profit. (No out-
door air control or heat recovery units included.)
(3) 8-ton units at $16,700 each

total on central loop (No outdoor air control or heat recovery
units included and zones 1, 2, 5, and 6 are served by two units
rather than one large unit.)
Ground Loop - 7,790 ft bore, 1 in. U-tubes, air
rotary, no surface casing, surface grout (20 ft),
(3) 2 in. headers, 2 fps ground loop purge

Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
3 6
ASHRAE Journal
October 1 9 9 8
The cost of the simple GCHP system is actually lower than
one of the conventional options. This situation, although not
common, has been reported.1 The opposite is more likely, but it
does not have to be that way. Reasons for higher GCHP cost
abound. The sidebar lists some of the common possibilities.
Conclusion
GSHPs (which includes ground water heat pumps) can be a
viable economic alternative when applied properly. Complexity,
gadgets and overdesign can make overcoming a high first cost
difficult. The costs given in the example are especially difficult
to attain by novice designers in areas without a GCHP infra-
structure. This includes not only engineers, but also contrac-
tors, equipment suppliers and building owners. However, meth-
ods are available to overcome these barriers if individuals are
committed to building efficient, reliable and economic GSHP-
HVAC systems.
References
1. ASHRAE Research Project 863. 1995. “Operating experiences with
commercial ground-source heat pumps.” Caneta Research.
2. Coad, W. J. 1996. “Indoor air quality: a design parameter.” ASHRAE
Journal 38(6):39–47.
3. Avery, G. 1996. “A new look at designing systems.” ASHRAE

ASHRAE Journal
October 1998
ASHRAE JOURNAL
ntroducing fresh air into air-
conditioned buildings in hot and
humid climates requires careful
analysis. Bringing in fresh air can
constitute a substantial portion of the total
energy consumed by the building. For ex-
ample, if this makeup air is improperly
treated and controlled, it can cause el-
evated humidity levels.
A primary/secondary system design
wherein fresh air is introduced and treated
via a dedicated fresh air-handling unit
(AHU) and then delivered to a second-
ary system offers system flexibility and
improved humidity control. This type of
system can also help save energy. In this
article, the application of runaround coils
configured in this type of a system is de-
scribed. This arrangement can save en-
ergy while improving humidity control.
The dominant application of runaround
coils is to exchange heat between fresh air
and exhaust air. In hot and humid climates
more than 50% of the load is latent. There-
fore, cooling coils have to over-cool the air
to achieve a sufficiently low sensible heat
factor. This usually forces the use of re-
heat to avoid high indoor humidity levels.
For many of the buildings for which analy-
ses were conducted, the primary cause of
high indoor relative humidity was
oversizing. A secondary, but still important
factor, was improper treatment of introduced
outside air.
Figure 1 illustrates an application of
the runaround coil, wherein one of the
coils is placed before the cooling coil and
the second after the cooling coil. The re-
covery of energy from the hot outdoor
air is used in reheating the overcooled
air, which achieves dual energy savings.
This scheme simultaneously reduces the
required cooling and reheat energy as
shown in Figure 2.
Runaround Coil Energy Savings
A simplified analysis is used to illus-
trate the possible energy savings. The
analysis is based on the following as-
sumptions:
• Constant volume of AHU operating
at 10,000 cfm (4719 L/s).
• Supply fresh air properties at stan-
dard conditions (70°F [21°C] and 50% RH
are used).
An equation is used to express the
runaround coil’s capacity Q at various
conditions for the previously specified
AHU:
Q = F (Tal – Twi)
(1)
Where Q is in Btu/h, F is the coil pro-
Fresh Air Treatment
In Hot and Humid Climates
I
About the Author
George J. Berbari is the manager of the
design department of Tabreed, a district
cooling service provider in the United
Arab Emirates.
By George J. Berbari
Member ASHRAE
Saving Energy
Using Runaround Recovery Coils
Figure 1: A schematic of the fresh air AHU with runaround recovery coils.
portionality factor, Tal is the temperature
of the air entering the coil, and Twi is the
temperature of the water entering the coil.
A manufacturer product selection pro-
gram was used to evaluate the coils at
three different ambient conditions:
DB/WB temp. = 115/85°F (46/29°C)
DB/WB temp. = 95/80°F (35/27°C)
DB/WB temp. = 75/70°F (24/21°C)
A polynomial was then utilized to in-
terpolate the equation variables F and Twi.
The basic solution is to equalize the log
mean temperature differential (LMTD) of
the two runaround coils:
1- Pre-cooling Runaround Coil
3- Re-heat Runaround Coil
2- Cooling Coil
4- Supplementary Heating Coil (Optional)

H U M I D I T Y
Twi = (Tal + Ta3) / 2 + 1.5303 × Tal – 0.029625 × Tal + 0.0000625
× Tal 2 F = 7277 + 52.78 × ( Tal – Twi) × ( Tal – Twi)2
(2)
Where Ta3 is the leaving air condition of the cooling coil
controlled at 56°F (13°C).
A simplified control system is assumed. The main cooling
coil should dehumidify the air to a level of 66.5 grains (.0095 Kg/
Kg of dry air), which corresponds to that of the comfort condi-
tions of 76°F (24°C) and 50% RH. This can be attained via a dew
point temperature controller or a simpler DB temperature con-
troller set at 56°F (13°C).
In this system the runaround coil is operated all summer with
an on-off control. A diverting valve can be used to control the
leaving air temperature of the second downstream runaround
coil, which should not exceed 70°F (21°C). An optional supple-
mentary reheat coil, if used, can control the final leaving air
temperature which should not drop below 70°F (21°C).
Two hot and humid climates were chosen for the analysis:
Augusta, Ga. in the United States and Abu Dhabi in the United
Arab Emirates. The Bin Hour method was used to estimate the
annual energy required.
Heat pipes are widely used now in place of runaround water
coils. Heat pipes are slightly more effective as they do not
require circulating pumps. They use refrigerant phase change
as a heat transfer media between hotter and colder deck. Run-
around coils use recirculating water for the same purpose.
Heat pipe manufacturer’s software was used to evaluate the
heat pipe’s performance using the same weather data for Au-
gusta, Ga. The heat transfer results and annual recovered en-
ergy for heat pipes were in close accordance with those of
runaround coils.
Benefits of Runaround Coils
The runaround coils reduced the cooling coils total annual
cooling energy by 19% for Augusta (from 2056.4 × 106 Btu to
1666.8 × 106 Btu [2170 GJ to 1758 GJ]) and by 21% for Abu
Dhabi (from 4137 × 106 Btu to 3280 × 106 Btu [4364 GJ to 3461
GJ]). The supplementary reheat coil total annual energy was
reduced by 49% for Augusta (from 786 × 106 Btu to 398.1 × 106
Btu [829 GJ to 420 GJ]) and by 62% for Abu Dhabi (from 1303.9
× 106 Btu to 495.7 × 106 Btu [1375.6 GJ to 523 GJ]). The Augusta
results are displayed in Tables 1 and 2.
A three-way diverting valve and a third recovery coil can be
used for winter heat recovery between exhaust air and fresh air.
Because water piping is much easier to install than air ducts,
exhaust and fresh air duct distance is not an issue. Supplemen-
tary reheat is generally not required if indoor relative humidity
is in the range of 60% to 65% at off-peak conditions.
At relatively low ambient conditions (below 70°F or 21°C)
the coils can be switched off and the system will operate in the
economizer cycle mode, further reducing the cooling energy
and eliminating supplementary reheat. In milder climates, the
system can be used with reduced effectiveness. In this applica-
tion, a lower supply air duct relative humidity is achieved, thus
reducing the possibility of mold or mildew build-up inside the
duct.
The system has proven its reliability with hundreds of in-
stallations in the UAE. As an example, one 5200 cfm unit (with-
out supplementary reheat) was delivering a leaving air tempera-
ture of DB/WB = 71.5/64°F (21.9/17.8°C) when ambient tem-
perature was 93°F (34°C) and 46% RH. After the runaround coil
circulating pump was switched off and the system was left to
stabilize, the leaving air condition was determined to be 60.5/
60°F (15.8/15.6°C).

Table 2: Energy analysis of a 10,000 cfm fresh air AHU summer cooling requirement in Augusta, Ga.

ASHRAE Journal
October 1 9 9 8
Cost Effectiveness of Coils
Runaround coils can achieve substantial energy savings in
hot and humid climates. The hotter and more humid the climate,
the more effective the system. The system’s design can be
flexible. The runaround coils recovery system is relatively inex-
pensive. The added cost for installing a 10,000-cfm (4719 L/s)
AHU is estimated at approximately US$8,000. This is offset by
the reduction of cooling plant capacity by about 15 tons (53
kW) for Augusta and 20 tons (70kW) for Abu Dhabi, with an
Figure 2: The psychometric process of the fresh air AHU
with runaround recovery coils.
Please circle the appropriate number on the Reader Service
Card at the back of the publication.
Extremely Helpful..................................................... 466
Helpful ................................................................... 467
Somewhat Helpful ................................................... 468
Not Helpful ............................................................. 469
installed cost of around US$800 per ton. Substantial energy
savings are gained mostly during peak summer demand.
The runaround recovery system described in this article pro-
vides a real opportunity for capital cost savings, as well as oper-
ating cost savings. It is a reliable and flexible system that can be
used with other system components to further save on energy.

ASHRAE Journal

ASHRAE JOURNAL
oday, most problems with build-
ing systems are detected as a
result of occupant complaints
or alarms provided by building
automation systems (BAS). Building op-
erators often respond by checking space
temperatures or adjusting setpoints (or
thermostat settings). The root causes of
operation problems are often not diag-
nosed, so problems reoccur and the op-
erator responds by making another ad-
justment.
When the operator diagnoses problems
more carefully by inspecting equipment,
controls or control algorithms, it is time con-
suming and often is based on rudimentary
physical reasoning and rules of thumb built
on personal experience. Many times a prop-
erly operating automatic control is over-
ridden or turned off, when it appears incor-
rect and possibly the cause of a problem.
Moreover, some problems do not manifest
themselves in conditions that directly af-
fect occupants in obvious ways and, as a
result, go undetected. These problems may,
however, affect energy costs or indoor air
quality. Furthermore, as performance con-
tracting for services becomes more preva-
lent, the need for tools to ensure good per-
formance will increase.
With the increased use of building
automation and the prevalence of per-
sonal computers in the workplace, the
computing infrastructure already exists
for deploying new automated tools for
building operation. The underlying meth-
ods for detecting and diagnosing faults
in building systems are emerging from
research and development efforts. The
BACnet™ standard (ANSI/ASHRAE
About the Authors
T
Diagnostics for Outdoor Air
Ventilation and Economizers
By Michael Brambley, Ph.D.,
Member ASHRAE
Robert Pratt,
David Chassin,
Srinivas Katipamula, Ph.D.,
Member ASHRAE
and
Darrel Hatley
Standard 135-1995, BACnet™—A Data
Communication Protocol for Building
Automation and Control Networks) will
contribute to easier implementation by
providing a standard mechanism for com-
munication between diagnostic software
and devices. The stage is set for practical
applications of fault detection and diag-
nosis to emerge within commercial prod-
ucts to help facility staff detect and solve
operation problems and ensure comfort-
able, healthy, indoor environments, while
reducing costs and providing building
staff greater job satisfaction.
Michael R. Brambley, Ph.D., is a staff scientist at Pacific North-
west National Laboratory, Richland, Wash. He is a past chair of
ASHRAE Technical Committee (TC) 1.5, Computer Applications
and is actively involved in TC 4.11, Smart Building Systems.
Rob Pratt is a staff scientist at Pacific Northwest National Labo-
ratory.
David P. Chassin is the technical group manager for building
sciences at Pacific Northwest National Laboratory. He is a gradu-
ate of and was formerly adjunct to the Rensselaer Polytechnic
Institute School of Architecture.
Srinivas Katipamula, Ph.D., is a senior research scientist at
Pacific Northwest National Laboratory. He has a doctorate in
mechanical engineering from Texas A&M University and is
active in TC 4.7, Load Calculations and TC 4.11, Smart Build-
ing Systems.
Darrel Hatley is a facility management controls specialist with
Pacific Northwest Laboratory.
Figure 1: User interface of the outdoor air/economizer diagnostician showing
hours of proper operation (green squares) and faulty operation (other colors).
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, October 1998. © Copyright 1998 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers,
Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
5 0
ASHRAE Journal
October 1 9 9 8
Early work in diagnostics for building operation focused on
the use of expert systems and data visualization for identifying
building performance problems.1, 2, 3, 7 More recently, work in
this field has focused on exploring other techniques for auto-
mating fault detection and diagnosis 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 11 and on labora-
tory testing.10 These technologies are beginning to move into
field testing and should begin to appear in commercially avail-
able building automation systems and software for building
management in the next few years.
Outdoor Air/Economizer Diagnostician
As part of its mission in commercial building research and
development, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) in collabo-
ration with industry is developing a tool that automates detec-
tion and diagnosis of problems associated with outdoor-air
ventilation and economizer operation. The tool, known as the
outdoor air/economizer (OAE) diagnostician, monitors the per-
formance of air-handling units (AHUs) and detects problems
with outside air control and economizer operation, using sen-
sors that are commonly installed for control purposes.
The current prototype of the OAE diagnostician works on
constant-volume systems and variable-air-volume (VAV) sys-
tems that do not use volume compensation (i.e., outside-air
intake is a constant fraction of the supply-airflow rate). Although
still prevalent in the field, uncompensated VAV systems do not
satisfy Standard 62-1989 under some conditions; therefore, the
OAE diagnostician will provide a warning during set up for
such systems. The AHUs may or may not use economizer con-
trol. For systems without economizers, the diagnostician de-
tects only ventilation problems, based on ventilation require-
ments established by ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-1989, Venti-
lation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality or specified by the
user during set up. For systems with economizers, it detects
both ventilation and economizer operational problems.
The diagnostician currently supports integrated economizer
operation, where simultaneous use of the economizer and me-
chanical cooling is permitted. Economizer control may be based
on a dry-bulb temperature differential, an enthalpy differential,
a high limit dry-bulb temperature or a high limit enthalpy.
The tool diagnoses the operating conditions of AHUs using
rules derived from engineering models of proper and improper
air-handler performance. These rules are implemented in a deci-
sion tree structure in software. The diagnostician uses data
collected periodically (e.g., from a BAS) to navigate the deci-
sion tree and reach conclusions regarding the operating state
of the AHU. At each point in the tree, a rule is evaluated based
on the data, and the result determines which branch the diag-
nosis follows. A conclusion is reached regarding the current
condition of the AHU when the end of a branch is reached.
The building operator or installer of the diagnostician enters
data during setup that characterizes each AHU and its control.
These data are entered only once, when the diagnostician is
configured for the AHU. During operation, the diagnostician
uses data collected periodically (e.g., hourly) to detect and di-
agnose problems with the air handler’s performance. The peri-
odically collected data can originate from a BAS, datalogger or
other data collection system. All data are stored in a database.
As a result, the diagnostician can operate in batch mode, pro-
cessing many hours or days of data at once, or in (near) real-
time, processing data as they are collected.
All data are filtered through a high-low range-checking rou-
tine before they are passed on to the diagnostic module. If any
variable that is critical for diagnostics is found to be out of
range, no diagnosis is provided for the time step. In addition,
tolerances are specified for each measured and derived vari-
able. These can be adjusted to increase the detection sensitiv-
ity or to decrease the false-alarm rate associated with noise in
the measured data.
When the outdoor air, return air and mixed air temperatures
are close to each other, the outdoor air fraction cannot be accu-
rately calculated; therefore, no diagnostics are provided when
such conditions occur. From the perspective of energy savings,
this is not a problem. When outdoor air and return air tempera-
tures are nearly equal, problems associated with the economizer
operation are less critical because the energy penalty (or ben-
efit) of using a greater amount of air from one source rather than
the other is small. However, problems associated with outdoor
air ventilation are still critical under these conditions and can be
Figure 2: Windows show a description of a problem, a potential cause of the problem and suggested corrective actions.
October 1 9 9 8
ASHRAE Journal
5 1
B U I L D I N G A U T O M A T I O N
diagnosed only if the position of the out-
door air damper is measured.
The current prototype detects about
20 different basic operation problems.
These problems are associated with:
• Inadequate outside air to meet ven-
tilation requirements, potentially result-
ing in poor indoor air quality.
• Too much outside air resulting in
wasted energy to heat or cool it.
• Economizer operating when it should
not or at an outside air intake rate that is
too high.
• Economizer not operating when it
should or at an outside air intake rate that
is too low.
• Control or sensor problems, such as
mechanical cooling operating when the
economizer is 100% open and the out-
door air conditions are sufficient to sat-
isfy the cooling load without mechanical
cooling.
• Incorrect setup of the diagnostician.
The diagnostician uses color coding
to alert the building operator when prob-
lems occur and then provides assistance
in identifying the causes of the problems
and in correcting them (see Figure 1). The
system also provides an explanation of
each diagnosis. In some cases the diag-
nostician can isolate a single cause of the
problem. In other cases, it identifies mul-
tiple potential causes. When this occurs,
the building operation and maintenance
staff must distinguish among the causes
by inspecting the air-handling unit.
For example, if the diagnostician finds
that too much warm outside air is brought
in for ventilation, it might identify two
potential causes: 1) the outside air damper
is stuck open and 2) an air temperature
sensor has failed and is causing poor
damper control. The diagnostician alerts
staff to the problem and directs them to
the most likely causes, but the operation
staff must still distinguish between these
causes by inspection. As a result, a prob-
lem that might have gone undetected is
found and repair efforts are targeted to
the most likely causes. Time is saved, but
some intervention by an operator is re-
quired. The next version of the OAE di-
agnostician will isolate the cause of a
problem to the one most likely, providing
even greater benefits.
As an example, Figure 2 shows pop-
up windows providing a description of a
problem, a potential cause
and suggested corrective
actions for the purple cell
for 5:00 a.m. on June 21,
1997 in Figure 1. These
windows are accessed by
clicking the mouse on the
cell and then clicking on
the “Causes” button. If
other potential causes ex-
ist, they can be revealed
by clicking on the “Next”
button.
Building Tests
The OAE diagnosti-
cian is presently installed
and operating on AHUs
in two buildings. The first
is the newly constructed and occupied
DOE William R. Wiley Environmental Mo-
lecular Sciences Laboratory (EMSL) in
Richland, Wash. The 200,000-ft2 (18,580-
m2) building houses laboratories, offices,
conference rooms and computer facilities.
A commercial BAS provides monitoring
and control of the facility using 3,421 sen-
sor points. This building is more highly
instrumented than most commercial build-
ings of similar size, but the data used by
the diagnostician are commonly found in
buildings with BASs. The diagnostician
currently monitors three AHUs in this
building. All AHUs are approximately 20-
ton (70-kW) cooling capacity.
The second building is the Technical
Management Center, also located on the
Richland campus of Pacific Northwest
National Laboratory. This 72,700-ft2 (6754-
m2) office building constructed in 1973
has four central AHUs with economizers.
A commercial BAS provides monitoring
and control of the facility using 420 sen-
sor points. The diagnostician monitors
all four AHUs in this building.
For each AHU, data are recorded
hourly from BAS sensors for outside air
temperature, return air temperature, mixed
air temperature, supply air temperature,
on/off status of the supply fan, and open/
closed status of the chilled water and hot
water valves (see Figure 3). To minimize
problems associated with stratification,
Figure 3: Schematic of an AHU showing the location of measured sensor points.
Figure 4: Location of the “failed” sensor.
5 2
ASHRAE Journal
October 1 9 9 8
the mixed air temperatures for all AHUs
are average values of temperatures mea-
sured across the cross-section of the
duct. No sensors were installed specifi-
cally for the diagnostician; all of these
data points are used by the BAS for con-
trol purposes.
The data are automatically transferred
each hour from the BAS to the
diagnostician’s database, using a dy-
namic data exchange (DDE) connection.
The diagnostician then periodically pro-
cesses the new data producing diagnos-
tic results that can be viewed on the user
interface.
Test Results
Of the seven air handlers monitored,
four were found to have problems shortly
after initial processing of data. The prob-
lems found included a sensor malfunction,
return air dampers not closing fully, a mixed
air sensor problem, and a chilled-water
controller problem. All problems have been
confirmed by inspection of the AHUs.
Figures 1 and 2 show some results for
AHU-06 in the EMSL building, as an ex-
ample. This AHU operates continuously
24 hours-a-day. The diagnostician de-
tected a problem with the outdoor air sen-
sor and identified one of the potential
causes as a failed temperature sensor.
Inspection of the air handler and its sen-
sors revealed that a problem with the out-
side air sensor did indeed exist. Rather
than a failure of the sensor itself, how-
ever, the location and installation of the
sensor caused it to read incorrect air tem-
peratures. Figure 4 shows the sensor lo-
cation. It was located in a non-aspirated
tube with the top of the tube sealed and
mounted in a corner under an overhang.
This arrangement did not allow the air to
circulate adequately. When the walls ad-
jacent to the tube were heated by sun-
light, the sensor indicated a temperature
closer to the wall temperature than the air
temperature.
This is shown in Figure 5, where the
outdoor air temperature indicated by this
sensor (O/A AHU-06) reads significantly
higher in the morning when it is exposed
to direct sunlight, than the outdoor air
temperature from a nearby air handler (O/
A AHU-10). Because each AHU in this
building has its own outside air sensor,
this problem can be corrected by simply
mapping one of the other outside air sen-
sors to this variable in the BAS.
Figure 6 shows the diagnostician
screen for another example, AHU-02 at
the Technology Management Center.
This AHU also operates continuously (24-
hours-a-day). The diagnostician detected
that cooling energy was being wasted
because the economizer was operating
partly closed even when the outside air
conditions were favorable for economiz-
ing. Inspection of the AHU revealed that
the return air damper was not closing com-
pletely when the economizer called for
100% outside air. The diagnostician pro-
vided this as one of the suggested causes.
Figure 6 also illustrates that the diag-
nostic results depend on outside and op-
erating conditions. These conditions de-
termine the mode in which the AHU oper-
ates and, therefore, whether a problem can
be observed or not. For example, when
the outside air condition is not favorable
for economizing, the damper problem is
not apparent, and the cells are green. In
some cases, an underlying cause may also
manifest as different problems at differ-
ent times. On Friday, June 27, for instance,
the diagnostician identifies (red cells in
Figure 6) that the economizer should not
be at part flow from midnight to 3 a.m. At
4 a.m., the diagnosis changes to purple,
in this case indicating that mechanical
cooling should not be off (although it is).
This apparent problem persists until 9
a.m., when the cells become red again.
Later in the day, the cells are green (start-
ing at noon), when conditions are such
that a problem is not apparent.
The underlying cause, which is the
leaky damper described earlier, has not
changed, but the apparent problem that
the diagnostician identifies has changed
because outside air conditions and oc-
cupancy changed. In each case, the un-
derlying problem was one of several iden-
tified as possible by the diagnostician
(details are not shown in the figure). By
observing the diagnostic results over
several hours, the operator can infer the
most likely cause of the problem. The
ability to automatically consider trends
to refine the diagnosis, as done manually
for this problem, is currently under de-
velopment.
Discussion and Conclusions
The OAE diagnostician has proven
effective in identifying installation and
operation problems in AHUs during ini-
tial field testing. Furthermore, the small
sample of air handlers monitored have
confirmed our suspicion in selecting out-
door-air intake control for as our focus—
improper installation, control, and opera-
tion are common. Four of the first seven
AHUs monitored had some type of prob-
lem. The results indicate that automated
diagnostic technology promises to help
identify and eliminate these common
problems.
The current version of the OAE diag-

and outdoor air temperature of AHU-10.


nostician is a prototype intended to dem-
onstrate the potential for this technology
and provide a methodology that ulti-
mately is deployed in different ways. For
example, the OAE diagnostician could
serve as a tool to support commission-
ing, routine building operation, or equip-
ment servicing. During commissioning,
the diagnostician could help ensure that
air handlers are installed and operating
properly. It would process data and iden-
tify problems, which would be eliminated
as part of the commissioning process.
To extend its application to support
routine building operation, control sys-
tem installers or manufacturers could de-
ploy the OAE diagnostician as an embed-
ded part of a control system, BAS, or su-
pervisory software. In this form, the di-
agnostician would provide on-demand
support to building operators or facility
managers. Operations staff could access
diagnostic results as often as desired to
support operation of their facilities.
By monitoring air handler performance
and diagnosing problems continuously,
the diagnostician would ensure that
equipment is maintained and operated
properly, providing the equivalent of con-
tinuous commissioning. If used at a cen-
tral location by a manager of several prop-
erties or a campus, the diagnostician
could process data from several different
buildings. This would reduce the fre-
quency of site visits, improve operation
and maintenance of air handlers and lower
operating costs.
The OAE diagnostician represents
only one application of automated diag-
nostics to building equipment and sys-
tems. Diagnostic technology could be
used to detect and diagnose problems
with many other HVAC components and
systems—boilers, chillers, variable-air-
volume (VAV) boxes, and thermal energy
storage systems, to name a few. Deploy-
ment of automated diagnostics and the
continuous commissioning that it could
provide should help improve building op-
eration, potentially bringing improved
comfort, air quality, longer equipment life,
and lower costs.

ASHRAE JOURNAL
Automated Control in Response
To Real-Time Pricing of Electricity
M
By Steven D. Gabel, P.E.,
Member ASHRAE
Laurence Carmichael,
and
Gideon Shavit, Ph.D.
Fellow ASHRAE
About the Authors
Steven D. Gabel, P.E., is a senior principal development engineer at Honeywell Tech-
nology Center in Minneapolis. He holds a degree in mechanical engineering from the
University of Wisconsin.
Laurence Carmichael is a technical manager at the Electric Power Research Institute,
Palo Alto, Calif. He has a bachelor’s degree in chemical engineering from the University
of California at Berkeley and a master’s degree in mechanical engineering from Stanford
University.
Gideon Shavit, Ph.D., is an independent consultant. Previously, he worked as a chief
engineering fellow at Honeywell Home and Building Control. He holds a doctorate in
mechanical and aerospace engineering from the Illinois Institute of Technology.
Figure 1: An automated RTP control system.
any states now allow elec-
tric utilities to offer real-time
pricing (RTP) rate struc-
tures to commercial build-
ing and industrial customers. Other states
are allowing tests of RTP tariffs. With the
deregulation of the electric utility indus-
try continuing to spread nationwide, RTP
and other methods of variable rate pricing
are expected to become widely available.
As a result, new control technologies are
needed to help building owners take ad-
vantage of RTP, hourly pricing contracts,
or the freedom to buy energy through the
power exchange or spot markets.1
The acronym RTP is used widely by
electric utilities. Readers from the HVAC
industry may find it more understandable
to think of it as load control in response
to variable rate pricing. The control re-
sponse happens in real time. The vari-
able rates are typically transmitted at in-
tervals (for example, a day in advance),
not continuously “in real time.”
In response to RTP, automated control
holds particular promise for commercial
buildings.2 The prospect of significant
savings on electric bills, which are a major
part of building operating costs, gives
commercial building owners an economic
incentive to adjust electric usage by shed-
ding, and/or rescheduling loads from build-
ing systems during periods when the
utility’s cost to provide power is high.
Electric utilities also have strong incen-
tives to offer RTP rates to encourage de-
mand side energy management by custom-
ers.3 Peak electric load reduction decreases
costs for generating, transmitting and dis-
tributing power. In addition, RTP adds
value as a money-saving customer service.
Manual RTP control
One major challenge in achieving the
potential of RTP savings has always been
the control method. A large commercial
building typically has dozens or hun-
dreds of heating, cooling, ventilating and
lighting electrical loads whose control
setpoints, start/stop schedules and other
operating parameters need to be managed
to take full advantage of RTP rates.
In the late 1980s, the major electric util-
ity serving New York City began a pilot
program to evaluate the benefits of RTP. A
select group of commercial building cus-
tomers participated in a pilot RTP program.
Each customer set up its own manual
process for receiving RTP rates, deciding
on control responses and executing con-
trol actions. The manual RTP control sites
achieved some energy cost savings, but
also created operational difficulties—
manual control was labor intensive and
inefficient. For example, the building op-
erator had to receive a fax of upcoming
RTP rates from the utility, then make real-
time decisions on the appropriate load
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, November 1998. © Copyright 1998 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers,
Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
N o v e m b e r 1 9 9 8
ASHRAE Journal
2 7
C O N T R O L S
management strategy to use — while fac-
toring in such variables as outdoor tem-
perature and predicted occupancy. One
common problem: the designated opera-
tor often was not available to manually
adjust controls. Second, changes to con-
trol setpoints, start/stop schedules and
other control parameters had to be made
precisely, item by item, to achieve savings
without sacrificing occupant comfort.
Clearly, manual RTP control was too
complicated, especially for large build-
ings. To be practical, automated control
was necessary. In 1992 an industry/gov-
ernment consortium collaborated to de-
velop an automated RTP control system
for commercial buildings.
Their cooperation included the devel-
opment of software that electric utilities
could use to compile and transmit RTP
rate information; a second software pro-
gram to provide an interface between the
utility and the building owner’s building
automation/energy management system;
and a RTP controller to command control
points (electrical loads) in the building
(see Figure 1).
This system enabled energy control
strategies for typical building mechani-
cal systems including fan systems,
pumps, lighting, chillers and miscella-
neous electrical loads. The control strat-
egies were configured to respond to
changing RTP rates, while accounting for
user-defined criteria such as occupancy
schedules and space temperatures.
Automated Control Installation
The site chosen for the first installation
of automated RTP control in a large com-
mercial building was the 51-story Marriott
Marquis Hotel in New York City, the larg-
est in the Marriott chain. The building,
completed in 1986, contains 1.8-million ft2
(167 200 m2) of floor space, with more than
1,900 guestrooms and many shops, res-
taurants and other public areas.
The mechanical systems include three
900-ton (3170 kW) electric centrifugal
chillers and dozens of constant volume
and variable-air-volume units. Heating is
done using purchased steam. The build-
ing does not have a thermal energy stor-
age system or fuel-switching capability.
The guestrooms (approximately
800,000 ft2 [74 00 m2]; 45% of total floor
space) were excluded from RTP control,
to ensure that guests retained primary
control of their room comfort conditions.
Some 70 air-handling, ventilating and
lighting systems in the remaining 55% of
the hotel’s floor space (about 1 million ft2
[93 000 m2] were placed under automated
RTP control, as were several large exte-
rior signs. These building areas included
a large central atrium, ballrooms, meeting
rooms, retail spaces, public walkways,
restaurants and non-public service areas.
Because extensive rewiring of lighting
was impractical for this project, only a
small part of lighting loads are involved.
The automated RTP control system in-
stalled in the hotel commands both ana-
log and discrete control points through
direct digital control (DDC) devices. The
software’s operator interface is integrated
with the existing building management
system.
The control strategy for each system
or piece of equipment has criteria that
must be met before its load can be shed,
reduced or rescheduled. These criteria,
which need to be set only once during
commissioning, include RTP rate limits,
occupancy schedules and space comfort
requirements.
During commissioning, each RTP-con-
trolled point was configured for its spe-
cific application requirements. An example
user screen (for an air-handling system)
is shown in Figure 2. If necessary, build-
ing operators can easily make changes
from the PC front-end to their building
management system. The control func-
tions available to the operator include
RTP control status for each controlled
load, configuration of control parameters
for each controlled load, and reports of
RTP rates, load reductions and calculated
energy and cost savings. For the screen
in Figure 2, the control logic includes:
If the RTP price is greater than 10¢/
kWh, and
Ø
If the air-handling system AC-2 is in
its occupied period, and
Ø
If the space temperature is not over
80°F (27°C), then
Ø
Command system AC-2 off.
In this example, if the above control
parameters are not met, AC-2 continues
to operate. This particular point-defini-
tion structure provides a two-state on/
off response. In more sophisticated ap-
plications, points are cascaded to provide
additional steps of control.
In this application, the utility provided
day-ahead notice of its upcoming vari-
able prices for electricity, with the next
day’s hourly RTP rates downloading from
the utility via dial-up phone line. The RTP
control system responds to the rate
schedule hour by hour, first by analyzing

Table 1: 1994 energy savings results (kWh) by equipment type.
its available, preprogrammed control
strategies for building systems under its
control, and then by executing the most
appropriate sequence of events.
It re-evaluates the control logic for each
controlled load each hour (adjustable to
half-hourly) and, if necessary, makes ad-
justments to controlled equipment. This
allows the RTP control system to respond
automatically, not only to hourly variations
in electricity prices, but also to unantici-
pated conditions such as changes in oc-
cupancy status or room temperature.
Approximately 70 building loads (pri-
marily air-handling system fans) were
configured for automated RTP control,
each with a specific RTP price trigger
setpoint and a projected peak kilowatt re-
duction. The maximum expected load re-
duction of 1,215 kW (not including re-
lated energy reductions at the chillers)
was 15 to 20% of total peak electrical de-
mand (6 MW) for the entire building, not
just for the 55% of floor space under au-
tomated RTP control.
Performance Results
Detailed performance data on all RTP
controlled systems was collected from
July 1993 through June 1995. (Less de-
tailed data, focusing largely on annual
energy and cost savings, continues to
be collected.) The three most recent years
with conventional time-of-use electric
rates prior to the automated RTP control
startup were used as a basis to compare
savings. In those years, electric consump-
tion averaged approximately 38 million
kWh per year, with a peak electric load of
approximately 6 MW — enough for a
small town. During this base period, the
hotel’s electricity bills averaged approxi-
mately $3 million per year.
The performance data shows that au-
tomated control in response to RTP is en-
abling the building owner to manage elec-
tricity consumption better and to achieve
significant savings on the hotel’s electric-
ity bills by shifting loads (including recov-
ery or “makeup” loads) to hours when elec-
tricity is cheaper. Key results include:
• With automatic control responses to
RTP rates, the building owner saved more
than $1 million in four years — an aver-
age of more than $250,000 per year. This
represents cost savings of approximately
8.3% compared to conventional time-of-
use electric rates.
• Average yearly kWh consumption
dropped by more than two million kWh
— from approximately 38 million kWh
during the baseline period to less than 36
million kW during the monitoring period.
This represents an energy savings of ap-
proximately 5.2% compared to conven-
tional time-of-use rates.
• Peak electric load was reduced by up
to 1500 kW during high price periods.
• These savings were achieved with-
out compromising comfort or service.
Energy savings were computed by log-
ging those periods when each of the vari-
ous RTP-controlled loads was curtailed.
Using a predetermined kilowatt load value
for each load, the total energy savings
could be computed directly.
Savings at the chillers were computed
using a simple model based on outdoor
ambient temperature, assuming a nomi-
nal chiller efficiency. No direct measure-
ments of individual loads and energy sav-
ings were made and there was no explicit
treatment of time-shifted chiller loads;
however, the computed savings values
were compared with daily and historical
electric meter data to validate the results.
The hotel’s director of engineering veri-
fied dollar savings.
Air-handling system fan loads typi-
cally accounted for 60% to 70% of total
monthly RTP cost savings. Chiller energy
savings accounted for up to 30% of the
monthly total during the summer months.
N o v e m b e r 1 9 9 8
ASHRAE Journal
2 9
C O N T R O L S
Figure 3: Automated RTP savings for July 8, 1994.
Other load categories provided the bal-
ance of the savings.
As an example, Table 1 summarizes
electric load reductions for each type of
RTP-controlled load for each month in
1994. Automated RTP control of air-han-
dling systems provided the majority of
savings. Predictably, load reduction and
cost savings were highest during the cool-
ing season. Load reductions for lighting
varied widely during this year due to op-
erator overrides and the pattern of usage
in the affected spaces.
The impact of RTP control on a day
with high electricity prices is particularly
impressive. Figure 3 shows results for
one day, July 8, 1994. Some load reduc-
tion was achieved every hour, with a maxi-
mum peak load reduction of approxi-
mately 1500 kW and a one-day electricity
cost savings of more than $18,000.
The long-term value of automated con-
trol in response to real-time pricing can
be summarized by the reductions in (a)
electricity use and (b) the hotel’s cost for
electricity. This value is demonstrated by
comparing a four-year operating period
with RTP control to three prior years of
operation with conventional electricity
rates.
There were average annual savings,
attributable to automated RTP control, of
5.2% in electricity consumption and 8.3%
in the hotel’s cost for electricity. These
savings were achieved even though only
about 70 loads, serving 55% of the total
building floor space, are under automated
RTP control.
Since the installation of the automated
RTP control system, the hotel’s occu-
pancy rate has increased by 28%. Al-
though the guestrooms are not under RTP
control, it is reasonable to assume that
there also has been a significant increase
in “occupancy” of the central atrium, ball-
rooms, meeting rooms, restaurants, retail
spaces and other public areas that were
under RTP control.
Conclusions
The hotel’s director of engineering, Ed
Pietzak, considers the automated RTP
control system a valuable tool. He said
that the system is managed very easily,
very conveniently, from one location
down in the chiller plant.
This first installation of automated RTP
control in a large commercial building
shows that, when compared to conven-
tional electric rates, automated RTP con-
trol can significantly reduce electricity
consumption and costs. It can achieve
these results even when only a portion
of the building and its mechanical and
lighting systems are controlled in re-
sponse to RTP. When compared to
manual RTP control, automated control
eliminates labor-intensive inefficiencies
and can encourage building owners/op-
erators to place more building loads un-
der RTP control.
The automated RTP control system,
integrated with the building’s energy
management system, is easy for opera-
tors to use. When needed, manual over-
rides are easily accommodated. The re-
turn on investment (ROI) for an automated
RTP control system will depend on the
characteristics of the building and the
expected electric utility RTP rate profile.
In most cases, automated control in re-
sponse to RTP can be added to building
energy management systems either di-
rectly or via an industry standard inter-
face such as BACnet
™.
When implementing an automated
RTP control system, building owners
and operators should carefully review
the overall energy efficiency of the
building and its mechanical, lighting
and other electrical equipment.4 The
potential for a given building will also
be a function of the degree of commit-
ment on the part of the building oper-
ating staff.
Acknowledgements
The authors wish to acknowledge the
interest, enthusiasm and active support of
Lou Accurso and Tim Lynch of Consoli-
dated Edison Co. of New York, Frank
Porretto and Ed Torrero of the Empire State
Electric Energy Research Corporation,
Mary Ann Bowers of the New York State
Energy Research and Development Au-
thority, and John Flood of Utility Consult-
ing Service. The authors also acknowledge
the important role of Ed Pietzak, director of
engineering, Marriott Marquis Hotel.
References
1. Brown, M. 1998. “Restructuring?” Hart’s
Energy Markets 3:29–38.
2. Hoffman, S. and R. Renner. “Taking advan-
tage of real-time pricing [of electricity],” EPRI
Journal 22(2):16–23.
3. Schweppe, F., et al. 1988. Spot Pricing of
Electricity. Boston: Kluwer Academic Pub-
lishers.
4. Norford, L., S. Englander and B. Wisely.
1996. “Demonstration knowledge base to aid
building operators in responding to real-time
pricing electricity rates.” Final Report on
ASHRAE Research Project 883-RP.

ASHRAE Journal
4 9
ASHRAE JOURNAL
his article describes the approaches used in the com-
missioning of two laboratory buildings at Rensselaer
Polytechnic Institute in Troy, N.Y. and the results
that were achieved. It is an approach based on the
requirements outlined in ASHRAE Guideline 1-1989, The HVAC
Commissioning Process and can be used where resources may
not be available for a full, formal commissioning process. The
following is a detailed description of the approach and a list of
20 of the deficiencies discovered during commissioning. Three
of these items are explored in detail and serve as examples of
how problems can occur and why commissioning is necessary.
This presentation of real examples of the benefits of commis-
sioning can be used to help to convince your project team to
commit the necessary resources to perform commissioning on
your next construction project.
The Owners’ Goals
We wanted to accomplish the following:
1. Avoid negative past experiences with buildings that were
not commissioned.
2. Ensure that operating costs during the first year and over
the life of the building would be minimized.
3. Involve the maintenance trades early to improve training
and their knowledge of the building, thereby creating a feeling
of ownership.
4. Demonstrate positive results to help justify commission-
ing for future projects.
The project team performed commissioning on two build-
ings on the campus (see Table 1). Although complete formal
commissioning was not supported by the project funds, a sig-
nificant amount of acceptance testing was accomplished. The
results justified the efforts of those involved.
The ASHRAE Approach
Full formal commissioning based on Guideline 1-1989 requires
many detailed steps, including:
1. Pre-Design Phase: Develop a strong program statement
clearly defining the goals of the facility. Develop an approach
to the commissioning process.
2. Design Phase: Clearly document all design criteria, de-
velop written commissioning plan and assign responsibilities
for the process.
3. Construction Phase: Review submittals to ensure accu-
rate operating information is available. Monitor construction to
ensure installations are accessible.
4. Acceptance Procedures: Perform acceptance testing of
the physical installation of all equipment. Perform functional
performance tests.
5. Post Acceptance Phase: Revise as-built documentation as
required and perform proper maintenance on the equipment.
Periodically retest equipment.
One consultant’s formal proposal for commissioning services
on Building 1 amounted to 2.7% of the total construction costs
for the project. Because this cost was not in the budget and
additional funds were not available, we did not hire this con-
sultant to perform commissioning services.
The Owners’ Approach
For both of our projects, we were not able to accomplish all
five steps as recommended by Guideline 1-1989. We began by
involving the maintenance and operations group in the design
reviews conducted during the design phase. As the owner, we
observed the design details and made modifications that we
saw as necessary to improve the maintenance, operability and
energy use of the systems. During construction, this same owner
review group observed the physical installation of equipment
to ensure adequate access and to ensure that specific compo-
nents that were incorporated into the specifications met our
facilities’ standards.
We concentrated most of our efforts on the acceptance
phase. We took two approaches toward documentation and
checkout for each project.
For Building 1, we obtained a budget for supporting some
commissioning work and hired the design engineer to develop
the required documentation and manage the field acceptance
testing work. In addition, the engineer was responsible for writ-
ing the building design description and helping to ensure the
operating and maintenance documentation submitted by the me-
chanical contractor was complete. The engineer was paid about
0.5% of the total construction costs for this scope of work.
Although some consider using the design engineer in this
capacity analogous to asking the “fox to watch the hen house,”
we were convinced that the design engineer was the best judge
of how to test the system they designed. Because the owner
remained a part of the process, we were not concerned that the
engineer might ignore design problems. In fact, the engineer dis-
covered and documented design problems that were corrected.
Commissioning Lab Buildings
By Steven L. Angle, P.E.
Member ASHRAE
About the Author
Steven L. Angle, P.E., is a facilities engineer in the Campus
Planning and Facilities Design department at Rensselaer Poly-
technic Institute in Troy, N.Y. He has a master’s degree in me-
chanical engineering from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute.
T
Small and Medium-Sized Facilities

5 0
ASHRAE Journal
N o v e m b e r 1 9 9 8
For Building 2, we used a mechanical
engineer from the campus facilities engi-
neering group assigned to the project.
This person was responsible for develop-
ing the check sheets that defined the static
and dynamic testing required on each sys-
tem, performing the acceptance testing and
documenting the results.
For both buildings, we did not receive
change order requests from the contrac-
tors for demonstrating equipment and
controls. Some of the systems were com-
missioned without the contractor
present, but for those that required the
contractor, we relied on sections of the
specifications that required eight to 24
hours for training and statements that
required demonstration of fully func-
tional operating systems. When it came time to request that
the contractors correct deficiencies noted during the commis-
sioning, the process was handled as part of the “normal”
punchlist routine.
The Results
Table 2 lists the major problems discovered during the com-
missioning process on the two lab buildings. They range from
minor issues involving locations of thermostats to major issues
involving building heating systems. In addition, each item is
listed with the potential impact on cost and on building comfort
that could have occurred if the items had not been discovered.
To understand why these deficiencies occurred, and why
commissioning was the only way to discover them before they
became real issues, three examples are explored in detail.
Example 1: VAV Boxes. In the Building 1 project, our formal
acceptance testing required that we demonstrate each VAV box
in full cooling (max. airflow, no reheat) and full heating (min.
airflow, max. reheat) modes. During this process we discovered
several VAV boxes that would not operate at maximum and mini-
mum airflow due to dampers that were not moving freely. Some
would close when air pressure was supplied to the pneumatic
damper, but would not open with the damper return spring. The
balancer briefly mentioned this condition during the balancing
of the system, but it was not clear what effect the damper prob-
lem had until commissioning was completed.
This is an excellent example of a case where all of the con-
tractors had completed their jobs properly. However, a defect in
these boxes, caused either by damage during construction or in
the manufacturing process, would have caused serious prob-
lems. The owner would not have experienced these problems
for several months or even years until the box was required to
increase its airflow and cool the space on a hot summer day.
Example 2: Boilers. In Building 2, project boilers heat a wa-
ter/glycol solution that is then circulated to the main supply air
handler heating coils and to reheat coils throughout the build-
ing. After completion of construction , we performed a dynamic
test to artificially load the boilers and test them close to their
design capacity. We found them to be under capacity—capable
of only about 60% of what the engineer required. This discov-
ery was made after all contractors completed their work and the
systems were running. Because we were commissioning the sys-
tem in the summer months and the two boilers were sized at 140%
of the building’s design capacity, this problem would not have
been discovered until the middle of the next winter, at which time it
could have created serious heating problems for the building.
After many days of testing and working closely with the
supplier, it was discovered that the boilers had two problems.
First, the boilers were operating with a glycol coil submersed
in a water jacket that was heated by the oil flame. Significant
stratification was occurring in the water jacket of the boiler
and circulating pumps had to be added to maintain a uniform
(higher) temperature in that jacket. The second problem was
that the boilers’ nameplate rating that met the engineering
specifications was for a water to water heat exchange and not
for the less efficient water to glycol solution heat exchange.
Although this problem was caused by the supplier’s errors
on the configuration of the boiler units, it is a good example of
a serious operational problem that would not have been de-
tected without formal commissioning. The problem was resolved
by the supplier at no cost to the project and was corrected
before the critical winter season began.
Example 3: Exhaust fans. Building 1 exhaust fans were de-
signed to exhaust all of the lab hood and general exhaust for the
building. During commissioning, it was discovered that the ex-
haust fans would only exhaust about 75% of the design airflow.
This was discovered after balancing (demonstrating each indi-
vidual VAV box at maximum airflow) was complete and the build-
ing was turned over and was fully operational.
The low exhaust airflow condition was demonstrated only
after a commissioning test opened fume hoods and forced the
rooms into full cooling mode to simulate a maximum design
airflow rate. This test of the building design airflow was not
required as part of the balancing specification.
The problem was caused by sound attenuators that were in-
stalled after startup to eliminate excessive noise. By design, the
sound attenuators should not have produced enough additional
static pressure drop to cause a problem. However, the actual

ASHRAE Journal
N o v e m b e r 1 9 9 8
field conditions resulted in a high static pressure across the
fans, which limited the fan’s ability to meet design airflow rates.
By detecting the problem during commissioning, we were able
to design and install a modification to the fans before the building
users experienced any problems.
Why Is Commissioning Necessary?
These results show that building systems won’t always work
if the contractors simply “do their job correctly.” Here are two
reasons as to why this is true:
Reason 1: First, the responsibilities of each of the players
involved in a major construction project need to be defined.
The engineer is responsible to supply a design complete
with drawings and specifications that define what equipment is
to be purchased and how it should be installed and controlled.
The general contractor or construction manager is respon-
sible to ensure an overall coordination of the subcontractors
work to accomplish the requirements of the specifications.
The electrical contractor is responsible for installing the elec-
trical support system as defined in the drawings and specifications.
The mechanical contractor, (including the balancing and
controls contractor) is responsible to supply, install, balance
and control the mechanical systems as required by the draw-
ings and specifications.
Table 2 (continued): Summary of the major problems discovered during commissioning.

ASHRAE Journal
5 5
C O M M I S S I O N I N G
These descriptions indicate that no one is directly respon-
sible for proving that systems work together and that the over-
all building achieves the design intent. Only the owner a com-
missioning agent is able to accomplish this.
Reason 2: Even the best contractors will not be able to accom-
plish a fully commissioned building for the following reasons.
1. Contractors do not necessarily know the engineer’s intent
for each system’s performance.
2. Their contracts are written to “furnish and install” equip-
ment and not to perform extensive troubleshooting on systems
that exhibit difficult dynamic problems.
3. Unless added as part of commissioning, their contracts do
not require them to perform testing that demonstrates building
systems in occupied/unoccupied or winter/summer modes.
4. There are many contractors involved in a project that can
be held responsible for a system that does not work. The elec-
trical, mechanical, controls and balancing contractors, in addi-
tion to suppliers, all have input into the final completed system
and can blame each other if problems arise.
5. At the end of most construction projects, there is a major
effort for contractors to complete their work and get off the site.
Unfortunately, this happens during the critical startup and com-
missioning period. Then, even small problems become major
issues the contractors must be called to make corrections.
For these reasons, many issues are left unresolved and can
be corrected only during a formal commissioning process.
Summary
The examples given of problems with VAV boxes, boilers
and exhaust fans prove that a need exists for a good commis-
sioning process. Even if contractors “do their jobs correctly,”
many complex interactions of systems and equipment are be-
yond the scope of any individual contractor. Without formal
commissioning, many systems in a new or renovated building
will be left in conditions that significantly increase the costs
for energy, maintenance and poor occupant productivity.
An investment in a moderate engineering fee or in using in-
house staff can produce effective commissioning. The approach
in this article is a worthwhile tool for small to medium size projects,
or where obtaining funding for commissioning is difficult.

ASHRAE Journal
4 1
ASHRAE JOURNAL
he advent of the variable speed
pump has prompted the formation
of many new procedures for operat-
ing hot water, chilled water and con-
denser water systems. The variable speed pump
has reduced the over-pressuring of water sys-
tems and reduced pump maintenance caused
by the excessive radial thrust often found in
constant speed pumps. Often, energy con-
sumption by variable speed pumps is a fraction
of that of constant speed pumps. However, most
of these advantages are lost when proper speed
control is not maintained in variable speed pump-
ing systems.
Variable speed pumps have been used suc-
cessfully since around 1967 in the HVAC in-
dustry. In the 1970s, they became popular
with the development of the variable fre-
quency drive. The variable frequency drive
simplified pump speed control by using a digi-
tal control for speed regulation instead of a
mechanical device. It became apparent that
specific control requirements were necessary
to achieve successful operation of variable
speed HVAC pumps. Some of these require-
ments are:
1. Variable speed pumps operated in paral-
lel have to be operated at the same speed.
Therefore, one control parameter is required
to operate a set of pumps running in parallel. A current require-
ment for successful variable speed operation is that all 1,780-
rpm pumps run within 20 rpm of each other. If the pumps do not
run within 20 rpm of each other, they will operate at less effi-
cient points on their curve. Too often, system operating prob-
Proper Control of HVAC
Variable Speed Pumps
About the Authors
Larry Tillack works at Systecon in electrical design engineering
for HVAC control systems.
James (Burt) Rishel, P.E., is chairman of Systecon. He is Re-
search Subcommittee chair of ASHRAE Technical Committee
8.10, Pumps and Hydronic Piping and is author of two books.
lems result from a lack of control of individual pump speed.
2. A requirement that has proven necessary is that differential
pressure, pressure or flow sensors that regulate pump speed
must have their calibration traceable to the National Institute of
Standards and Technology (NIST). This is the only specification
that guarantees that the quality and reliability of these instru-
ments is adequate for HVAC pump speed control.
3. The most successful method of varying the pump speed
to suit the demands of the water system, from minimum to maxi-
mum load on that system, is to properly use a differential pres-
sure transmitter that is located in the chilled or hot water sys-
tem. The actual location of the differential pressure transmitter
will be described in the next section and in Figures 1 through 3.
Since the differential pressure transmitter often is located at
T
By Larry Tillack
and
James B. Rishel, P.E.
Life Member/Fellow ASHRAE
Figure 1: Location of differential pressure transmitters on hot and chilled
water, variable speed pumping systems.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, November 1998. © Copyright 1998 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers,
Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
4 2
ASHRAE Journal
N o v e m b e r 1 9 9 8
the end of long loops where water hammer can be damaging,
the casing design pressure for the instrument should be 2,000
lbs (8900 N) working pressure.
4. A great advantage of using the differential pressure trans-
mitter is that the actual operating pressure maintained at the
various points in the system is indicated on the system PC.
Any excess pressure developed by the pump control system is
immediately known, because the PC can display the setpoint
and the actual pressure, the difference being the excess pres-
sure. If the pump speed control is operating properly, the differ-
ence between the setpoint and the actual pressure should not
be more than ±0.75 to 1 ft of pump head at the controlling
differential pressure transmitter. Such control of the differential
pressure eliminates energy loss rated by widely varying the
differential pressure.
5. The rate of response for the PID loop or DDC feedback
control must be fast enough to avoid hunting for the pump
speed. More than thirty years of experience with hundreds upon
hundreds of HVAC systems has proved that a rate of response
of 500 ms (twice per second) is adequate for most HVAC appli-
cations. This is a standard for commercially available PID loops
and is not considered very fast in the control industry. This will
be discussed further under Rate of Response.
6. A nagging problem about variable speed pump control is
that the building management system (BMS) computer is often
loaded down with critical functions. This computer (or comput-
ers) is monitoring much more than just the water management
system. This often means that the water management system
receives a low priority for useful bandwidth on the computer
network. As a result, signals buffered through a BMS computer
may not be updated in a timely or reliable (meaning at the same
time interval) manner. This requires the differential pressure
signals to be wired directly from the transmitter to the pump
speed controller.
These six parameters have proved to be indispensable in
achieving a successful and efficient HVAC variable speed pump-
ing system.
Location of the Differential Pressure Transmitter
The differential pressure transmitter must be located at the
point in the water system that will remove the variable friction
head from the control signal. The location of this transmitter is,
therefore, dependent upon the piping used to connect the heat-
ing or cooling coils. Figure 1 describes the connection for
direct and reverse return loops. The reverse return loop re-
quires a differential pressure transmitter on each end of the
loop, particularly on long loops where there may be as much as
50 ft (15 m) of pipe friction in the loop itself.
Figure 2 illustrates the need to remove the variable friction
from the control signal. This is the reason why the differential
pressure transmitter must be located at the far end of the direct
return piping loop.
Use of Valve Position to Control Pump Speed
Attempts have been made to use coil control valve position
as a means of controlling pump speed. It is questionable whether
this procedure insures that the pumps are operated at the most
efficient speed. Figure 3a demonstrates that there is no valve
position that is indicative of the total head generated by the
pumps. Figure 3b demonstrates that valve position can be a
valuable tool for reducing the differential pressure maintained
at the differential pressure transmitters. The BMS adjusts the
differential pressure transmitter setpoint with control valve
position. If any control valve is fully open, the setpoint might
Figure 2: Proper location of differential pressure transmitter. The differential pressure transmitter at pumps must be set at
100% design head, eliminating the value of the variable speed pump.
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
Advertisement in the print
edition formerly in this space.
4 6
ASHRAE Journal
N o v e m b e r 1 9 9 8
Figure 4b. In this case the PID controller
is still updating its output twice per sec-
ond, but the process value is only being
updated once every twelve seconds. In
classic control theory it is demonstrated
that sometimes a fine line exists between
a good feedback controller and a good
oscillator. The phenomenon that is hap-
pening here can be explained best by an
example.
Suppose that a man is placed in a field
and that this man has no way of judging
any distance he moves, but travels about
be 25 ft (8 m); if all valves are at least 50%
closed, the setpoint could be 15 ft (4.5 m),
and if all valves are near to the closed
position, the setpoint could be 10 ft (3 m).
The valve position/setpoint program
would be developed for each chilled or
hot water system.
Rate of Response
One question that always arises when
dealing with variable speed pump con-
trol, specifically through the use of PID
controllers, is “How fast does the pro-
cess value have to be updated?” The best
answer for this question is twice per sec-
ond or faster. The reason is that the pres-
sure in a system changes instanta-
neously, and the controller’s function is
to maintain that pressure at a setpoint.
From a simplified interpretation of the
Nyquist Criterion 1 for stable control, a
controller must update approximately
twice as fast as the fastest process that it
is trying to control. In our case, pressure
imparted by the pumps changing speed
can be realized in the system within one
second.
This means that the controller must
update at least twice per second to main-
tain stable control, and the inputs to the
controller must be updated at the same
rate or faster than the output of the con-
troller. Therefore, the inputs must be up-
dated twice per second or faster to main-
tain stable control.
For the typical pump speed PID appli-
cation, the system can be expected to
settle between 10 and 20 seconds from
the time any upset is introduced (this may
take slightly longer at system startup).
The typical application consists of a PID
controller with an update (response) rate
of twice per second and a process value
that updates at the same rate. This typi-
cal response can be seen in Figure 4a.
The system will actually run slightly
over its setpoint and then be brought back
to the correct operating point. This is nor-
mal because the controller will continue
to increase the speed of the pumps until
it receives “feedback” from the system
indicating that the pumps need to be
slowed. This is a small price to pay for
such a rapid response.
When the process value is not up-
dated in a timely manner the PID response
becomes unstable. This can be seen in
4 fps (1.2 m/s). Now the man is told that
he needs to move 20 ft (6 m) and once he
starts moving he cannot stop until com-
manded to do so. The man starts moving
and is not given the command to stop for
twelve seconds. This means he has
moved 48 ft (15 m), which is 28 ft (8.5 m)
past his destination! Now, he is given
feedback telling him that he is 28 ft (8.5 m)
from his destination and he starts on his
way. He again travels 48 ft (15 m), putting
him back to where he started!
This cycle will continue indefinitely,
Figure 3b: This diagram shows differential pressure reset with control valve
position.
Figure 3a: This diagram shows the problem of using valve position for pump
speed control.
N o v e m b e r 1 9 9 8
ASHRAE Journal
4 7
P U M P S
creating a nice oscillator, but a poor controller. While this may
seem extreme, systems like this can happen and the blame is
usually attributed to variable speed pumping instead of to the
real culprit, unreliable (slow) update from the process value.
One possible solution to this problem of oscillation would
be to slow down the PID response to match the update rate of
the process value. Doing this will create a nice stable control-
ler. The problem is that it takes a long time for the pumps to
reach their proper operating speed. If there is concern for the
efficiency of the system, this is not a good solution. In addi-
tion, if any equipment in the system relies on fast stabilization
(such as chillers), this is definitely undesirable. The response
of such a system can be seen in Figure 4c.
Suppose that this theory is applied to the man in the field.
However, now he can take only one step towards his destina-
tion every twelve seconds. While he will eventually reach his
destination and maintain his position, it will take him several
minutes to arrive there. Although this solves the problem of an
oscillating controller, it is not the best solution. Due to the
dynamics of the pressure in the system, the PID controller
really needs to update its calculation at least twice per second
to provide proper control of the system.
With all of the control knowledge extant in our industry,
