ASHRAE JOURNAL
Zoned Smoke Control Applications
By William A. Webb, P.E.
Member ASHRAE
must be significantly greater and depend on the ceiling height
of the space. The minimum value for a space having a 9-ft (2.7
m) ceiling height is 0.1 in. of water (25 Pa).
Examples of Zoned Smoke Control
The examples that follow describe different smoke control
approaches, although the smoke control objectives are similar
in each case. The three examples are as follows:
• Low-rise modular building.
• Low-rise atrium lobby.
• Car park podium of a high-rise complex.
Low-Rise Modular Building
This building is a four-story office building having a total
ground floor area of approximately 49,400 ft2 (4600 m²), com-
posed of symmetrical modules, each of approximately 8,000 ft²
(740 m²). Each of the modules contains a central atrium and
circular grand stair through all four levels. The modules are
separated from each other by two-hour horizontal exit walls
having 1.5-hour fire-rated doors held open by electromagnetic
hold-open devices. The building is equipped throughout with
an automatic sprinkler system and has smoke detectors installed
around the atrium openings. Figure 1 is a schematic plan view,
and Figure 2 is a schematic section of a typical module.
The smoke control system objectives are to limit smoke
spread from one compartment module to another and to limit
smoke spread from one floor to another long enough to allow
persons in the module to exit.
The smoke control system uses the building’s HVAC system,
which is a variable air volume (VAV) system with each module
having its own air supply and return. The supply air is ducted
through the ceiling to the office areas and the return is through
each atrium to the individual air-handling unit for the module.
Actuation of a ceiling-mounted smoke detector, a duct-type
smoke detector or a sprinkler water flow device will cause the
VAV system for the affected module to go into the following
smoke mode:
1. The air supplied in the affected module goes to 75% out-
side air.
2. The return fan for the affected module operates at 100%
O
Using HVAC Systems to Limit Smoke
f the various methods to control the movement of
smoke, the zoned smoke control system probably
has become the most prevalent. This article describes
zoned smoke control, explains how it works and
gives some examples.
According to the National Fire Protection Association’s
publication NFPA 92A, a smoke control system is defined
as “an engineered system that uses mechanical fans to pro-
duce airflows and pressure differences across smoke barri-
ers to limit smoke and direct smoke movement.”1 A zoned
smoke control system is defined by NFPA 92A as “a smoke-
control system that includes smoke exhaust for the smoke
zone and pressurization for all contiguous smoke control
zones. The remaining smoke control zones in the building
also may be pressurized.” These systems are commonly
known as pressure sandwich systems.
Location of Zoned Smoke Control Systems
Zoned smoke control systems need compartmented vol-
umes. In this context, the compartment is formed by a smoke
barrier which is defined by NFPA 92A as “a membrane, either
vertical or horizontal, such as a wall, or ceiling assembly, that is
designed and constructed to restrict the movement of smoke.
A smoke barrier may or may not have a fire-resistance rating.
Smoke barriers may have openings protected by closing
devices or adequate airflows.”
Zoned smoke control systems are used in high-rise
buildings, hospitals and similar buildings or occupan-
cies where the objective is to prevent or limit smoke spread
from one compartmented area to another. In the high-
rise building, it will usually be from one floor to another.
In the hospital, it will usually be from one smoke zone on
a floor to another.
In the typical configuration, the system uses the building’s
HVAC system to establish a pressure difference across the
compartment boundary. This is achieved by controlling the
HVAC system to exhaust without return using HVAC return
fans and to supply adjacent compartments without exhaust.
To avoid excessive pressure difference, it may be necessary to
modulate the supply to less than 100%. For buildings pro-
tected by automatic sprinkler systems, the pressure difference
across the compartment boundary need be no more than 0.05
in. of water (12.5 Pa). The values for non-sprinklered buildings
About the Author
William A. Webb, P.E., is CEO of Performance Technology
Consulting, Lake Bluff, Ill. He is Chair of ASHRAE Standard
Project Committee 149, Laboratory Methods of Testing Fans
Used to Exhaust Smoke in Smoke Management Systems and
Program Subcommittee Chair of ASHRAE Technical Committee
5.6, Control of Fire and Smoke.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, December 1998. © Copyright 1998 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers,
Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
D e c e m b e r 1 9 9 8
ASHRAE Journal
3 7
S M O K E C O N T R O L
exhaust, exhausting through return
airducts at approximately 41,000 cfm (19.3
m³/s).
3. Air return stops in adjacent modules.
4. The air supply fans adjacent to the
modules operate at 100% outside air.
5. Doors on hold-open devices in the
module and contiguous modules are re-
leased to close in alarm.
6. Dampers between the modules close.
Figure 3 is a schematic plan and Fig-
ure 4 is a schematic section view of a
module in alarm.
The system has been tested to deter-
mine that sufficient pressure difference is
created in the smoke-control mode to pre-
vent smoke spread from the alarm module
to adjacent modules. The pressure differ-
ence was not too great to prevent egress
doors from being opened in the alarm
module. The tests also demonstrated that
the system controlled smoke long enough
for occupants in the alarm module to exit.
Lobby Atrium Building
This building is a three-story museum
and library that has a ground floor area of
approximately 42,000 ft² (3900 m²). A cen-
tral atrium lobby provides access to each
wing, one for the library and one for the
art gallery. Figure 5 is a schematic plan
view of the building. Except for the atrium
lobby, the building is protected by pre-
action automatic sprinkler systems and
wet pipe sprinkler systems. The atrium
lobby does not have automatic sprinklers
because it is constructed of concrete and
other noncombustible materials.
Transient combustibles are limited
and consist of items patrons or staff
carry, cleaning carts and library or mu-
seum items in transit through the lobby.
These would be small because there are
separate delivery areas that have di-
rect access without passing through
the lobby.
The lobby connects all levels of the
building. Rolling steel fire shutters and
fire doors at some of the openings limit
the number of interconnected levels to a
maximum of two.
The building has a complete automatic
detection system consisting primarily of
smoke detectors except for small
nonpublic areas that have heat detectors.
The detection system is used to actuate
the pre-action sprinkler system zones.
The HVAC system supplies approxi-
mately 1 to 1.5 cfm/ft² (5 to 8 L/s/m2). Each
gallery floor has an individual air-handling
unit with ducted supply and ducted re-
turn. The library consists of two floors
and has a single air-handling unit with
ducted supply and ducted return.
The smoke control system objective
is to limit smoke spread from one smoke
control zone to another. Those zones co-
incide with the HVAC system zones.
The smoke control system is initiated
by zone. The ceiling-mounted detectors,
duct-mounted smoke detectors and wet
pipe sprinkler system water flow indica-
tors activate the smoke control system.
When the detection system activates, the
HVAC system for the affected zone shuts
down the supply to that zone and ex-
hausts to the outside using the return
fans. Contiguous zones have their air re-
turn shut down, and air is supplied at a
minimum of 25% outside air.
The systems were tested and demon-
Figure 2: An HVAC system schematic section of a typical module.
Figure 1: Schematic plan view of modular building at normal condition.
3 8
ASHRAE Journal
D e c e m b e r 1 9 9 8
strated that the objective could be
achieved. The pressure difference across
boundaries in alarm was adequate to
cause airflow to the fire module from the
contiguous module when the building was
in smoke control mode.
Car Park Podium
The project consists of a five-level park-
ing garage that forms a podium for mul-
tiple high-rise towers above. The towers
are residential and office buildings. Auto-
matic sprinklers protect the entire complex.
The architect considered alternate
schemes for whether or not to compart-
ment the garage. Under the compartment
scheme, the garage would be separated
by two-hour walls at each level to seg-
ment the parking garage according to the
tower below which it is located. The
alternative scheme would be an undivided
five-level parking garage. In each case,
the parking garage podium would be
separated from the towers above by a
two-hour fire-resistive assembly.
The purpose of the study was to evalu-
ate the parking garage compartment
schemes to recommend conceptual smoke
control provisions. The objectives of the
study were as follows:
1. Recommend an extract rate adequate
to allow safe egress of parking garage oc-
cupants.
2. Recommend smoke control measures
to restrict smoke spread to the towers.
3. Evaluate the benefits of compartment
walls in the garage.
This study demonstrates how a smoke
management system in one area of a build-
ing can be a component of a zoned smoke
control system in another area of the build-
ing. The first objective was to be achieved
by a smoke management system. The
other two objectives used zoned smoke
control systems.
The extract rate was determined from
equations in NFPA 92B2 and confirmed
by the plume filling rate equation in ver-
sion 3.2 of FPETool, a computer program
prepared by the National Institute of Stan-
dards and Technology.3 The factors de-
termining the extract rate are as follows:
1. Heat release rate of the fire.
2. The clear layer or distance from the
fire to the smoke layer.
Heat Release Rate. A parking garage
fire is expected to involve a single auto-
mobile. The literature is replete with ar-
ticles confirming this fact based on fire
tests and actual field experience involv-
ing private passenger automobiles with
steel fuel tanks.
The heat release rate of three separate
automobiles was measured in a series of
tests performed in Finland.5 This analy-
sis uses the average heat release rate of
the three tests. The average peak heat
release rate is 1,420 Btu/s (1500 kW).
The clear layer height for these calcu-
lations is 6.6 ft (2 m), the approximate
height of a tall person plus a factor of
safety. The reasonable maximum height
of a person is generally considered to be
6 ft (1.8 m).
Applying the equations of NFPA 92B
for a smoke temperature of 165°F (74°C),
the automatic sprinkler temperature rating,
yields an extract rate of approximately 9,320
cfm (4.4 m3/s). FPETool yields approxi-
mately 9,110 cfm (4.3 m3/s) for the extract
rate, confirming the NFPA 92B value.
Figure 3: Schematic plan of a typical module response when alarm is received.
Figure 4: A schematic section view of a module in alarm.
D e c e m b e r 1 9 9 8
ASHRAE Journal
3 9
S M O K E C O N T R O L
This extract rate applies irrespective of
the compartment size. The normal ventila-
tion rate for the parking level is eight air
changes per hour. The fans providing ven-
tilation are activated by carbon monoxide
sensors. Ventilation will be arranged with
air supplied at each level on one end of the
garage and exhausted from each level on
the other side of the garage. The ventila-
tion rate to achieve eight air changes per
hour for the smallest compartment is ap-
proximately 75,400 cfm (35.6 m³/s).
The smallest compartment is be-
tween exterior walls and two-hour walls.
This is based on an area of approxi-
mately 47,600 ft² (4420 m²) and a ceiling
height of 9.8 ft (3 m). It is clear that the
normal ventilation rate has more than
adequate capacity for the previously
described fire scenario.
Restricting Smoke Spread. Smoke
spread from the parking garage to the tow-
ers can be restricted by maintaining an
adequate pressure difference across bar-
riers separating the towers from the park-
ing garage. NFPA 92A recommends a
minimum design pressure difference of
0.05 in. of water (12.5 Pa) for spaces pro-
tected by automatic sprinklers.
The pressure difference should be
positive on the towers’ side of the barrier
so that air flows from the towers to the
parking garage and thence to the exterior.
This will be accomplished by the afore-
mentioned parking garage extract system
and the barriers between the garage and
the towers, including shafts. Those
shafts consist of stairs and elevators, both
of which are pressurized to maintain a
minimum of 0.05 in. of water (12.5 Pa) posi-
tive pressure from the shaft to the con-
necting space. In addition, the elevators
and stairs are protected by vestibules at
all garage levels.
Next, the minimum airflow needed to
maintain 0.05 in. of water (12.5 Pa) leak-
ing from the towers through cracks and
small openings in the floor and around
the vestibule doors at the shafts needs
to be determined. Because the parking
garage system consists of exhausting air
from the garage, one must also consider
the leakage between compartments of the
garage.
The airflow rate can be estimated ac-
cording to the following “orifice” equa-
tion from the ASHRAE/SFPE manual.4
Q = Kƒ A (∆P)½
(1)
Q =
Volumetric flow rate through the
path, cfm (m³/s)
A =
Flow area (also called leakage
area), ft² (m²)
∆P = Pressure difference across path,
in. of water (Pa)
Kƒ = Coefficient
In this case, ∆P = 0.05 in. of water (12.5
Pa). A is the leakage area determined by
estimating the leakage rate through the
floor between the garage and the tower
and between the parking garage compart-
ments through the compartment walls.
Leakage through the walls to the earth is
assumed to be 0.
The leakage area for the walls was cal-
culated using leakage rate factors from
the aforementioned ASHRAE/SFPE
Manual as 8.9 ft² (0.83 m²).
Substituting in Equation 1 yields:
Q = 5,230 cfm (2.47 m³/s)
(2)
All levels within compartment walls
would constitute a single zone. This is
because there are openings at the
ramps between the parking garage lev-
els that would make restricting smoke
between levels impractical. Therefore,
it was proposed that the exhaust fans
on all levels within a zone operate and
that 50% of the supply fans operate
during smoke mode. The stair and el-
evator shaft pressurization fans would
also operate upon a fire signal from the
parking garage. Further, the compart-
ment doors would close in the affected
compartments. Ventilation in other ar-
eas would remain normal.
Under this arrangement the required
airflow rate of approximately 5,300 cfm
(2.5 m³/s) is less than the smoke required
extract rate of 9,320 cfm (4.4 m³/s) and
less than the planned ventilation rate of
eight air changes per hour 75,400 cfm
(35.6 m³/s). Based on this analysis, the
planned ventilation system should be
effective in restricting smoke spread
from the parking garage to the towers.
The compartment walls separating the
parking garage by tower area are intended
to restrict fire and smoke to the area gen-
erally related to the tower. The previous
calculations demonstrate that the normal
ventilation has adequate capacity to main-
tain the pressure difference between the
compartments adequately to restrict
smoke spread so long as the compartment
doors close. One can calculate the air-
flow needed to limit smoke spread through
the rolling door opening. That equation
is as follows:
Opposed airflow through opening:
v = 38[gH(Tƒ – T o)/(Tƒ)]½
(3)
Where:
v = air velocity, ft/min (m/s)
g = acceleration of gravity, 32.2 ft/s²
(9.8 m/s²)
H = height of the opening, ft (m)
Tƒ = temperature of heated smoke, °R
(°K)
Figure 5: Schematic plan of the atrium lobby building.
4 0
ASHRAE Journal
D e c e m b e r 1 9 9 8
T o = temperature of ambient air, °R (°K)
In this case, the opening is 29.5 ft wide by 8 ft high (9 m by
2.5 m). Tƒ is taken as the sprinkler operating temperature.
Substituting in the equation yields v = 533 fpm (6.06 mph)
(2.71 m/s). For the opening, this yields approximately 130,000
cfm (61 m³/s). Clearly, this exceeds the available capacity and
therefore is not practical. Furthermore, this is for a single open-
ing and there are six such openings in an internal compartment.
Therefore, the doors are needed to maintain the pressure differ-
ence to restrict smoke between the compartments.
Other Considerations
It is important that the objective of a smoke control system is
clearly established at the outset of the design and that concur-
rence of those objectives is achieved with all stake holders.
Smoke control systems depend on controlling the fire size.
In each of the cases described in this article, automatic sprin-
kler protection was used to achieve that goal. Other methods
such as occupancy controls can be used if credible procedures
assure limits on combustible load.
Simplicity of control and design enhances the likelihood that
the smoke control system will achieve its objective. Finally,
thorough testing of the system is needed to verify that the
completed system will function properly.
References
1. NFPA 92A-1996, Recommended practice for smoke-control sys-
tems. Quincy, Mass.: National Fire Protection Association.
2. NFPA 92B-1995, Guide for smoke management systems in malls,
atria, and large areas. Quincy, Mass.: National Fire Protection Asso-
ciation.
3. Deal, S. 1994. NISTIR 5486, Technical reference guide for FPETool
version 3.2. Gaithersburg, Md.: National Institute of Standards and
Technology.
4. Klote, J.H., and J.A. Milke. 1992. Design of Smoke Management
Systems. ASHRAE.
5. Mangs, J. and O. Keski-Rahkonen. 1994. “Characteristics of the
fire behaviour of a burning passenger car. Part I: car fire experiments.”
Fire Safety Journal 23:17–35.

ASHRAE JOURNAL
or more than ten years, trade journals have been flooded with
interesting articles discussing problems with and solutions for vari-
able flow air and water systems. Many issues are encompassed:
how to control the related variable speed drives; where to locate the dif-
ferential pressure (DP) control sensor; and whether or not to use balanc-
ing valves. The best control solutions require a good knowledge of all
three issues.
Variable Flow—
A Control Engineer’s Perspective
By Harris Bynum
Member ASHRAE
and
Ed Merwin
ping at the design operating point. The
curve, based upon fan/pump Law 2, is a
square root derivation of the operating
point, such that at one-half design flow
the head is one-fourth the design head,
etc. A primary use of a design system
curve is in the selection of fans and
pumps.
• Proportional flow: Variable flow ap-
plications when the percent of full load flow
through each load is equal, i.e., if flow is
80% through one load, the flow through all
loads is 80%.
• Diverse flow: Variable flow appli-
cations when the percent of full load
flow through each load is not equal.
All loads may not be in operation, or
the flow through each load is not pro-
portional. In HVAC applications, other
than at full load, some form of diverse
loading usually exists.
Laws
When related to typical HVAC sys-
tems, the fan and pump laws basically
state that (fan and pump Law 1) flow is
proportional to shaft rotational speed,
(fan and pump Law 2) system resistance
head is proportional to the square of shaft
rotational speed (and flow) and (fan and
pump Law 3) power is proportional to the
About the Authors
Harris Bynum
Harris Bynum
Harris Bynum
Harris Bynum
Harris Bynum retired from Honeywell in June
1997 after 36 years. His last position was national
manager of control applications.
Ed Merwin
Ed Merwin
Ed Merwin
Ed Merwin
Ed Merwin is a member of Honeywell’s Engineer-
ing Specifier Coverage Team.
The authors believe that air and water
flow traditional “system curves,” fan laws
and pump affinity laws can be mislead-
ing when related to HVAC-type variable
flow systems. This article discusses
where traditional system curves, fan laws
and pump affinity laws are relevant;
shows why they can be misleading when
applied to HVAC-type variable flow sys-
tems; and offers solutions.
Definitions
Fan and pump laws are well defined in
the 1991 ASHRAE Terminology of
HVAC&R (see Laws later). The terms
“operating point” and “system curve”
are not defined. The following definitions
are offered here:
• Design operating point: The fan
and pump head required to produce de-
sign flow within HVAC systems (such as
600 gpm [38 L/s] at 48 ft [143 kPa] head).
• Actual operating point: The point
on a pump or fan curve at which the sys-
tem operates.
• Design system curve: A curve start-
ing at zero flow and zero head and stop-
cube of shaft rotational speed (and flow).
Law 3 is a simple derivation of Laws 1
and 2; i.e., work (power) is proportional
to the weight-per-minute of the media
handled times the head. If, at half flow,
the head is one-fourth of the full flow
head (fan and pump Law 2), the power is
one-eighth (¼ × ½) of the full flow power.
A system upon which the design sys-
tem curve and fan and pump laws are
based is shown in the 1996 ASHRAE
Handbook—Systems and Equipment,
Page 18.6 and 18.7 (Figure 1).
This is a static system and may include
coils, outlets, balancing dampers, etc.; but
if dynamic elements such as modulating
control dampers exist, movement of those
dynamic devices create a new system curve
and the laws based upon the design sys-
tem curve no longer apply.
In a VAV system, the fan will not re-
quire one-eighth power at half flow be-
cause control dampers moved (which
causes a change in the system resistance)
to reduce the flow.
In a typical variable flow hydronic
HVAC system, the pump will not require
one-eighth power at half flow because
control valves moved to reduce the flow.
An example where the fan laws would
relate would be a single zone AHU where
a space thermostat controls the fan speed
with no change to the system resistance
characteristics, Figure 2.
Curves/Laws Related to HVAC
The fan design system curve would
be the actual system curve (and the ac-
tual operating point curve) if the fan
F

Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper
form without permission of ASHRAE.

A S H R A E J o u r n a l
2 7
V A R I A B L E F L O W
speed was varied with all VAV box dampers fixed at their design
flow position. The pump design system curve would be the
actual system curve (and the actual operating point curve) if
the pump speed was varied with all control valves full open. We
assume balancing valves are set, or all control valves are at
their design flow position if balancing valves are not used
Since, in HVAC applications, the cause of flow variations is
the change of damper and valve positions (and the resultant
change in system resistance), design system curves and de-
sign-based laws do not apply at part load.
Figure 3 is a simple direct return water system with design
flows and design pressure drops noted. In this example, bal-
ancing valves are included. The balancing valve at AHU 1 is
set to take a 12 ft (36 kPa) drop at the design condition.
Figure 4 shows the resultant design system curve and de-
sign operating point for the Figure 3 system. The dotted line
curve on Figure 4 shows approximately what the design sys-
tem curve would look like with the pump selected and all bal-
ancing valves full open. In reality this dotted curve would be
lower because the selected coils drops would probably be less
than 8 ft (24 kPa) and the selected control valves drops would
probably be less than 8 ft (24 kPa).
Prior to further system analysis, the position and setpoint
of the DP sensor/controller must be determined (Figure 3).
Without balancing valves and with a fixed DP setpoint, the
sensor should be positioned across AHU 6 (position 3) and
set for 16 ft (48 kPa). Each AHU requires 16 ft (48 kPa) at its full
load. If AHU 6 has 16 ft (48 kPa), all others have at least 16 ft (48
kPa) at all cases of diverse loadings.
With balancing valves. With balancing valves and with a
fixed DP setpoint and if diverse loading (AHUs running at
different loadings or all AHUs not running) must be accommo-
dated, AHU 1 must always have a 28 ft (84 kPa) DP. The logical
DP sensor position is therefore across AHU 1 (Position 2). The
setpoint is 28 ft (84 kPa). If the DP sensor is placed across AHU
6, the setpoint must still be 28 ft (84 kPa) to accommodate AHU
1 running alone (Saturday) and more wiring may be required.
Figure 5 shows operating points for this system with the DP
sensor across AHU 1 and AHU 6 at proportional and various
diverse loadings. Figure 5 shows that placing the control sen-
sor across AHU 1 not only may require less wiring, but also
allows the pump to run at lower speeds, thus requiring less
energy. With proportional loading and the DP sensor across
AHU 6 set for 28 ft (84 kPa), the pump runs at full speed until
75% flow is reached.
Determining operating points at partial flow requires the
flow demands of each AHU, the head losses for each system
segment and the DP sensor location and setpoint.
The actual pump head equals the DP controller setpoint
plus the sum of all the piping losses between the pump and the
DP sensor.
Operating Point Calculation
If AHUs 4, 5 and 6 were operating at design conditions,
other AHUs were off, balancing valves were in and set and the
DP sensor was at Location 3 (Figure 3) and set for the re-
quired 28 ft (85 kPa) drop, the operating point would be as
noted by the circle noted “A” of Figure 5. The head loss
within each static piping segment is calculated using pump
Law 2. For the supply piping segment between AHUs 1 and 2,
the design loss is 1 ft (3 kPa), the design flow is 1000 gpm (63
L/s), the actual flow is 600 gpm (38 L/s) and the actual loss is
0.36 ft (600/1000 squared times 1 ft) (1 kPa [38/63 squared times
3 kPa]). Calculating and adding the losses for each piping seg-
ment to and from AHU 6 and adding the 28 ft (84 kPa) DP
controller setpoint at AHU 6 gives an operating point of 40 ft
(120 kPa) head at 600 gpm (38 L/s).
Without balancing valves, the calculations would be identi-
cal except the setpoint at AHU 6 would be 16 ft (48 kPa) and the
pump head would be 28 ft (84 kPa) (point “A” on Figure 6).
Without balancing valves. Figure 6 shows various operat-
ing points if balancing valves were omitted, the DP sensor was
located at AHU 6 and the setpoint was fixed at 16 ft (48 kPa).
If, in this system with no balancing valves, all control valves
positioned full open and the pump ran at full speed, the pump
would put out about 1,280 gpm (80.5 L/s) at 33.5 ft (100.5 kPa)
head and the flows through AHUs 1–6 would be 246, 235, 233,
202, 192 and 182 gpm (15.5, 14.8, 14.7, 12.7, 12.1 and 11.5 L/s)
respectively.
In both cases, the head difference between the design sys-
tem curve and the operating point must be managed by the
control valves, 8 ft (24 kPa) at design, 28 ft (84 kPa) (Figure 5)
or 16 ft (48 kPa) (Figure 6) at zero flow and varying up and
down in between.
Figure 1: System upon which fan laws apply.
Figure 2: HVAC system upon which fan laws apply.
2 8
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
Since, in all cases, the actual operating point
is only on the design system curve at design
load and zero load, the design system curve
cannot be used to calculate part load energy
savings with variable flow systems; and the
design system curve cannot be used to deter-
mine when to start/stop lag pumps when paral-
lel pumping exists.
Solutions
Much has been written lately about what
BACnet™ and Echelon communication proto-
cols do for Direct Digital Control (DDC) moni-
toring and operation and little has been written
about what they can do for control. The great-
est difference between pneumatic control and
DDC is that every DDC controller has access to
any (within reason) system data needed for
good control.
The design system curve shown on Figures
4, 5 and 6 is the objective (perfect world) oper-
ating point curve for variable flow systems. To
accomplish this objective, the following condi-
tions must be met (and all pump laws will be
complied with):
1. All AHUs on.
2. Proportional loading.
3. Pump speed controller DP setpoint low-
ered at part load as required to keep most de-
manding control valve (with proportional load-
ing, all valves demand equally) full open.
The best news is that with this (perfect
world) scenario, the curve is the same with and
without balancing valves and it does not mat-
ter where the DP sensor is. In reality:
1. 90% of the time, all AHUs are usually on.
2. During this time, diverse (not proportional)
Figure 4: Design system curve.
Figure 3: Example of a water system.
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
2 9
V A R I A B L E F L O W
loading exists at part load. Dependent upon the nature and
magnitude of this diversity, the actual operating point will al-
ways be above the design system curve when the loads are
under control.
3. To attempt to keep the most demanding valve full open
may be too ambitious for stable control. Anytime pump speed
varies, flow through all loads varies and temperature con-
trols must correct this variation and return to stability. It
may be desirable to have the system load fast to recover
from inadequate flow and to unload slower for increased
control loop stability.
A better control concept might be:
• Anytime all control valves are at a position less than 90%
open, decrement the DP setpoint 0.6 ft (1.8 kPa) every 10 min-
utes down to a minimum of 7 ft (21 kPa).
• Anytime any control valve is at a position greater than
96% open, increment the DP setpoint 0.8 ft (2.4 kPa) every
eight minutes up to a maximum of 28 ft (84 kPa) (dependent
upon where the DP sensor is located).
An equal control concept is to vary the pump speed di-
rectly (in lieu of varying the DP controller setpoint) in order to
keep the most demanding valve between 90 and 96% open.
This concept would always run the pump at its maximum speed
(rather than at a controlled pressure value) anytime any valve
(even if only one AHU is in operation) is greater than 96%
open. A DP sensor at AHU 1 or at the pump is recommended
for monitoring and trending during the tuning process.
All values are adjustable and may be field “tuned” to assure
there is enough hysterisis to maintain stable pump speed and
AHU temperature control. The operating point varies from the
design system curve due to diverse loading and the incre-
ment/decrement values and timings.
These control concepts obviously get the pump horsepower
down to a level expected for variable speed pumps. Before
specifying these concepts make sure the commissioning party
is familiar with all the above HVAC data, the difference be-
tween linear and equal percentage control valves and the speed
of response of the AHU temperature control loops (fast dis-
charge air temperature control of VAV AHUs or slow space
temperature control of single zone AHUs?).
If pneumatic valve actuators are used, the relationship be-
tween the DDC internal control output value (usually 0 to 100)
and the pneumatically operated control valve position must be
considered. Pneumatic pilot positioners are recommended to
accommodate varying water line pressures. Field adjustable
software conditioning of the % open value is recommended to
allow calibration to take care of spring range or pilot positioner
setting errors (a one psi error could produce a calculation valve
Figure 6: Operating points, no balancing valves and fixed
setpoints.
Figure 5: Operating points, with balancing valves and fixed
setpoints.
3 0
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
position error as great as 25%) and pneumatic transducer er-
rors. Physical stem position transmitters are not recommended.
Summary
•
The design system curve cannot be used to calculate
part load energy savings with variable flow systems.
•
The design system curve cannot be used to determine
when to start/stop lag pumps when parallel pumping
exists.
•
In variable flow HVAC systems, operating points are only
on the design system curve at design flow and at zero
flow.
•
The DP control sensor location and setpoint should vary
dependent upon whether or not balancing valves are
used.
•
DP reset based upon AHU load demands solves many
issues about DP sensor location and setpoint and the
effect of balancing valves on variable flow system op-
eration.
In conclusion, the authors have a mild preference for in-
stalling balancing valves and for using one of the above con-
trol concepts that consider control valve positions. Consider-
ing control valve positions and with the omission of balancing
valves, designers must consider the adverse effects of poor

owner/operator setpoint discipline (if, to resolve an occupant
complaint, the discharge air temperature control setpoint is
lowered from a design of 55°F (12.8°C) to 50°F (10°C), the par-
ticular AHU control valve will remain open much of the time,
thus interfering with these enhanced concepts).

ASHRAE JOURNAL
rior to the late 1800s, rotating shaft power was limited mainly
to trains and ships. There were wind and water mills for pump-
ing water and grinding grains and some steam-driven water
pumps and ventilation fans. However, few people lived in windy
regions or near fast-moving streams. Some ventilation fan de-
signs for mine shafts were powered by man, wind or water
pressure.
Early Refrigeration
Refrigeration was provided by ice from frozen ponds and
rivers in the more northern climates. This ice was harvested and
stored in winter and then sold during warmer weather. Ice trade
became a major industry in the United States. Frederic Tudor of
Boston started shipping ice to warmer climates in 1815. He be-
came known in 1864 as the “Ice King of the World,” and Boston
ice was shipped to every major seaport in South America, the
Far East, China, the Philippines, India and Australia.1
As pollution around the cities increased, particularly in the
form of sewage run-off, the market emerged for artificial ice. Use
of steam boilers to drive the thermodynamic process cycle and
produce ice was growing rapidly. Daniel L. Holden and J. An-
drew Muhl built and operated mechanical refrigeration plants
in 1865 and 1867 in San Antonio, Texas. Holden designed im-
provements to the Carré absorption units of Paris, which made
San Antonio in 1867 and New Orleans in 1869 the southern
regional leaders in making artificial ice2 (see Figure 1).
The areas that could harvest “natural” ice tried to convince
people that the “artificial” ice was somehow unnatural and to
be avoided. But the areas that were producing manufactured
ice were able to convince people that “natural” ice was con-
By Bruce L. Flaniken, P.E.
Member ASHRAE
P
This is the second article in our special series. As we ap-
proach the year 2000, these articles commemorate a cen-
tury of innovation in the HVAC&R arts and sciences and
salute the start of a new millennium, which will undoubtedly
bring further progress.
taminated. As costs came into line with the natural ice costs,
producers of artificial ice were able to make significant inroads
into the northern monopoly on ice.3
Ice delivery was hard work. Imagine lugging around a 300 to
400 lb (140 to 180 kg) block of ice on your shoulder like the
young man above!4 By 1909, ice was produced in approximately
2,000 refrigeration plants.5
Heating: From Stoves to Steam
Heating was provided by wood-burning fireplaces, which
were later replaced with cast-iron radiant stoves that burned
wood and/or coal. As cities became more populated, fire con-
trol became much more important. Fireproof construction was
mandated by some city codes after several large and costly
fires destroyed central districts.
Steam boilers became a standard for heating buildings. Cen-
tral district steam plants that sold steam to buildings were de-
Application of Electric Power
In HVAC&R Systems
From Blocks of Pond Ice to Hermetic Motors
Application of Electric Power
In HVAC&R Systems
About the Author
Bruce L. Flaniken, P.E., heads the mechanical department
for Callins, Haggard & Associates Consulting Mechanical,
Electrical & Plumbing Engineers, Corpus Christi, Texas. He
is a past chapter historian of ASHRAE Region 8.
The First Century of Air Conditioning
Application of Electric Power
In HVAC&R Systems
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, January 1999. © Copyright 1999 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes
only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
3 8
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
veloped. The steam boilers were large and
expensive to operate and required super-
vision and maintenance. Coal-handling
bunkers and stokers also constituted a
portion of the overhead.
As steam-driven engines developed
as a reliable means of rotating shaft power
and heat sources, they began to be used
as the power source for refrigeration and
ventilation systems (see Figure 2). Of the
steam engines used for this purpose in
1914, more than 90% exceeded 15 million
calories/hour (63 MJ).
Facilities that had a large steam
boiler used the steam to provide for
ventilation with large, low rpm centrifu-
gal fans because they could convert
the steam to rotary shaft work. Smaller,
steam-driven piston engines were de-
veloped for the purchasers of central
district steam to convert steam to ro-
tary shaft horsepower for all manner of
equipment (see Figure 3).
Wood- and coal-burning steam-
driven vehicles were developed in the
late 1890s. An experienced operator
could achieve sustained vehicle move-
ment or sustained rotation of the steam-
driven power take-off wheel from a cold
start after about 1 ½ to 2 hours of hard
work. The engines were quite power-
ful, but they weighed several tons. The
Stanley Steamer automobile was fairly
impressive in appearance, size and
speed.
Early Ventilation
In other areas, ventilation was almost
non-existent. Natural ventilation was con-
trolled by the building orientation and
placement of windows to catch the pre-
vailing breezes. High ceilings and large,
open central staircases with ventilated
domes provided some assistance to gra-
vity and Mother Nature. Ventilation did
not truly take off until after the mid-1880s,
when the use of steam and electricity had
spread. An exception was a kerosene-
powered fan sold by the Whirlwind Fan
Figure 1: Holden’s improved ice plant. The structure in the center is a condenser with a “zig-
zag” heat exchanger (from Scientific American, May 22, 1880, p. 322).
Figure 2: Early steam fan from B. F. Sturtevant Co. (from Heating and Ventilation, June
1895, p. xvii).
Company. The sales slogan for this unit
boasted, “It will give you greater effi-
ciency and enable you to do more and
better work.”
The electrical industry was growing
rapidly. New patents and ideas for resi-
dential electrical appliances were being
advertised as the best or most natural way
of life. The world was rather unsophisti-
cated and uneducated, but some of the
biggest changes to affect humankind
were taking place. The development of
steam and electric-powered equipment
surged ahead due to better understand-
ing of electrical, mechanical and thermo-
dynamic principles.
Direct or Alternating Current?
The competition between direct cur-
rent (DC) and alternating current (AC)
was fierce. Thomas Edison was a prime
proponent of DC power because of his
involvement in the design and marketing
of direct current lighting, power distribu-
tion and motors. He felt that AC power
was only good for electrocutions. DC
power did provide a flicker-free light and
could vary the speed of a motor by vary-
ing the voltage. Its major drawback, how-
ever, was its inability to transmit via wire
over long distances.
George Westinghouse, Jr. was the prin-
cipal proponent of AC power. The flicker
problem with incandescent lighting was
resolved by using more cycles per sec-
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
3 9
H I S T O R Y
ond, ranging from 133½ to 25 cycles per
second.6 The various voltages and cycles
for AC power transmission were being
tested, and the ones that worked best
were retained.
By 1914, most frequencies above 60
cycles per second had been abandoned,
but it took another 20 to 25 years for 60
cycles per second power to become the
standard in the United States. Some other
countries settled on 50 cycles per second.
Generally, the only electrical load of a
central power generator plant was the
street lighting at night in the central busi-
ness district. Some single-phase, induc-
tion-type motors with brushes and com-
mutators became popular, especially
among small industries in suburban
towns.
Use of small electric fans, curling irons,
irons for laundry, washing machines and
hair dryers increased the use of electric
power, particularly during the idle day-
time period when the generators were
under-utilized.7,8 This trend has reversed
in the present day. Modern utility com-
panies now offer premiums to shift the
daytime power consumption to evening
hours because their generating capacity
is underutilized at night.
Single-phase induction and synchro-
nous motors for AC power were popular
with the public (see Figure 4). To prop-
erly start synchronous motors, however,
required experience.9 The power utility
companies liked the synchronous motors
because they could be operated with a
1.0 or higher leading power factor to help
correct the lagging power factor of the
utility distribution system.10 Automatic
starters were developed later. Because it
was difficult to control the motor speed,
almost all the applications used belt-drive
connections to drive the machinery.
Advances in Motor Technology
The physicist and inventor Nikola
Tesla began working on AC electrical de-
signs as a college student in Croatia dur-
ing the 1870s. At this time, it was thought
impossible to construct a motor without
a commutator. Tesla worked as a tele-
phone engineer before quitting to develop
in his mind a complete alternating current
power system with generators, transform-
ers and motors. He then took a job as an
Figure 4: Precursor to open drip-proof motors (from Engineering Review, Feb. 1902,
p. xxi).
electrical engineer, but no one was inter-
ested in his seemingly incredible ideas.
He developed working models of genera-
tors and motors while on a long-term as-
signment in Strasbourg, then part of Ger-
many. Tesla moved to the United States
in 1887, at age 31, where he filed for 25
patents covering virtually the entire field
of alternating current generation, distri-
bution and polyphase, brushless induc-
tion AC motors. Shortly after, he sold his
patents to George Westinghouse, who
implemented widespread use of alternat-
ing current.11
Prior to 1891, there had been no dem-
onstration of electrical transmission of any
considerable amount of power over any
considerable distance. A demonstration
of the capability to transmit 100 hp (75
kW) of AC electricity a distance of 106
miles (171 km) from Lauffen to Frankfurt
in Germany was staged in 1891 for a tech-
nical exhibition. The wye-connected,
three-phase generators were wound for
55 volts and driven by hydraulic turbines.
A transformer increased the voltage to
approximately 30,000 volts.
The three copper conductors were
only 4 mm (0.16 in.) in diameter each. The
incoming power was stepped-down to
drive a 60-hp (45-kW), four-pole poly-
phase induction motor at a speed of 1,200
rpm and supplied power for incandescent
lights and some other small motors.12
The AC polyphase motor became
more popular. The HVAC&R industry was
able to capitalize on its popularity as the
Figure 3: Two single-acting duplex
compressors and steam engine direct
connected (from the Victor Ice and
Refrigerating Machines, 1899.
Stilwell-Bierce & Smith-Vaile Co.,
Dayton, Ohio).
4 0
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
Figure 5: Early electric-drive refrigeration compressor (from Ice and Refrigeration, Aug.
1895, p. 88).
polyphase motor spread to small, com-
mercial applications. Electric power costs
in 1897 averaged about $0.10/kWh.13 The
price dropped to approximately $0.03/
kWh in 1950 and is now approaching
$0.10/kWh again.
Fans and pumps continued to be di-
rect-drive with several available rpm con-
figurations. By 1925, the electric motor
was the preferred method of drive, pro-
viding 62%, vs. 32% for steam engine
drives, while diesel and gas engines were
used 4% and 2% of the time, respectively.3
As refrigeration systems that were pow-
ered by electrical motors became avail-
able (see Figure 5) and the electrical in-
frastructure developed, the use of natu-
ral and artificial ice for refrigerating effect
was discontinued. This was a welcome
development for merchants and consum-
ers who had suffered the messiness of
the ice refrigeration systems.14 The Ice
Refrigeration Blue Book listed 100 dif-
ferent refrigeration equipment options in
1909, which increased to more than 260 in
1911.15
The use of hermetic motors evolved in
the early 1930s, driven by the competi-
tion between General Electric, Frigidaire,
Servel and Westinghouse to capture the
greatest portion of the emerging home
refrigerator market and the expanding
commercial refrigeration market. West-
inghouse applied a variant of the hermetic
refrigeration compressor to air condition-
ing by making a console-style room air
conditioner in 1933.16
Conclusion
Comfort air conditioning has expanded
from the wealthy residential and upscale
commercial establishments to become a
References

Heat & Cold—Mastering the Great Indoors,

tion and ice making from a central station point

8. Kimball, F. 1907. “The utility of the single
phase motor.” General Electric Review

tors.” General Electric Review 35(4):455–464.
11. Nagengast. B. 1982–83. Pioneers in Elec-
trical Research, 1–2. ASHRAE Research Pam-
phlet.

refrigeration systems, Part 3.” ASHRAE Jour-

From Ice and Refrigeration, Aug. 1905, p. 55.
virtual necessity in contemporary times.
It is possible in many areas to go from an
air-conditioned home to an air-condi-
tioned office in an air-conditioned car
without experiencing more than a few mo-
ments of discomfort from the ambient
outdoor temperature and humidity. And
modern refrigerators require no one to
carry hundreds of pounds of ice up rick-
ety ladders propped precariously against
the side of a building.

ASHRAE JOURNAL
Steam tracing is usually accomplished
by running steam lines in direct contact
with the piping or vessels to be heated.
The entire assembly is then insulated to
direct the heat into the process fluid.
Many technical articles have been writ-
ten concerning steam tracing along with
their associated heat loss equations and
graphs. In the author’s experience,
younger engineers, maintenance person-
nel, pipe fitters and others who steam trace
do not always use effective methods for
winterization and process temperature
maintenance. This article presents some
of the basics in this art.
Applications
Process applications for tracing gen-
erally revolve around three main areas of
concern: freeze protection, product qual-
ity and ease of transport. Outdoor ex-
posed water lines where water may be-
come stationary and freeze during peri-
ods of low usage can lead to costly re-
placement of piping, as well as downtime
for the areas of the plant they service. It
is important to keep the water above the
freezing point. However, excess heating
is a waste of energy and money.
Concentrated aqueous solutions such
as sodium hydroxide present a two-fold
problem: they will solidify at an elevated
temperature (relative to water), yet excess
heating can lead to product degradation
and increased pipeline corrosion. This
situation exists in the paper industry.
Thus, temperature control on steam trac-
ing takes on more significance than just
energy conservation.
Self-acting temperature control
valves are one method of temperature
control. Other methods exist; however,
these are more expensive than the self-
operating units. These methods in-
clude electric/pneumatic, all pneumatic
or all electric temperature control. Such
systems include a positioner, a setpoint
controller, a temperature sensor and a
power supply.
We have all heard the comment: “It’s
as thick as molasses in January.” Yet, en-
gineers in the process industry frequently
are asked to move such materials. The
most common response is to heat it, be-
cause most materials become less viscous
as their temperature is increased. The en-
ergy used in heating will be more than
compensated by using less power to
move the material around, as well as al-
lowing the use of smaller, cheaper pumps.
One often-overlooked situation is that
high viscosity materials do not transmit
heat well when they are cold. Thermal
currents of warmer material through the
cooler portion are slow to form in vis-
cous substances and the lack of heat
transmission gives rise to localized over-
heating. This can arise during a restart
Practical Tips for Operation
Effective Steam Tracing
By Thomas M. Kenny
About the Author
Thomas M. K
Thomas M. K
Thomas M. K
Thomas M. K
Thomas M. Kenny
enny
enny
enny
enny, chairman of Ogontz Corpora-
tion, Willow Grove, Pa., holds three patents on tem-
perature actuated valves. He has a bachelor’s de-
gree in mechancial engineering from Villanova Uni-
versity.
following a cold weather shutdown
where the tracing heat is considered “un-
necessary” during the shutdown.
Sizes and Lengths
The most common diameters of steam
tracing are 3/8 in. (10 mm) and 1/2 in. (13 mm)
OD copper or stainless steel tubing. Diam-
eters of 5/8 in. (16 mm) and 3/4 in. (19 mm)
OD tubing have been used; however, this
entails greater expense. Tubing that is 3/8
in. (10 mm) is more easily plugged by sedi-
ment and/or debris, so it is less frequently
used. Type K and Type L are popular cop-
per tubing materials. Copper is preferred
for its heat transfer characteristics, while
stainless steel displays better resistance in
corrosive environments.
Runs
Most steam tracing has a run length
between its supply valve and terminat-
ing steam trap of 75 ft to 150 ft (23 m to 46
m). This length is influenced by the con-
figuration of the tracing, the supply header
locations and the location suitable to
traps and return lines. Horizontal runs of
process pipe can have tracers on the
sides (see Figure 1) or at the bottom,
depending on the heat transfer load. In
addition, thermal transfer cement, a graph-
ite-filled medium, is used to enhance heat
transfer from the tracer to the process pipe
(bonding).
Many times the tracing can develop a
droop between bands holding it to the
process line. These should be avoided
because the condensate formed by the
team tracing is the application of steam heat to piping and vessels
for the purpose of maintaining desired process temperatures, freeze
protection, viscosity control or to obtain a temperature control point
within a fluid for its chemical interaction in a process.
S
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, January 1999. © Copyright 1999 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper
form without permission of ASHRAE.
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
4 3
S T E A M
heat transfer process can form puddles
in the drooped lengths. These puddles
can impede steam flow as well as freeze
during outages and times when a tem-
perature control valve may be closed.
Vertical runs can pose special prob-
lems where the steam is flowing uphill
while the condensate formed is pulled
downhill by gravity. Such a case exists
where process lines must be elevated
to clear a roadway. At this point, a con-
densate-separating chamber should be
installed. The inlet tracer comes in hori-
zontally and leaves from the top of the
chamber. A steam trap is connected to
the bottom “drain” port, allowing the
removal of condensate. As the pipe and
tracer drop to a normal height after
bridging over the roadway, a second
steam trap may be installed as an op-
tional device. It is not mandatory in the
vertical drop situation since the con-
densate and steam are co-current.
Insulation
Process lines are routinely insulated to
conserve the energy required to maintain
the desired temperature. Tracers are in-
cluded inside the insulation, requiring the
insulation to be stepped up in ID to ac-
commodate the tracer. Where the process
pipe has a flange, tracers are frequently
brought outside the OD of insulation and
a joint is installed in the tracer. While this
leads to extra costs in installation, it
makes replacement of a pipe section
easier in the future should repairs or al-
terations be necessary.
Insulation thickness is usually opti-
mized as a balance between energy loss
and cost of insulation. Thicker insulation
conserves energy but costs more for ma-
terial and takes more time to install. Fi-
berglass or calcium silicate are the most
common insulation materials. Other ma-
terials are used depending on process
temperature (see Table 1).
It should be remembered that steam
temperature is necessarily hotter than
the process maintenance temperature
and that all materials should be quali-
fied for the steam temperature. An outer
cover of aluminum or stainless steel
provides weatherproofing. The cover
should be installed to prevent rain or
moisture from ruining the insulation’s
heat loss resistance.
Control Valves
In all three of these application cat-
egories, an automatic temperature sens-
ing control valve can be of great utility.
By sensing the appropriate relevant tem-
perature (ambient atmosphere, surface
wall or material insertion), the valve can
judiciously regulate when and how much
steam should be supplied to the traced
process line. It can avoid overheating as
well as set-up or freezing. Its automatic
operation replaces the manual setting of
steam valves. Avoiding overlooking an
obscure valve and reducing maintenance
are additional benefits.
Modulating temperature control
valves can reduce shocks on the steam
system by opening and closing slowly.
Abrupt pressure changes, water hammer
and thermal shock are reduced.
Traps
On the other end of the tracer, the type
of steam trap used is significant. Where
temperature control valves are used, it
is predictable that they will be in a closed
position when the tracing duty is satis-
fied. This means that the pressure driv-
ing condensate through a trap will de-
crease to zero. Hence, traps should be
selected that do not require pressure to
operate or are classified as free drain-
ing. An example of this is a temperature
sensing or thermostatic trap. In addition,
it must be oriented in a free draining po-
sition. Failure to select and install the
proper trap will plug the tracer line and
ultimately render the tracer useless.
Vacuum Breaker
Just after the control valve closes, the
steam in the tracer will condense to water
at a much lower specific volume (com-
pare the specific volume of 50 psig [345
kPa] saturated steam 7 f3/lb [109 kg/m3] to
Table 1: Thermal conductivity typical of insulation materials, Btu·in. (h·ft²·°F).
Table 2: Low-pressure steam generally
is used for steam tracing.
50 psig [345 kPa] condensate 0.02 f3/lb
[0.27 kg/m3]). This process creates a
vacuum in the tracer. To eliminate the si-
phon effect, a vacuum breaker installed
after the control valve lets air in to re-
place the volume formerly filled with
steam. Thus, condensate is able to drain
freely through the trap. It is not held back
by the vacuum created at the valve end
of the tracer. The siphon effect is broken
and the condensate drains out.
Supply Line Trap
The steam supply line should have a
trap immediately before the temperature
control valve. On branch lines, which are
distant from the steam header, or in cold
climates, a trap on the supply line helps
to prevent freezing in the steam line as
well as insure that dry steam is supplied
to the valve and tracer.
Steam Pressure
The choice of steam to be used for
tracing must be consistent with the tem-
perature to be maintained in the process
line. Generally, low pressure steam (less
than 100 psig [690 kPa]) (see Table 2) is
used unless maintenance temperature re-
quirements are very high. Tracing duty

5. Henry, K. June 1986. Introduction to Heat Tracing. p. 1. Cold Re-


makes use of the heat liberated in the conden-
sation of steam. Low-pressure steam is pre-
ferred because it actually has a higher amount
of heat liberated through the condensation pro-
cess per pound of steam used. It also is cheaper
to produce.
Subcooling Traps
Subcooling traps, which release condensate
only after it has cooled well below the satura-
tion temperature, can enhance the energy effi-
ciency of tracer systems by allowing sensible
heat to also contribute to the heating duty.
Normally, saturated steam pressure is used
for steam tracing (winterization), varying from
input pressures of 15 psig to 125 psig (100 kPa
to 860 kPa), depending upon the temperature
requirements of a process or the utility lines
being traced. Usually steam supply manifolds
are used with a manual valve controlling the
feed from the steam header to the steam supply manifold.
Generally, the manifold will consist of a 1 in. (25 mm) or 2 in.
(50 mm) header and will handle from three to 20 trace lines,
depending upon the applied pressure. Each trace line coming
from the supply manifold should have its own isolation valve,
which is in turn connected to the trace line and may be either
3/8 in. (10 mm) or 0.5 in. (13 mm) copper tubing.
Care should be taken in regard to the location of the trace
lines so the manual stop valves will not interfere with one an-
other while still providing a compact arrangement. All trace
lines should have a strainer installed with a blow-down valve
ahead of the steam trap at the end of the run.
Sufficient care should be taken when running the trace lines
to insure there are no pockets to collect condensate and that
the condensate has a free fall or pitch from the trace header to
the condensate trap at the far end of the trace line. All steam
trace tubing bends should be made using a tube bender to pre-
vent crimping the tubing. Tracers should be secured to the pro-
cess piping with banding installed 12 in. to 18 in. (300 mm to
460 mm) apart.
Tracing should be banded on the bottom of the line being
traced (see Figure 1); however, if shoes for linear expansion
are provided, the tracer should be located near the web of the
shoe, as close to the center of the pipe as possible.
When heat transfer cement (bonding) is required, tracers
with bonding should be placed along the top of the pipe to aid
in the installation procedures. It is normal practice to provide
an expansion loop wherever a union is installed or where the
tracer interferes with flanges. The union should be located
outside the insulation material.
Where required, heat transfer cement should be utilized and
applied according to the manufacturer’s instructions. All valves,
wraps and strainers should be individually tagged using stain-
less steel wire for future reference.
Bibliography
1. Bilbro, J. and J. Levines. 1969. “Electric heat tracing—state of the

ASHRAE JOURNAL
his article discusses how com-
putational fluid dynamics
(CFD) might be applied to
HVAC&R projects, explains
the steps involved with completing a
CFD project using commercial software
packages, and illustrates the cost and la-
bor requirements necessary to implement
CFD analysis. The ideas in this article are
targeted to consulting engineers, and at-
tempt to convey that CFD, a tool that has
been reserved mainly for researchers and
manufacturers with research and devel-
opment budgets, is ready for use as an
HVAC&R consulting engineering tool.
Advantages of CFD
Complex finite element analysis (FEA)
is routinely performed for machine and
structural analysis. It is probable that en-
gineers involved in the manufacturing of
complex machinery cannot envision a
product design process without optimiz-
ing the design of components through
FEA. Many HVAC&R engineers have
been exposed to FEA in heat transfer
course work where the temperature dis-
tribution through a slab or a rod was ex-
amined. HVAC&R engineers may also
have been exposed to FEA during a col-
lege level fluids lab; in this case FEA would
be referred to as computational fluid dy-
namics (CFD). However, it is unlikely that
a complex, 3-D CFD problem specific to
HVAC&R was used.
A serious commitment is required for a
CFD in the Real World
consulting firm to begin offering CFD
analysis. To obtain meaningful CFD so-
lutions, an engineer must be familiar with
the use of complex software, must have
knowledge of fluid mechanics and the
ability to relate the results to real world
situations. Some CFD software develop-
ers are involved with providing CFD so-
lutions as a service, and might be avail-
able to work as an experienced partner
for some projects. The advantage of CFD
is that a comprehensive analysis can be
applied to several design alternatives in
a cost-effective manner.
Common Applications for CFD
CFD is traditionally thought of as a
tool for aerodynamic research. However,
CFD also has been applied to analyze the
combustion process through boilers,
blood flow through vein and artery sur-
gery sites, chemical mixing vessels, auto-
mobile heating system ductwork, passen-
ger-cabin ventilation for jumbo jetliners,
Formula 1 race cars and numerous other
applications. Many of these applications
included verification of the CFD model
with smoke tests, calibration of the model
against physical prototypes or verifica-
tion with wind tunnels.
Most consulting engineering projects
do not have research and development
budgets that permit physical verification
or calibration of a CFD model. Yet, this
does not mean CFD cannot be commer-
cially applied for everyday consulting en-
gineering use. After all, how many cool-
ing load and economic analysis simula-
tions go through a physical verification
process? Instead, an experienced engi-
neer, knowledgeable about the physical
process and software, will analyze the
software output for meaningful results
About the Author
Paul Martin
aul Martin
aul Martin
aul Martin
aul Martin, P.E., is the principal owner of Sigma
Psi Consulting in Gansevoort, N.Y. He is a graduate
of Hudson Valley Community College in Troy, N.Y.,
and a continuing education student at Union College
in Schenectady, N.Y.
By Paul Martin, P.E.
Member ASHRAE
and errors. Using this approach, CFD can
be a valuable and economically feasible
tool for a consulting engineering office.
Clients often ask me, “what happens if
industry practice is not followed?” I have
frequently defended a design that has
gone through several iterations. HVAC&R
engineers do not always have a cook-
book design solution taken from rigid stan-
dards such as the NEC or one of the
plumbing codes. Frequently, the best
available answer is “based on my engi-
neering judgment” or “based on my past
experience.”
These responses sometimes result in
a client requesting an analysis to justify
the proposed design, frequently at no
additional design fee. CFD offers design
support for these situations, not only for
the fluid flow, but also for the heat trans-
fer aspect of the design. CFD can pro-
vide powerful insight when developing
design solutions to unusual situations.
For example, various HVAC alterna-
tives might be explored for the comfort
of employees working near a radiant heat
source of 2,000°F (1100°C) when shield-
ing is not a permissible alternative. CFD
can be used to examine the optimum dif-
fuser placement for critical areas. Room
velocities, fume and particle capture, es-
cape of pollutants from negatively pres-
surized rooms, CO2 tracking for ventila-
tion effectiveness and optimum prod-
uct stacking for cold storage are all prob-
lems that can be analyzed using CFD. A
typical plot of room velocity vectors
from a CFD calculated flow field is shown
in Figure 1.
• HVAC&R Applications
• Software and Hardware
• Creating a CFD Model
• Reducing Project Costs
T

C A L C U L A T I N G
CFD Package Costs
CFD usage on a commercial scale re-
quires a software package capable of pro-
ducing a finite element solution set to sev-
eral flow and energy variables over a sig-
nificantly sized computational domain. The
CFD modeling software must also have an
efficient user interface to make it commer-
cially practical. For example, program menus
should lead the user through the steps re-
quired to setup a problem. Error and warn-
ing messages should use English text,
rather than alphanumeric codes.
During my 1990 Fluid Dynamics
course, it was stated that the commercial
license fee for the CFD package used by
the college was $50,000, and that it re-
quired a main frame computer system. In
addition, the license fee was renewed
yearly. This sort of budget typically is
not available for HVAC&R engineers.
That same software can now be pur-
chased for $18,000 per year, and can be
installed on a Pentium desktop PC run-
ning Windows ’95 or Windows NT (there
is still a yearly license renewal fee). Ex-
amples of other CFD software packages
are as follows:
• A three-month consultant’s license
for $2,500 followed by $1,950 quarterly
fees when there are active projects, while
no fee is charged if the consultant does
not have active CFD projects.
• A package with an annually renew-
able license of $11,000 per year running
under Windows NT/95.
• $18,000 for one package’s perpetual
license.
• $16,000 to $23,000 per year for single
seat, annually renewable licenses of main-
frame or UNIX packages.
Hardware costs for a single worksta-
tion can range from $4,000 for a desktop
300 MHz Pentium II to $8,000 for a dual
Pentium desktop (which requires Win-
dows NT for parallel processing), $5,000
for a DEC Alpha station and approxi-
mately $20,000 for a UNIX graphics work-
station.
One manufacturer offers a turnkey
CFD product installed on the appropriate
workstation for $17,000. While these costs
are substantial and would require serious
commitment, they are comparable to the
costs for early CAD systems. If prices
follow the general computer software and
hardware trend, they will continue to drop.
Competition between CFD packages
is increasing with new companies enter-
ing the market. Presently, approximately
10 to 20 companies offer a CFD package.
Some of the companies also market other
types of FEA packages, while others ex-
clusively produce CFD packages. Many
of the CFD products have been devel-
oped from software used in academic re-
search. Often there is a 10-year or more
history with the software’s use. There-
fore, it is likely that the CFD package has
had prior users who have found bugs and
requested improvements. This means
that the purchaser will not be exposed to
Version 1.0 of the software.
Shopping for a CFD package should
be approached as an in-depth, informa-
tion gathering project. The purchaser
should ask about HVAC type applica-
tions, technical support, training, user
base across the country, how long the
Figure 1: Airflow through a TB isolation room.
Figure 2: Mean particle path through a TB isolation room.
2 2
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
product has been ported to Windows or UNIX and what mod-
ules are included with the basic package.
Technical Training
Once the software and workstation are purchased, engi-
neering personnel must have an efficient working knowledge
of the CFD package. Fortunately, a CFD software licensing fee
typically includes three to five days of training. The learning
curve associated with a CFD package is similar to learning to
use a fully-featured CAD system and a fully-featured cooling/
heating load analysis package simultaneously.
The training candidate should be an engineer with knowl-
edge of fluid mechanics. In addition to factory training, techni-
cal support may be available as part of the software license.
Usually, technical support consists of proper CFD problem
setup as well as support for software glitches. As with early
CAD use, the labor-hours required to learn how to efficiently
apply the software package can equal or exceed the cost of the
newly purchased software and hardware.
Creating a CFD Model
Creating a CFD simulation involves four major steps. First, a
physical model must be created. A suitable drawing package
can be used to create the design domain, or perhaps the domain
can be defined in simple rectangular terms of length, width and
height. Second, a grid is created that divides the design domain
into control volumes. The control volumes are then defined as
walls, flow inlets, flow outlets, pressure, rotating, cyclic and
other types of boundaries. Control volumes that need to be
numerically solved for the flow field are designated as live cells.
Third, the problem must be numerically solved. For the last
step, the numerical solution output is generated, generally with
graphical color plots of vectors and contours overlaid onto the
physical features of the design domain.
Ideally, a preexisting CAD drawing could simply be imported
into the CFD package. However, HVAC&R consulting CAD
drawings typically are developed to convey bidding and con-
struction information. These drawings do not have a true 3-D
representation of the design space. Commercial CFD packages
include CAD-style drawing software that is used to create a 3-
D model and may permit the import of CAD drawings to use as
a starting point.
Once the design geometry is created, a grid must be devel-
oped from the model. The grid is used by the CFD solver to
develop the numerical solution set. The grid is actually an
array of fluid dynamics control volumes. Various types of grids
exist with associated pros and cons. The structured grid is
perhaps the earliest commercial grid used for CFD. Structured
grids use orthogonal grid lines to form brick-like control vol-
umes. Orthogonal bricks are computationally efficient. How-
ever, it can be difficult to depict many geometric shapes using
only bricks. The unstructured grid uses triangles to form
tetrahedrals, hexahedrals, bricks, wedges, etc. Therefore, un-
structured grids provide multiple shapes to use as basic build-
ing blocks, and control volumes can be fit to a complicated
geometry. The drawback to an unstructured grid is that the
numerical solver will require more computational time and power
to obtain a solution.
The practical hardware requirements for large, unstructured
grids may quickly exceed the computing power of a desktop
PC. For example, a complex structured grid solution may require
18 to 24 hours of continuous computing time; however, the
same problem would require 10% more time using an unstruc-
tured grid. Intensive problems beyond the straightforward HVAC
application might require up to four or five days of continuous
desktop computing time using an unstructured grid. Some CFD
packages have parallel processing capabilities so that multiple
UNIX workstations can be networked together to solve large,
Figure 3: Entrance door velocity vec-
tors for a TB isolation room (0.003 in.
wc positive to corridor).
J a n u a r y 1 9 9 9
A S H R A E J o u r n a l
2 3
C A L C U L A T I N G
unstructured grid problems with shared computing power (re-
sulting in reduced solution times).
The accuracy of a CFD solution increases with finer grids,
that is, smaller, more numerous control volumes. However, finer
grids require more computing power. The adaptive grid auto-
matically will provide a finer grid in areas of need, and retain a
coarser grid in areas that do not require as much computing
power for the same level of accuracy. Adaptive grid software
codes are provided with higher end CFD packages. A less ex-
pensive basic CFD package may only permit adaptive grid so-
lutions through the use of add-on software modules at addi-
tional costs.
Many HVAC&R problems can be solved with a structured
grid, and therefore, solved with a desktop PC or workstation.
Structured grids for CFD refer to orthogonal mesh lines as noted
previously. Simple grids are created in a Cartesian coordinate
system that may be suitable for many simple HVAC&R models.
However, a Cartesian grid will be limited for curvilinear shapes
since bricks in a stair step pattern must be used to represent
curves. The CFD solver obtains all flow field geometry informa-
tion from the grid, and therefore, will produce erroneous results
at boundary walls. To avoid this problem, a curvilinear grid can
be created that has its topography (which is developed from
grid lines curved to match the surface of the design domain)
mapped to an orthogonal computational grid. This type of struc-
tured grid is known as a body fitted grid, and must be created
using a CAD-style pre-processor software package.
Third party pre-processors exist as well as software that is
available with the CFD package. An engineer should have a good
understanding of the grid requirements before completing this
phase of a CFD model. Once the grid is established, it is difficult
to modify it without redefining the entire CFD problem setup.
After creating the grid, control volumes must be setup as
either boundary cells or live cells. Boundary cells define the
CFD problem in terms of inlet flows, flow temperatures, pres-
sure, boundary walls, etc. This portion of the CFD simulation
setup offers the most flexibility. For example, a problem can be
setup with positive pressure outside a room. Then a new simu-
lation can be established for negative pressure outside the room
with only minutes of additional labor. Minimal labor is required
to examine the same room for different diffuser layouts, cooling
vs. heating airflows, open vs. closed doors, etc. Here is where
the power of CFD simulations can be valuable to the HVAC&R
engineer. A design can be tested for flexibility, future condi-
tions, expansion and sensitivity to operating conditions inside
and outside the design domain. Options for particle tracking
(dispersed phase modeling) may also be available in the CFD
package, which can be used to track contaminants through the
flow field (see Figure 2).
When all boundary conditions are set, the problem is ready
for the CFD solver. The governing differential equations will be
discretized across the finite element domain. That is, the gov-
erning differential equations are applied to uniformly sized, dis-
creet control volumes, and the differentials dy/dx, dx/dy, dy/dz
etc. can be reduced to 1/1 or other finite values. Thus, the
differential equations can be rewritten in terms of multidimen-
sional algebraic linear equations indexed to the finite element
control volumes. It is the job of the CFD solver to obtain a
solution set for the linear equations. The solver may contain
multiple methods for obtaining a solution for the same flow
field variables. The different CFD solver methods may con-
verge more quickly to a satisfactory solution depending on the
type of CFD problem.
It is up to the engineer, through study of the software docu-
mentation (which should be in depth) and technical support, to
apply the appropriate CFD solution methodology to the
problem’s initial conditions. CFD models exist for Newtonian
and non-Newtonian flows, compressible and incompressible
Figure 4: Pressure contours of typical

dor).

fluids, phase change of fluids, chemical reactions, turbulence
energy and energy transfer including natural convection, ra-
diation and conductive heat transfer.
An HVAC&R problem could be solved according to the fol-
lowing process.1 The governing partial differential equations
are solved for the conservation of mass, momentum, energy
and chemical species as applicable. The governing PDEs could
each take the form of a Cartesian tensor, and noted as:

control volumes of the design domain. The resulting algebraic
equations can be noted as:
